text: What does HTML stand for?
options: Hyper Transfer Markup Language
High Tech Modern Language
Home Tool Markup Language
Hyper Text Markup Language
explanation: HTML stands for Hyper Text Markup Language, the standard language for creating web pages.

text: Which language is primarily used for styling web pages?
options: HyperText Markup
JavaScript Logic
Python Scripting
Cascading Styles
explanation: CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is used to style and layout web pages.

text: What is a web browser?
options: Software that displays web pages
A system for managing databases
A tool for compiling source code
A backend application framework
explanation: A web browser is software that retrieves and displays web pages from the internet.

text: Which of the following is a web browser?
options: Google Chrome
Apache HTTP
MySQL Shell
Node.js CLI
explanation: Google Chrome is a popular web browser developed by Google.

text: What does URL stand for?
options: Uniform Reference Locator
Uniform Resource Locator
Universal Resource Locator
Universal Reference Link
explanation: URL stands for Uniform Resource Locator.

text: Which language adds interactivity to web pages?
options: HTML markup
JavaScript
SQL queries
CSS styling
explanation: JavaScript is the programming language used to add interactive behavior to web pages.

text: What is a website?
options: A single standalone web page
A program that runs web servers
A plugin installed in browsers
A collection of related web pages
explanation: A website is a collection of related web pages hosted under a single domain.

text: What is the role of a web server?
options: To browse and view the internet
To write and compile source code
To design visual page layouts
To store and serve web pages
explanation: A web server stores, processes, and delivers web pages to clients upon request.

text: Which file extension is used for HTML files?
options: .py
.html
.js
.css
explanation: HTML files use the .html or .htm file extension.

text: What is front-end development?
options: Configuring the network settings
Managing and querying databases
Building the user-visible interface
Programming logic on the server
explanation: Front-end development deals with the user interface that users interact with directly in the browser.

text: What is the difference between static and dynamic websites?
options: Static sites load faster but always have fewer total features
Static sites rely on databases while dynamic ones do not
Dynamic sites are completely unable to use HTML markup
Static sites have fixed content while dynamic sites generate it
explanation: Static websites display fixed content, while dynamic websites generate content dynamically.

text: Which technology stack is referred to by the term MERN?
options: MongoDB, Express, React, Node.js
MySQL, Express, React, Node
MariaDB, Ember, React, Nginx
MongoDB, Electron, Redux, Node.js
explanation: MERN stands for MongoDB, Express.js, React, and Node.js.

text: What is the purpose of a domain name?
options: To compile JavaScript code for production
To store website files on a server
To provide a human-readable web address
To encrypt website data during transfer
explanation: A domain name provides a human-readable address that maps to a server IP address.

text: What does IDE stand for in web development?
options: Internal Data Exchange
Integrated Development Environment
Internet Development Engine
Interactive Design Editor
explanation: IDE stands for Integrated Development Environment.

text: Which of the following is a version control system?
options: Docker
Jenkins
Webpack
Git
explanation: Git is a distributed version control system widely used to track source code changes.

text: What is responsive web design?
options: Designing websites for one single device
Designing websites that adapt to screen sizes
Designing websites that include animations
Designing websites that respond very quickly
explanation: Responsive web design ensures websites adapt layout to different screen sizes.

text: What is the role of a back-end developer?
options: Designing the visual user interface
Creating front-end CSS animations
Testing cross-browser compatibility
Managing server-side logic and data
explanation: A back-end developer handles server-side logic, databases, and APIs.

text: What is a CMS?
options: Central Monitoring Service
Client Module Setup
Code Management System
Content Management System
explanation: A CMS allows users to create and manage website content without coding.

text: Which protocol is used for secure communication on the web?
options: HTTPS
FTP
HTTP
SMTP
explanation: HTTPS encrypts communication between the browser and server using TLS/SSL.

text: What is a wireframe in web development?
options: A type of client-side JavaScript framework
A server-side configuration settings file
A basic visual guide for web page layout
A CSS grid system for responsive design
explanation: A wireframe is a low-fidelity sketch that outlines the structure of a web page.

text: Which rendering strategy delivers HTML generated at request time from the server?
options: Static Site Generation
Server-Side Rendering
Client-Side Rendering
Incremental Static Regeneration
explanation: SSR generates HTML on the server for each request.

text: What is the main advantage of a Single Page Application (SPA)?
options: It avoids full page reloads by dynamically updating
It does not need to communicate with any APIs
It renders only a single HTML element on screen
It can function with no JavaScript whatsoever
explanation: SPAs dynamically update content using JavaScript, avoiding full page reloads.

text: What is the JAMstack architecture?
options: jQuery, Angular, MongoDB
Java, Apache, MySQL stack
JSON, AJAX, MVC
JavaScript, APIs, and Markup
explanation: JAMstack stands for JavaScript, APIs, and Markup.

text: Which tool is used for bundling JavaScript modules?
options: Git
Postman
Parcel
Webpack
explanation: Webpack is a module bundler that processes and bundles JavaScript files.

text: What is the purpose of a service worker?
options: To compile TypeScript into JavaScript
To manage and process database queries
To enable offline functionality and sync
To minify and bundle CSS file assets
explanation: Service workers enable offline caching, push notifications, and background sync for PWAs.

text: What is Progressive Enhancement?
options: Starting with a basic experience and adding enhancements for capable ones
Starting with advanced features and removing them for old browsers
Optimizing progressive image loading formats for slower connections
Restricting support exclusively to modern browsers with new features
explanation: Progressive Enhancement starts with baseline functionality, then layers on advanced features.

text: What distinguishes a Progressive Web App (PWA) from a regular web app?
options: PWAs do not use standard HTML markup or CSS styling
PWAs require React as a mandatory framework dependency
PWAs work offline, can be installed, and send notifications
PWAs only function on mobile devices and tablets
explanation: PWAs work offline via service workers, can be installed, and support push notifications.

text: In web development, what is hydration?
options: Applying water-themed CSS styling to elements
Loading and parsing external CSS style sheets
Attaching JavaScript event handlers to server HTML
Compressing and optimizing image file formats
explanation: Hydration is the process where client-side JavaScript takes over server-rendered HTML.

text: What is tree shaking in modern web development?
options: Balancing binary search trees using JavaScript
Reorganizing the DOM tree for better performance
Clearing the browser cache for fresh content
Removing unused code from the final build output
explanation: Tree shaking removes unused exports from the final bundle.

text: What is the critical rendering path?
options: The network path from the server to the browser
The route a URL takes through the DNS resolution
The order in which CSS files are loaded and parsed
The sequence of steps the browser takes to render
explanation: The critical rendering path is the sequence of steps the browser follows to convert code into pixels.

text: What does HTTP stand for?
options: Hyper Text Transport Protocol
High Tech Transfer Protocol
Hyper Text Transfer Protocol
Home Transfer Text Protocol
explanation: HTTP stands for Hyper Text Transfer Protocol.

text: What is a client in web architecture?
options: The web server that hosts content
The database that stores records
The network cable carrying signals
The device or browser making requests
explanation: A client (typically a browser) sends requests to a server and displays the response.

text: What is the default port for HTTP?
options: Port 443
Port 21
Port 8080
Port 80
explanation: Port 80 is the default port for HTTP communication.

text: What does DNS stand for?
options: Dynamic Network Server
Data Network Service
Digital Naming Standard
Domain Name System
explanation: DNS translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses.

text: Which HTTP method is used to retrieve data from a server?
options: POST
DELETE
PUT
GET
explanation: The GET method requests data from a resource without modifying it.

text: What is an IP address?
options: A human-readable domain website name
A server-side scripting language for web
A software application like a web browser
A numerical label assigned to each device
explanation: An IP address is a unique numerical label assigned to each device on a network.

text: What does the status code 200 mean in HTTP?
options: OK (Success)
Not Found Error
Redirect Response
Internal Server Error
explanation: HTTP 200 means the request was successful.

text: What is the role of a web server in the client-server model?
options: To store only the user password data
To forward requests to other clients
To process requests and send responses
To display web pages to the end user
explanation: A web server processes client requests and sends back appropriate responses.

text: Which protocol is used for sending emails?
options: SMTP
DNS
HTTP
FTP
explanation: SMTP is the standard protocol for sending emails.

text: What is a web API?
options: An interface for applications to communicate
A CSS framework for responsive design
A relational database management system
An HTML element used for page layout
explanation: A web API allows different software applications to communicate over the web.

text: What is the difference between HTTP and HTTPS?
options: HTTPS delivers content more quickly
HTTPS encrypts data using TLS/SSL
HTTP provides stronger security features
There is no practical difference at all
explanation: HTTPS adds a layer of security by encrypting data in transit using TLS/SSL.

text: Which HTTP status code indicates a resource was not found?
explanation: HTTP 404 means the server could not find the requested resource.

text: What is a RESTful API?
options: An API built solely with the React library
An API that always requires authentication
An API that exclusively uses GET requests
An API that follows REST design constraints
explanation: A RESTful API follows REST principles: stateless, client-server, uniform interface.

text: What is the purpose of the HTTP POST method?
options: To retrieve existing data from a resource
To submit data for processing by the server
To delete a specific resource from storage
To update only the HTTP request headers
explanation: POST submits data to the server for processing.

text: What is a proxy server?
options: A specialized type of relational database
A redundant backup server for data recovery
An intermediary server between client and target
A domain name resolution DNS server system
explanation: A proxy server acts as an intermediary, forwarding requests from clients to servers.

text: What is latency in web architecture?
options: The total download size of a given web page
The processing speed of the server CPU
The number of concurrent users currently online
The delay between a request and a response
explanation: Latency is the time delay between sending a request and receiving the first byte of the response.

text: Which HTTP header specifies the content type of a response?
options: Cache-Control
Content-Type
Accept-Type
Authorization
explanation: The Content-Type header tells the client what media type the response body contains.

text: What does a CDN do?
options: Manages relational database connections
Compiles source code into machine language
Distributes content across global servers
Designs web page layouts automatically
explanation: A CDN caches and serves content from servers closer to the user.

text: What is WebSocket used for?
options: Managing relational database schemas and queries
Full-duplex real-time client-server communication
Applying visual styles to web page elements
Compressing files to reduce transfer size
explanation: WebSocket provides a persistent, full-duplex communication channel for real-time data exchange.

text: What is the purpose of the HTTP PUT method?
options: To update or replace a resource at a URL
To delete an existing resource completely
To retrieve existing data from a resource
To establish a new connection to a server
explanation: PUT replaces the entire resource at the given URL with the request payload.

text: What is the difference between HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2?
options: HTTP/2 does not support any form of HTTPS encryption
HTTP/1.1 is significantly faster in performance than HTTP/2
HTTP/2 supports multiplexing requests over one connection
HTTP/2 is text-based while HTTP/1.1 uses binary framing
explanation: HTTP/2 introduces binary framing, multiplexing, header compression, and server push.

text: What is CORS in web architecture?
options: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing, allowing cross-domain requests
A NoSQL document-oriented database for storing data
A lightweight JavaScript library for DOM manipulation
A popular CSS utility-first framework for styling apps
explanation: CORS allows servers to specify which origins can access their resources.

text: What is the purpose of the OPTIONS HTTP method?
options: To retrieve existing data from a server resource
To update existing data on the specified server
To permanently delete a resource from the server
To describe the communication options for a resource
explanation: OPTIONS is used in preflight requests to determine supported HTTP methods and headers.

text: What does the HTTP status code 304 indicate?
options: Bad request due to client error
Not modified, use the cached version
A permanent redirect to another URL
Internal server processing error
explanation: 304 tells the client the resource has not changed, so the cached version can be used.

text: What is HTTP/3 based on?
options: FTP protocol
TCP protocol
WebSocket API
UDP via QUIC
explanation: HTTP/3 uses QUIC, a transport protocol built on UDP.

text: What is the Same-Origin Policy?
options: A security measure restricting cross-origin script access
A policy requiring all files on a single web server
A CSS naming convention for component-scoped styling
A database access rule governing read-write permissions
explanation: The Same-Origin Policy restricts web pages from making requests to a different domain, protocol, or port.

text: What is a reverse proxy?
options: A client-side mechanism for caching web assets
A type of VPN for securing remote connections
A proxy system that blocks all incoming traffic
A server that forwards client requests to backends
explanation: A reverse proxy forwards client requests to backend servers while providing load balancing and caching.

text: What is content negotiation in HTTP?
options: Negotiating the pricing for a website project
The process of agreeing on the response format
A handshake protocol for secure connections
A type of compression for reducing file sizes
explanation: Content negotiation uses headers like Accept to agree on the format of the response.

text: What is the purpose of an ETag header?
options: To specify the content encoding type
To set and manage session cookies
To encrypt transmitted data in transit
To provide a version identifier for cache
explanation: An ETag is a unique identifier for a resource version, enabling efficient cache validation.

text: What is the difference between stateful and stateless protocols?
options: Stateful protocols are always significantly faster
Stateless protocols store all data on the server
Stateless protocols do not retain session information
Stateful protocols are unable to use any cookies
explanation: In a stateless protocol like HTTP, each request is independent.

text: Which HTML tag is used to create a paragraph?
options: <pg>
<para>
<text>
explanation:
The

tag defines a paragraph in HTML.

text: What is the correct HTML tag for the largest heading?
options: <head> tag
<heading>
<h6> tag
<h1> tag
explanation: The <h1> tag defines the largest heading in HTML.

text: Which HTML tag is used to create a hyperlink?
options: <url>
<link>
<href>
explanation: The <a> (anchor) tag creates hyperlinks in HTML.

text: Which attribute specifies the URL in an anchor tag?
options: href
url
link
src
explanation: The href attribute specifies the URL that the hyperlink points to.

text: What is the correct HTML tag for inserting an image?
options: <photo>
<image>
<pic>
explanation: The tag embeds images in HTML.

text: Which HTML tag creates an unordered list?
options: <list>
<ul>
<li>
<ol>
explanation: The <ul> tag creates an unordered (bulleted) list.

text:
What does the
tag do?
options: Creates a border
Makes text bigger
Creates bold text
Inserts a line break
explanation:
The
tag inserts a single line break.

text: Which HTML tag makes text bold?
options: Only the <b> tag
Only the <strong> tag
The <bold> tag only
Both <b> and <strong>
explanation: Both <b> and <strong> make text bold; <strong> also conveys semantic importance.

text: What is the root element of an HTML document?
options: <html>
<head>
<doctype>
<body>
explanation: The <html> tag is the root element that wraps all content.

text: Which tag defines a table row in HTML?
options: <td> tag
<tr> tag
<th> tag
<table> tag
explanation: The <tr> tag defines a row within an HTML table.

text: What is the purpose of the <meta> tag?
options: To provide metadata about the document to browser
To link external CSS files to the HTML document
To display visible metadata on the page directly
To create a navigation menu within the header area
explanation: The <meta> tag provides metadata such as character set, viewport settings, and descriptions.

text: Which HTML5 tag is used for navigation links?
options: <links> element
<nav> element
<navigation> tag
<menu> element
explanation: The <nav> tag defines a section of navigation links in HTML5.

text: What is the difference between <div> and <span>?
options: They are identical in every behavior
<span> is not able to contain any text
<div> is inline, <span> is block-level
<div> is block-level, <span> is inline
explanation: <div> is a block-level container, while <span> is inline.

text: Which attribute provides alternative text for an image?
options: label
desc
title
alt
explanation: The alt attribute provides alternative text for accessibility and when images fail to load.

text: What is the purpose of the <form> tag?
options: To create a form for user input
To format and style text content
To create a page footer section
To add mathematical formulas
explanation: The <form> tag creates a form that collects user input.

text: Which input type creates a checkbox?
options: type='checkbox'
type='box'
type='check'
type='tick'
explanation: Setting type='checkbox' on an <input> creates a checkbox.

text: What is the purpose of the action attribute in a form?
options: To apply CSS styles to the form
To specify where form data is sent
To validate the form input fields
To add a JavaScript event handler
explanation: The action attribute specifies the URL where form data is sent.

text: Which HTML5 element is used for self-contained content like a blog post?
options: <article>
<div>
<section>
<aside>
explanation: The <article> element represents self-contained content.

text: What does target='_blank' do in an anchor tag?
options: Closes the current browser tab
Opens the link in a new tab
Downloads the linked file
Opens the link in the same tab
explanation: target='_blank' opens the linked URL in a new tab or window.

text: Which tag embeds a video in HTML5?
options: <embed>
<movie>
<video>
<media>
explanation: The <video> tag embeds video content in HTML5.

text: What is the purpose of the data-* attribute in HTML5?
options: To define strict data types for form fields
To store custom data accessible via JavaScript
To store CSS data in external stylesheets
To create connections to external databases
explanation: data-* attributes store custom data on elements accessible via JavaScript's dataset property.

text: What is the Shadow DOM?
options: A hidden HTML element in the page structure
An encapsulated DOM tree that isolates styles
A dark mode feature provided by CSS natively
A deprecated feature from earlier HTML versions
explanation: The Shadow DOM provides encapsulation by attaching a hidden DOM tree to an element.

text: What is the purpose of the <template> tag in HTML5?
options: To create reusable email template layouts
To define HTML content not rendered until activated
To create reusable CSS template style blocks
To import external HTML files into a document
explanation: The <template> tag holds HTML content not rendered until instantiated via JavaScript.

text: Which attribute makes an HTML element editable?
options: editable='true'
edit='true'
writable='true'
contenteditable='true'
explanation: The contenteditable attribute makes an element's content editable directly in the browser.

text: What is the purpose of the srcset attribute in ?
options: To set the image dimensions and size
To provide multiple sources for responsive images
To add a link to the image source code
To set the primary image source URL
explanation: srcset allows the browser to choose the best image source based on device resolution.

text: What is an HTML Web Component?
options: A standard built-in HTML tag element
A server-side rendering technique for pages
A type of CSS framework for web styling
A reusable custom element with encapsulated code
explanation: Web Components combine Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, and HTML Templates for reusable elements.

text: What does the defer attribute do on a <script> tag?
options: Prevents the script from ever running on page
Downloads script in parallel and runs after parsing
Makes the script load and execute asynchronously
Defers the loading of CSS stylesheets
explanation: defer downloads the script without blocking parsing and executes it after the document is fully parsed.

text: What is the difference between defer and async in script loading?
options: async preserves execution order; defer does not order them
defer blocks page rendering; async does not block rendering
defer runs after parsing in order; async runs on download
They behave the same in all situations
explanation: defer executes after parsing in document order, while async executes immediately when downloaded.

text: What is ARIA in HTML?
options: A type of interactive HTML form input element
A client-side JavaScript rendering framework
A CSS animation and transition effects library
Accessible Rich Internet Applications attributes
explanation: ARIA provides attributes that make dynamic web content more accessible to users with disabilities.

text: What is the purpose of the <picture> element in HTML5?
options: To embed inline SVG graphics within the page
To provide multiple sources for responsive images
To create a drawing canvas for vector graphics
To display a photo gallery with thumbnails
explanation: The <picture> element allows the browser to choose the best image based on media conditions.

text: What does CSS stand for?
options: Colorful Style Sheets
Computer Style Sheets
Creative Style System
Cascading Style Sheets
explanation: CSS stands for Cascading Style Sheets.

text: Which CSS property changes the text color?
options: text-color
color
font-color
fg-color
explanation: The color property sets the color of text content.

text: How do you select an element with the ID 'header' in CSS?
options: #header
header
.header
*header
explanation: The # selector selects elements by their ID.

text: Which CSS property sets the background color?
options: color-property
back-color-css
background-color
bgcolor-attr
explanation: The background-color property sets the background color of an element.

text: How do you select elements with the class 'active' in CSS?
options: active
*active
#active
.active
explanation: The dot (.) selector selects elements by their class name.

text: Which property changes the font size?
options: text-font
text-size
font-size
text-style
explanation: The font-size property sets the size of text.

text: What is the correct CSS syntax to change font to Arial?
options: text-font: Arial;
font-family: Arial;
family-font: Arial;
font: Arial;
explanation: The font-family property specifies the font for text content.

text: Which property adds space inside an element's border?
options: padding
spacing
margin
gap
explanation: Padding adds space between content and border.

text: Which property adds space outside an element's border?
options: spacing
margin
padding
outer-space
explanation: Margin adds space outside an element's border.

text: How do you add a comment in CSS?
options: <!-- comment -->
/* comment */
// comment
# comment
explanation: CSS comments are written between /* and */.

text: What is the CSS Box Model?
options: A responsive layout framework for components
A model of content, padding, border, margin
A 3D modeling technique for page effects
A CSS animation model for element transitions
explanation: The CSS Box Model describes how every element is rendered with content, padding, border, and margin.

text: What is the difference between display: none and visibility: hidden?
options: display: none only hides text content but keeps the element
visibility: hidden removes elements from the document flow
display: none removes from flow; visibility: hidden keeps space
They behave identically in every rendering situation
explanation: display:none removes the element from layout, while visibility:hidden hides it but preserves space.

text: Which CSS property is used to float an element?
options: position
align
float
display
explanation: The float property positions an element to the left or right.

text: What does position: relative do?
options: Makes element fixed on scroll
Positions element relative to the viewport
Positions element relative to its normal position
Removes element from document flow
explanation: position:relative offsets an element from its normal position without removing it from flow.

text: What is specificity in CSS?
options: The algorithm determining which CSS rule applies when multiple match
The order in which CSS properties are declared
The speed at which the browser renders CSS styles
The total number of CSS files loaded on the page
explanation: Specificity determines which CSS rule takes precedence when multiple selectors match.

text: Which CSS pseudo-class applies when the user hovers over an element?
options: :focus
::hover
:hover
:active
explanation: The :hover pseudo-class applies styles when the pointer hovers over an element.

text: What is the z-index property used for?
options: Setting the zoom level of an element
Setting the total width of an element
Creating 3D visual effects on elements
Controlling the stack order of elements
explanation: z-index controls the vertical stacking order of positioned elements.

text: What does box-sizing: border-box do?
options: Sets the box model back to the content-box
Adds a shadow effect around the element box
Includes padding and border in total dimensions
Removes the border from around the element
explanation: border-box includes padding and border within the specified width and height.

text: Which selector targets the first child element?
options: :first
::first-child
:first-child
:child(1)
explanation: The :first-child pseudo-class selects an element that is the first child of its parent.

text: What is the inherit value in CSS?
options: A reserved keyword in the JavaScript language
Takes the same computed value as parent element
A CSS variable declared with a custom property
Resets the property to its initial default value
explanation: inherit forces a property to take the computed value from its parent element.

text: What is the difference between em and rem units?
options: em is an absolute unit measured in exact pixels
em is relative to parent font; rem to the root
rem is relative to parent; em is relative to root
They are identical in how they calculate sizing
explanation: em is relative to the parent element's font size, while rem is relative to the root element's font size.

text: What are CSS custom properties (variables)?
options: A type of CSS selector for targeting elements
JavaScript variables referenced from within CSS
User-defined values with -- prefix and var() access
Predefined CSS values built into the browser
explanation: CSS custom properties are declared with -- and accessed using var().

text: What is the will-change CSS property?
options: An animation delay property for scheduling effects
A transition property controlling animation timing
A property used to change element content
A hint about which properties will change for optimization
explanation: will-change informs the browser about expected changes for rendering optimization.

text: How does the CSS calc() function work?
options: It calculates the total page loading time
It performs math with mixed units in CSS values
It calculates JavaScript expressions in CSS
It counts the number of DOM elements on page
explanation: calc() allows mixing units in mathematical expressions for dynamic sizing.

text: What is the stacking context in CSS?
options: A method of combining multiple CSS stylesheets
A CSS Grid feature for placing items in cells
A method for stacking div elements vertically
A 3D conceptualization of elements along z-axis
explanation: A stacking context determines the rendering order of elements along the z-axis.

text: What does the @supports rule do in CSS?
options: Provides support for external web fonts
Checks if a browser supports a CSS feature
Enables support for older browser versions
Adds browser support for legacy features
explanation: @supports applies CSS rules only if the browser supports the specified property and value.

text: What is the difference between :nth-child() and :nth-of-type()?
options: :nth-child only counts elements sharing the same type
They are identical in their selection behavior
:nth-of-type counts all siblings regardless of their type
:nth-child counts all siblings; :nth-of-type counts same type
explanation: :nth-child() counts among all siblings, while :nth-of-type() counts only siblings of the same element type.

text: What is a CSS combinator?
options: A color combination and palette tool
A CSS framework for rapid prototyping
A character explaining selector relationships
A CSS minification and optimization tool
explanation: Combinators define the relationship between selectors in CSS.

text: What is the contain property in CSS?
options: A flexbox property for aligning child items
A property indicating subtree independence for optimization
A property to contain overflowing text content
A text wrapping property for long strings
explanation: The contain property isolates an element's subtree from the document for rendering optimization.

text: What is the CSS clamp() function?
options: A function constraining a value between min and max
A function to lock the scroll position on a page
A function to fix element positions in a container
A function to group elements together visually
explanation: clamp(min, preferred, max) constrains a value between a minimum and maximum.

text: What is CSS Flexbox?
options: A JavaScript library for DOM element manipulation
A CSS keyframe animation type for effects
A third-party CSS framework for styling pages
A layout model for one-dimensional rows or columns
explanation: Flexbox distributes space among items in a container along a single axis.

text: Which CSS property creates a flex container?
options: display: flex
flex: true
layout: flex
container: flex
explanation: Setting display: flex makes an element a flex container.

text: What is CSS Grid?
options: A JavaScript grid plugin for data tables
A third-party CSS component library
A replacement exclusively for HTML tables
A two-dimensional layout for rows and columns
explanation: CSS Grid is a two-dimensional layout system for rows and columns simultaneously.

text: Which property defines grid columns?
options: column-template
grid-template-columns
display-columns
grid-columns
explanation: grid-template-columns defines the number and size of columns in a Grid container.

text: What is a media query in CSS?

gRPC requires no schema definition while REST mandates strict schemas
explanation: gRPC uses Protocol Buffers and HTTP/2 for efficient, strongly-typed service communication.

text: What problem does consistent hashing solve in distributed web systems?
options: It minimizes key redistribution when nodes are added or removed from clusters
It encrypts all data at rest using a consistent algorithm everywhere
It ensures that all web pages are cached for exactly the same duration
It guarantees that all database queries return results in constant time
explanation: Consistent hashing minimizes data redistribution when the number of nodes changes.

text: What is the purpose of circuit breaker pattern in distributed architectures?
options: To physically disconnect failing hardware servers from the data network
To automatically scale up server resources when traffic increases sharply
To encrypt inter-service communication using rotating TLS certificates
To prevent cascading failures by stopping calls to unresponsive services
explanation: Circuit breakers prevent cascading failures by halting requests to failing services.

text: What is eventual consistency and when is it acceptable in web systems?
options: Data is never consistent and requires manual synchronization by admins
Data consistency is guaranteed only during server maintenance windows
Data is always immediately consistent across all distributed system nodes
Data will become consistent over time and suits high-availability needs
explanation: Eventual consistency means data converges over time, suitable for high-availability systems.

text: Which HTML tag creates a paragraph?

<span> tag
<div> tag
tag
explanation:
The

tag defines a paragraph in HTML.

text: What does the <a> tag create?

text: Which tag is used for the largest heading?

<h2> tag
<h3> tag

text: What does the tag do?
options: Generates a table layout
Inserts an image element

Defines a text paragraph

text: Which HTML tag creates a line break?

tag
<hr> tag
<nl> tag
explanation:
The
tag inserts a line break in HTML content.

text: What is the purpose of the <title> tag?

To set the browser tab title text

text: Which tag creates an unordered list?

<ul> tag
<ol> tag

text: What does the <body> tag contain?

Page metadata and style links
Server configuration settings
External script file references

text: What is the difference between the <div> and <span> elements?
options: <div> can only contain text while <span> can contain any HTML element

<div> is block-level and <span> is inline in default display behavior

text: What is the purpose of semantic HTML elements like <article> and <section>?

They provide meaning to content structure for accessibility and SEO
They replace all div elements and are required for valid HTML5 pages

text: What does the required attribute do on a form input element?

It prevents the form from submitting until the field has been filled

text: How does the element differ from a standard tag?

It creates an image gallery carousel with built-in navigation controls
It provides multiple sources for responsive image selection by browser
It only supports SVG format images and cannot display any raster type

text: What is the purpose of the data-* attribute in HTML5?

To define database connection strings directly within HTML elements
To store custom data private to the page or application on elements
To encrypt sensitive user information within the DOM tree structure
explanation: data-* attributes store custom data on HTML elements accessible via JavaScript.

text: What does the <template> element do in HTML5?

It holds client-side content not rendered until activated by scripts
It applies a predefined visual theme to the entire web page document

text: What is the role of the <meta charset='UTF-8'> tag?

It defines the viewport width for mobile responsive design layouts
It specifies the character encoding used for the HTML document text

text: What is the difference between the <strong> and <b> tags?
options: <strong> indicates semantic importance while <b> is only visual bold
Both tags are completely identical with no semantic differences at all
<strong> is deprecated in HTML5 and <b> is the recommended replacement

text: What attribute makes an input field read-only but still submittable?

disabled attribute

text: What is the purpose of the <fieldset> and <legend> elements in forms?

text: A page has multiple forms. How does the form attribute on an input element help?

text: What is the Shadow DOM and how does it relate to HTML web components?
options: A caching technique that stores DOM elements in browser memory shadow

An encapsulation mechanism providing scoped styles and DOM for components
A server-side rendering method that generates HTML in a shadow process
explanation: Shadow DOM encapsulates component markup and styles, preventing style leakage.

text: What is the purpose of the <slot> element in HTML web components?

It creates a placeholder for user-provided content in shadow DOM trees

text: How does the Content Security Policy meta tag protect against XSS attacks?

It restricts which sources of scripts and resources the browser loads
It blocks all JavaScript execution and relies solely on server actions
It automatically sanitizes all user input before inserting into the DOM
explanation: CSP restricts which sources the browser can load scripts from, mitigating XSS risks.

text: What accessibility issue arises from using div and span elements for interactive controls?

They lack built-in keyboard support, roles, and accessibility semantics
They are invisible to all users including those not using screen readers
explanation: Divs and spans lack built-in ARIA roles, keyboard handling, and focus management.

text: What is the purpose of the inert attribute introduced in HTML?

text: How does the <dialog> element improve over custom modal implementations?

It enables modals to persist across page navigations without JavaScript
It automatically translates modal content into multiple user languages
explanation: The <dialog> element provides built-in focus management, backdrop, and accessibility.

text: What is the Intersection Observer API commonly used for in HTML pages?

To observe and report CSS syntax errors found within style sheet rules
To monitor server response times and report performance metrics data
explanation: Intersection Observer detects element visibility changes for lazy loading and infinite scroll.

text: What is the purpose of the popover attribute in modern HTML?

It converts any element into a fixed-position navigation sidebar panel
It defines print-specific styles that appear only during page printing
explanation: The popover attribute creates native popup behavior without JavaScript.

text: How does the fetchpriority attribute affect resource loading in HTML?

It hints to the browser the relative priority for fetching a resource
It sets the maximum timeout duration for network requests to complete
It determines the order in which CSS rules are applied to the document

text: Which CSS property changes text color?

border
color
font-size

Using a dot prefix

text-background
color property
background-color

The weight or boldness of the text

text: What is the purpose of the CSS calc() function?

text: What is a CSS pseudo-class?

text: How does the CSS contain property improve rendering performance?

text: What is the difference between min-content and max-content sizing?

text: What is the purpose of the CSS will-change property and its potential pitfall?

text: What is the CSSOM and how does it relate to the DOM?

text: How does container query sizing differ from viewport-based media queries?

text: What does a media query do in CSS?

display: table
display: inline
display: block

text: What unit is relative to the viewport width?

em unit
vw unit

text: What does the flex-direction property control?

Sets the font size of grid text

text: What does justify-content do in flexbox?

text: What is the rem unit relative to?

text: What does align-items do in flexbox?

text: How does the minmax() function work in CSS Grid?

It calculates the minimum and maximum viewport size for media queries
It computes the smallest and largest margin values for element spacing

text: What is the purpose of the grid-template-areas property?

text: How does the flex shorthand property work?

It combines flex-grow, flex-shrink, and flex-basis into one declaration

text: What is the difference between auto-fit and auto-fill in CSS Grid?
options: auto-fill expands items to fill space while auto-fit keeps empty tracks
auto-fill requires explicit row counts while auto-fit calculates them all
auto-fit only works with fixed pixel values while auto-fill uses percents
auto-fit expands items to fill space while auto-fill keeps empty tracks

text: How do CSS transforms affect the document flow?

Transforms remove the element entirely from the normal document flow
Transforms only work when the element has position absolute or fixed set
explanation: CSS transforms visually modify elements without changing their position in document flow.

text: What is the purpose of the clamp() function in CSS?

It restricts the number of flex items that appear on a single row line

text: How does the order property work in flexbox?

It specifies the order in which CSS transitions are applied to each item
It changes the visual order of flex items without altering the DOM order

text: What is the aspect-ratio property used for?

To set the ratio of padding to margin for consistent element spacing
To define the ratio between font size and line height in text blocks
To maintain a width-to-height ratio for an element automatically in CSS

text: How does the place-items shorthand work in Grid?

text: What is the difference between grid-column and grid-area?

text: How do CSS container queries change the approach to component-based responsive design?

They require JavaScript to detect container dimensions and apply styles

text: What is the purpose of the @supports rule and how does it enable progressive enhancement?

text: How does the CSS scroll-snap property improve scroll-based user experiences?

text: What is the difference between implicit and explicit grid tracks in CSS Grid?

text: How does the isolation property interact with stacking contexts and blend modes?

text: What challenges arise when combining CSS Grid and flexbox in complex layouts?

text: How does the CSS :is() pseudo-class improve selector maintainability?

It validates that CSS property values are within acceptable valid ranges

text: What is the logical properties model in CSS and why is it important?

It applies mathematical logic to calculate responsive font sizes for text

text: How does the CSS accent-color property simplify form styling?

It sets the accent color for form controls like checkboxes and radio buttons

text: What is the purpose of the @property rule for CSS custom properties?

text: What keyword declares a variable that can be reassigned?

text: What is the result of typeof null in JavaScript?

text: What does console.log() do?

text: How do you write a single-line comment?

text: What is an array in JavaScript?
options: A CSS property for arranging page elements
A function that processes text strings only
A single numeric value stored in memory
An ordered collection of values in a list
explanation: An array is an ordered collection of values accessed by index.

text: What does the push() method do to an array?
options: Adds an element to end
Removes the last element
Sorts elements in order
Reverses array elements
explanation: push() adds one or more elements to the end of an array.

text: What is a function in JavaScript?
options: A reusable block of code that performs a task
A network protocol for exchanging data between nodes
A database query that retrieves stored information
A CSS selector that targets specific HTML elements
explanation: A function is a reusable block of code designed to perform a specific task.

text: What value does a variable have before assignment?
options: zero value
undefined
null value
empty string
explanation: Variables declared but not assigned have the value undefined.

text: Which method converts a string to a number?
options: parseInt() method
toString() method
concat() method
stringify() method
explanation: parseInt() parses a string and returns an integer.

text: What is the difference between let and var in JavaScript?
options: let is used for numbers only while var works with all data type values
let cannot be reassigned while var allows reassignment of the variable
let is function-scoped while var is block-scoped in JavaScript code
let is block-scoped while var is function-scoped in JavaScript code
explanation: let is block-scoped; var is function-scoped and hoisted differently.

text: What is hoisting in JavaScript?
options: Moving DOM elements to the top of the page during initial rendering
Moving declarations to the top of their scope during the compile phase
Raising the priority of event listeners above other queued callbacks
Uploading JavaScript files to a remote server for production hosting
explanation: Hoisting moves variable and function declarations to the top of their scope.

text: What is the difference between == and === in JavaScript?
options: == coerces types before comparison while === checks type and value both
== is used for strings while === is used for numbers and boolean values
== compares only types while === compares only values of the operands
== is deprecated in modern JavaScript while === is the only valid option
explanation: == performs type coercion; === checks both value and type without coercion.

text: What is a callback function in JavaScript?
options: A function that is called automatically when the page finishes loading
A function passed as argument to another function for later execution
A function that calls itself recursively until a condition is satisfied
A function that returns another function as its computed return value
explanation: A callback is a function passed to another function to be called later.

text: How does the ternary operator work?
options: It creates three variables at once using a single declaration statement
It evaluates a condition and returns one of two values based on result
It performs three separate operations on an array simultaneously today
It loops through an array exactly three times for processing elements
explanation: The ternary operator (condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse) is a shorthand conditional.

text: What is destructuring assignment in JavaScript?
options: Deleting properties from an object permanently from the memory storage
Removing elements from arrays by overwriting them with undefined values
Extracting values from arrays or objects into distinct variables easily
Converting complex objects into simple primitive values automatically
explanation: Destructuring extracts values from arrays or properties from objects into variables.

text: What does the spread operator do?
options: It expands an iterable into individual elements in function calls or arrays
It distributes network requests evenly across multiple server endpoints
It removes duplicate values from an array and returns a new unique set
It creates a deep copy of nested objects including all inner references
explanation: The spread operator (...) expands iterables into individual elements.

text: What is the purpose of template literals in JavaScript?
options: To allow embedded expressions and multiline strings using backtick syntax
To define strict type templates for validating function parameter values
To create reusable HTML templates that are rendered on the server side
To generate PDF templates from HTML content for download by site users
explanation: Template literals use backticks for string interpolation and multiline strings.

text: How does the map() array method work?
options: It filters array elements and returns only those that match a condition
It modifies the original array in place by replacing each element value
It creates a geographical map visualization from array coordinate data
It creates a new array by applying a function to each element of source
explanation: map() returns a new array with results of calling a function on every element.

text: What is the difference between null and undefined?
options: null is an intentional empty value while undefined means no value assigned
null is used only for objects while undefined is used only for primitives
null triggers an error when accessed while undefined is silently ignored
null is a declared variable with no value while undefined is not declared
explanation: null is an intentional absence of value; undefined means a variable hasn't been assigned.

text: What is a closure and why is it useful in JavaScript?
options: A function that retains access to its outer scope variables after return
A design pattern for preventing memory leaks in event listener callbacks
A syntax for immediately terminating asynchronous operations mid-flight
A method for closing browser windows programmatically using JavaScript
explanation: A closure is a function that retains access to its lexical scope even after the outer function returns.

text: How does the event loop handle microtasks versus macrotasks?
options: Microtasks execute after current task before the next macrotask is taken
Microtasks and macrotasks are processed in the exact same queue together
Macrotasks always have higher priority and execute before any microtasks
Microtasks are processed after all macrotasks in the queue are completed
explanation: Microtasks (Promises) execute after the current task, before the next macrotask (setTimeout).

text: What is the Temporal Dead Zone for let and const declarations?
options: The gap between an event being triggered and its handler being executed
The period between scope entry and declaration where access throws error
The time period between page load and when DOMContentLoaded event fires
The delay between a network request being sent and response being received
explanation: The TDZ is the period from scope entry until the let/const declaration is reached.

text: How does prototypal inheritance differ from classical inheritance?
options: Prototypal objects inherit directly from other objects via prototype chains
Prototypal is only available in strict mode while classical works anywhere
Prototypal inheritance requires TypeScript while classical uses JavaScript
Prototypal uses classes and constructors while classical uses object linking
explanation: In prototypal inheritance, objects inherit directly from other objects through the prototype chain.

text: What is the purpose of WeakMap and how does it differ from Map?
options: WeakMap automatically expires entries after a time while Map keeps forever
WeakMap encrypts its values while Map stores all values as plain text data
WeakMap holds weak references to keys allowing garbage collection of them
WeakMap stores only string keys while Map can store any data type as key
explanation: WeakMap holds weak references to object keys, allowing garbage collection when no other references exist.

text: How does the JavaScript engine optimize code using hidden classes?
options: It hides CSS class names from the DOM to improve rendering performance
It compresses class definitions to reduce memory usage in the heap space
It encrypts class methods to prevent reverse engineering of the codebase
It creates internal type representations to speed up property access times
explanation: Hidden classes are internal type structures V8 creates to optimize property access.

text: What problem does the structured clone algorithm solve that JSON parse/stringify cannot?
options: It validates data types during cloning and throws errors for mismatches
It encrypts the cloned object data for secure storage in local sessions
It handles circular references, typed arrays, and more complex data types
It compresses the cloned data to use less memory than the JSON approach
explanation: Structured clone handles circular references, Dates, RegExps, typed arrays, and more.

text: How does tail call optimization affect recursive functions in JavaScript?
options: It limits recursion depth to a fixed number regardless of memory available
It caches results of previous recursive calls to speed up the computation
It automatically converts recursive functions into iterative loop versions
It reuses the current stack frame for tail position calls preventing overflow
explanation: TCO reuses the stack frame for calls in tail position, preventing stack overflow.

text: What is the difference between a shallow copy and a deep copy of an object?
options: Shallow copies only copy top-level properties while deep clones all nested
Shallow copies are faster but create read-only versions of the object data
Deep copies can only be created using third-party libraries not native code
Shallow copies duplicate all nested levels while deep copies only copy top
explanation: Shallow copies clone top-level properties; deep copies recursively clone all nested levels.

text: How does optional chaining (?.) handle deeply nested property access safely?
options: It logs a warning to the console but continues execution with null value
It automatically creates missing nested properties with default values set
It returns undefined instead of throwing when accessing nullish references
It throws a custom error message when a nested property is not found yet
explanation: Optional chaining returns undefined if a reference is nullish, avoiding TypeError.

text: What is a Promise in JavaScript?
options: A guaranteed synchronous operation result value
A CSS animation timing function for elements
A method for declaring global variables only
An object representing a future async outcome
explanation: A Promise represents the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation.

text: What keyword makes a function asynchronous?
options: defer keyword
await keyword
yield keyword
async keyword
explanation: The async keyword before a function makes it return a Promise.

text: What does the await keyword do?
options: Pauses async function execution until a Promise settles
Creates a new thread for parallel processing of data
Stops all JavaScript execution on the current web page
Delays code execution by a specific number of milliseconds
explanation: await pauses an async function until the awaited Promise resolves or rejects.

text: What is the DOM?
options: A database object model for SQL queries
A design optimization module for CSS
A disk operating mechanism for files
A document object model for web pages
explanation: The DOM (Document Object Model) represents the HTML document as a tree of objects.

text: What does JSON stand for?
options: JavaScript Object Notation
Joint System Object Network
JavaScript Oriented Namespace
Java Standard Object Naming
explanation: JSON stands for JavaScript Object Notation, a data interchange format.

text: What is an arrow function?
options: A shorter syntax for writing function expressions
A function that draws arrows on a canvas element
A special function that handles keyboard arrow keys
A function that only works with array operations
explanation: Arrow functions (=>) provide a shorter syntax for function expressions.

text: What is the purpose of try-catch?
options: To test network speed before loading page resources
To test CSS styles before applying them to elements
To handle errors gracefully without crashing the code
To catch mouse click events on specific HTML elements
explanation: try-catch handles runtime errors gracefully without stopping execution.

text: What does the fetch API do?
options: Makes network requests to get or send data
Retrieves CSS styles from a stylesheet file
Downloads and installs browser plugin extensions
Fetches DOM elements by their class name value
explanation: The fetch API makes HTTP requests to retrieve or send data.

text: What is localStorage?
options: A CSS property for element positioning
Client-side persistent key-value storage
A method for caching DNS query results
Server-side database for user data
explanation: localStorage provides persistent key-value storage in the browser.

text: What is an event listener?
options: A function that waits for and responds to a specific event trigger
A CSS property that detects hover and focus states on an element
A server that listens for incoming network connections from clients
A debugging tool that records all console output to a log file
explanation: An event listener is a function that executes in response to a specific event.

text: What is the difference between Promise.all and Promise.allSettled?
options: Promise.all waits for all but allSettled only waits for the first result
Promise.all runs promises sequentially while allSettled runs them parallel
Promise.all rejects on first failure while allSettled waits for all outcomes
Promise.all only works with two promises while allSettled handles any count
explanation: Promise.all short-circuits on first rejection; allSettled waits for all to settle.

text: How does event delegation work in JavaScript?
options: It assigns separate event handlers to every individual child element node
It delegates event processing to a separate web worker thread for speed
It attaches a single handler to a parent to handle events from children
It automatically prevents default browser behavior for all fired events
explanation: Event delegation attaches one handler to a parent, using event bubbling to handle child events.

text: What is the purpose of the Symbol primitive type?
options: To generate Unicode emoji characters for display in user interface text
To create mathematical symbols for complex equation calculations in code
To create unique identifiers that avoid property name collisions in objects
To define currency symbols for formatting monetary values on web pages
explanation: Symbols create unique, immutable identifiers to avoid property name collisions.

text: How does the async iterator protocol work with for-await-of?
options: It limits the iteration count based on available system memory resources
It runs multiple for loops in parallel across different threads at once
It allows asynchronous iteration over streams of data with await support
It converts synchronous iterators into cached versions for faster access
explanation: for-await-of iterates over async iterables, awaiting each value in sequence.

text: What is the Proxy object used for in JavaScript?
options: To create a network proxy for routing HTTP requests through a server
To create backup copies of objects for undo functionality in the editor
To define custom behavior for fundamental object operations like get/set
To convert JavaScript objects into XML format for SOAP API integration
explanation: Proxy lets you define custom behavior for fundamental operations on an object.

text: What is the difference between Object.freeze and Object.seal?
options: freeze makes objects immutable forever while seal makes them temporary
freeze is for arrays only while seal is for plain objects exclusively used
freeze prevents all changes while seal allows modifying existing properties
freeze works in strict mode only while seal works in any execution mode
explanation: Object.freeze prevents all changes; Object.seal allows modifying existing properties but prevents adding/removing.

text: How does the AbortController work with fetch requests?
options: It converts fetch responses into readable streams for large file handling
It automatically retries failed fetch requests up to three times in a row
It batches multiple fetch requests into a single network call for speed
It provides a way to cancel in-flight fetch requests using abort signals
explanation: AbortController provides an AbortSignal to cancel in-flight fetch requests.

text: What is the purpose of generators in JavaScript?
options: They automatically generate documentation from JavaScript source code
They compile JavaScript into machine code for better runtime performance
They generate random numbers for use in cryptographic security functions
They create functions that can pause and resume execution using yield
explanation: Generators are functions that can pause and resume execution, yielding values on demand.

text: How does the MutationObserver API work?
options: It monitors CSS style mutations and prevents unauthorized modifications
It observes network requests and mutates their headers before sending
It tracks variable mutations and logs them for debugging purposes only
It watches for changes to the DOM tree and triggers callbacks for them
explanation: MutationObserver watches for DOM changes and asynchronously triggers callbacks.

text: What is a WeakRef and when would you use it?
options: A weak reference to an object that does not prevent garbage collection
A reference to a CSS variable that resolves at runtime when accessed
A reference to a DOM element that automatically updates when it moves
A reference counted pointer similar to those in C++ or Rust languages
explanation: WeakRef holds a weak reference to an object, allowing it to be garbage collected.

text: How does the JavaScript engine handle memory management and garbage collection?
options: Developers must manually allocate and free memory using explicit commands
The engine uses mark-and-sweep to automatically reclaim unreachable objects
Memory is allocated once at startup and never freed during page lifetime
The engine writes unused data to disk swap files for later reuse if needed
explanation: JS engines use mark-and-sweep garbage collection to automatically reclaim unreachable memory.

text: What is the purpose of SharedArrayBuffer and Atomics in JavaScript?
options: They share CSS stylesheets between multiple browser tabs simultaneously
They automatically split large arrays into smaller chunks for processing
They enable shared memory and atomic operations between web worker threads
They synchronize database transactions across multiple server instances
explanation: SharedArrayBuffer enables shared memory between workers; Atomics provides thread-safe operations.

text: How does the Reflect API complement the Proxy API in JavaScript?
options: Reflect provides default implementations for Proxy trap operations used
Reflect converts Proxy objects back into normal JavaScript plain objects
Reflect enables Proxy objects to intercept CSS style computations too
Reflect optimizes Proxy performance by caching trap results in memory
explanation: Reflect provides default behavior for proxy traps, making trap implementation cleaner.

text: What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism in JavaScript?
options: They are identical concepts with no practical difference in any language
Concurrency manages multiple tasks interleaved while parallelism runs simultaneously
Parallelism is single-threaded while concurrency requires multiple threads
Concurrency is only possible in Node.js while parallelism works in browsers
explanation: Concurrency interleaves tasks on one thread; parallelism executes simultaneously on multiple threads.

text: How do private class fields (#) differ from closure-based privacy in JavaScript?
options: Private fields work only in strict mode while closures work in any context
Private fields are only syntactic sugar with no actual encapsulation enforced
Private fields are slower because they require additional proxy wrapper objects
Private fields use hard privacy enforced by the engine unlike closure convention
explanation: Private fields (#) provide engine-enforced hard privacy, unlike convention-based closure privacy.

text: What is the purpose of the FinalizationRegistry in JavaScript?
options: It manages the order in which CSS animations complete their final frames
It ensures all pending network requests complete before page unload event
It finalizes database transactions and ensures data integrity on commit
It registers callbacks invoked when registered objects are garbage collected
explanation: FinalizationRegistry lets you register cleanup callbacks for when objects are garbage collected.

text: How does the import() dynamic import function differ from static import?
options: Dynamic import returns a Promise and loads modules conditionally at runtime
Dynamic import loads modules at compile time while static loads at runtime
Dynamic import only works with JSON files while static works with all types
Dynamic import is synchronous while static import is always asynchronous
explanation: Dynamic import() returns a Promise and loads modules conditionally at runtime.

text: What is the Transferable interface and how does it optimize worker communication?
options: It transfers ownership of data to a worker without copying the memory buffer
It compresses messages between workers to reduce inter-thread bandwidth use
It copies data between workers using a shared clipboard buffer mechanism
It translates data formats between workers running different script versions
explanation: Transferable transfers ownership of ArrayBuffers to workers without copying, improving performance.

text: How does the TC39 proposal process work for new JavaScript features?
options: Proposals go through stages 0-4 with increasing maturity before inclusion
The ECMA committee implements features first then writes specifications after
Features are voted on by developers through public polls on social media
Features are added by browser vendors independently without coordination
explanation: TC39 proposals progress through stages 0 (strawman) to 4 (finished) before inclusion.

text: What is the Iterator Helpers proposal and what problem does it solve?
options: It helps iterate over CSS selectors to find matching DOM elements faster
It provides helper functions for converting arrays into linked list types
It adds chainable methods like map and filter directly to iterator objects
It creates automatic pagination helpers for iterating over API result sets
explanation: Iterator Helpers add methods like map, filter, take directly on iterator objects.

text: What is React primarily used for?
options: Server-side database management
Operating system development work
Network protocol implementation
Building user interface components
explanation: React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces using components.

text: What is a component in frontend frameworks?
options: A database table configuration file
A reusable piece of user interface
A CSS file that styles a single page
A server route handler for requests
explanation: A component is a reusable, self-contained piece of UI.

text: What is Vue.js?
options: A mobile operating system app
A database query language spec
A backend server framework
A progressive frontend framework
explanation: Vue.js is a progressive JavaScript framework for building user interfaces.

text: What does Angular use for templates?
options: Python template syntax
Ruby ERB template files
Enhanced HTML with directives
Plain JavaScript strings
explanation: Angular uses enhanced HTML templates with directives and data binding.

text: What is JSX in React?
options: A JSON schema extension for validation
A CSS preprocessor for React styles
A JavaScript testing framework tool
A syntax extension mixing HTML with JS
explanation: JSX is a syntax extension that lets you write HTML-like code in JavaScript.

text: What is state in a frontend framework?
options: Data that determines how a component renders and behaves
The geographic location of the server hosting the application
The CSS styling applied to an element at any given moment
The HTTP status code returned by the server for each request
explanation: State is data that drives a component's rendering and behavior.

text: What is npm used for?
options: Managing JavaScript packages
Running database migrations
Compiling TypeScript to JS
Styling web page elements
explanation: npm (Node Package Manager) manages JavaScript packages and dependencies.

text: What is a Single Page Application?
options: An app that works only on single monitor setup
A website with only one paragraph
A web page that disables all user scrolling
An app that updates without full page reloads
explanation: An SPA dynamically updates content without full page reloads.

text: What is the virtual DOM?
options: A database for storing HTML template files
A lightweight copy of the real DOM tree
A physical server that stores DOM data
A CSS framework for virtual reality pages
explanation: The virtual DOM is an in-memory representation of the real DOM for efficient updates.

text: What is Svelte?
options: A compiler-based frontend framework
A backend REST API framework tool
A CSS animation library for web pages
A database migration utility script
explanation: Svelte is a compiler that generates efficient vanilla JavaScript at build time.

text: How does React's reconciliation algorithm work?
options: It replaces the complete DOM with a new version on each state update call
It diffs the virtual DOM against the previous version and patches changes
It uses web workers to rebuild the DOM tree in a background thread process
It compares the entire DOM tree node by node on every single render cycle
explanation: React's reconciliation diffs virtual DOM trees and applies minimal DOM patches.

text: What is the purpose of React hooks?
options: To hook into browser APIs that are not available in standard JavaScript
To add state and lifecycle features to functional components without classes
To create HTTP endpoint hooks for RESTful API communication with servers
To connect React components to external CSS stylesheet files for styling
explanation: Hooks let functional components use state, effects, and other React features.

text: How does two-way data binding work in Angular?
options: Binding only works for string values and not numbers or boolean data types
Data flows only from the template to the component class using event calls
Data is bound at compile time and cannot be updated after initial render
Changes in model automatically reflect in view and vice versa in real time
explanation: Two-way binding syncs data between model and view automatically using [(ngModel)].

text: What is Vue's reactivity system based on?
options: Manual DOM manipulation with document query selector calls in components
Server-sent events that push data changes from backend to the frontend
Proxy-based observation that tracks dependencies and triggers re-renders
Polling intervals that check for data changes every fixed time interval
explanation: Vue 3's reactivity uses JavaScript Proxies to track dependencies and trigger updates.

text: What is the difference between controlled and uncontrolled components in React?
options: Controlled components are server-rendered while uncontrolled are client-only
Controlled components are class-based while uncontrolled are function-based
Controlled components manage form data via state while uncontrolled use DOM
Controlled components use Redux while uncontrolled use local component state
explanation: Controlled components manage form data via React state; uncontrolled use the DOM directly.

text: What is the purpose of a state management library like Redux?
options: To manage the state of network connections and retry failed API call requests
To handle database state synchronization between client and server layers
To manage CSS state transitions and animations across all the components
To manage shared application state in a predictable centralized store object
explanation: Redux provides a centralized, predictable state container for managing shared application state.

text: How does server-side rendering differ from client-side rendering in frameworks?
options: SSR requires WebSocket connections while CSR uses standard HTTP request calls
SSR only works with Angular while CSR is exclusive to React and Vue frameworks
SSR generates HTML on the server for faster initial load and better SEO results
SSR generates HTML on the client while CSR generates it on the server side
explanation: SSR generates HTML on the server, improving initial load time and SEO.

text: What is lazy loading in the context of frontend frameworks?
options: Deferring component or module loading until they are actually needed on page
Loading CSS styles lazily after all JavaScript has finished executing first
Slowing down animations intentionally to create a relaxed user experience
Delaying all network requests until the user explicitly clicks a load button
explanation: Lazy loading defers loading of components or modules until they're needed.

text: What is the purpose of Angular's dependency injection system?
options: To automatically inject error handling code into every component method call
To inject HTML content from external files into Angular template directives
To inject CSS styles directly into component templates without stylesheets
To provide services and dependencies to components without manual creation
explanation: Angular's DI system provides class dependencies without manual instantiation.

text: What are React Server Components and how do they differ from client components?
options: Server components only work in development mode and are removed in production
Server components run on client with server-like APIs for database access
Server components are deprecated and replaced by client-only rendering now
Server components render on server and send serialized output to the client
explanation: React Server Components render on the server and send serialized output to the client.

text: How does Svelte's compile-time approach differ from React's runtime approach?
options: Svelte only works with TypeScript while React supports only plain JavaScript
Svelte uses a larger runtime library while React compiles away its framework
Svelte compiles components to imperative DOM code eliminating virtual DOM need
Svelte requires manual DOM updates while React automates them with diffing
explanation: Svelte compiles components to efficient imperative DOM code, removing the virtual DOM overhead.

text: What is the islands architecture and which frameworks implement it?
options: A network architecture where each microservice runs on separate server nodes
A pattern of interactive components in static HTML used by Astro and Fresh
A database sharding pattern where each table is stored on a separate island
A pattern where each page is an isolated island with no shared state at all
explanation: Islands architecture hydrates only interactive components within static HTML pages.

text: How does React's Suspense mechanism work for data fetching and code splitting?
options: It pauses server execution and buffers all response data until completion
It suspends the browser event loop until all pending promises are resolved
It shows fallback content while waiting for lazy components or async data
It automatically caches all fetch responses to prevent redundant API calls
explanation: Suspense displays fallback UI while waiting for lazy-loaded components or async data.

text: What is the purpose of Angular's change detection strategy OnPush?
options: It only checks the component when its inputs change or events fire within
It enables automatic push notifications from the server to client updates
It pushes all changes to a remote server for persistence before rendering
It forces synchronous rendering of all child components before the parent
explanation: OnPush only triggers change detection when inputs change or events occur, improving performance.

text: How does Vue's Composition API improve code organization over the Options API?
options: It eliminates the need for templates and uses only render functions for views
It compiles Vue components into native mobile applications automatically
It converts Options API components into Composition API at build time process
It enables logic reuse through composable functions grouped by feature concern
explanation: The Composition API groups logic by feature concern using composable functions.

text: What is the fiber architecture in React and what problem does it solve?
options: It provides fiber optic network optimizations for faster data fetching use
It enables incremental rendering allowing React to pause and resume work
It creates a fiber-based threading model for true parallel component rendering
It converts React components into web components for cross-framework usage
explanation: React Fiber enables incremental rendering, allowing work to be paused and prioritized.

text: How does Solid.js achieve fine-grained reactivity without a virtual DOM?
options: It delegates all DOM updates to web workers for off-main-thread rendering
It requires developers to manually specify which DOM nodes need updating
It uses a virtual DOM internally but markets itself as not having one
It uses signals and reactive primitives that update only affected DOM nodes
explanation: Solid.js uses signals for fine-grained reactivity, updating only specific DOM nodes.

text: What are the tradeoffs of using micro-frontends with module federation?
options: Module federation eliminates all build complexity and has no tradeoffs at all
It enables independent deployment but adds complexity in shared dependency management
It requires all micro-frontends to use the same exact version of every dependency
Module federation only works with React and cannot be used with other frameworks
explanation: Module federation enables independent deployment but introduces shared dependency management complexity.

text: How does streaming SSR improve time-to-first-byte compared to traditional SSR?
options: It progressively sends HTML chunks as they render without waiting for all data
It splits the HTML into multiple separate HTTP requests sent in parallel order
It sends the complete HTML document only after all data fetching is finished
It compresses the HTML response using streaming compression before any sending
explanation: Streaming SSR sends HTML chunks progressively as components render, improving TTFB.

text: What is the concept of signals in modern frontend frameworks like Preact and Angular?
options: Network signals indicating connection quality used for adaptive content loading
Reactive primitives that track dependencies and update only subscribers directly
Audio signals processed by the Web Audio API for multimedia web applications
Database signals that notify the frontend when backend data changes on server
explanation: Signals are reactive primitives that track dependencies and efficiently update only affected subscribers.

text: What is a server in web development?
options: A computer that serves resources
A browser extension for debugging
A CSS framework for styling
A front-end layout component
