explanation: A server is a computer that processes requests and serves resources to clients.

text: What is an API?
options: A database management system tool
An interface for software communication
A visual design tool for web pages
A type of web browser extension
explanation: An API (Application Programming Interface) enables software-to-software communication.

text: What does REST stand for?
options: Remote Execution Server Tool
Real-time Event Stream Technology
Representational State Transfer
Responsive Element Style Template
explanation: REST stands for Representational State Transfer.

text: What is a database used for?
options: Creating user interface layouts
Compiling source code files
Storing and retrieving data
Styling web page elements
explanation: A database stores, organizes, and retrieves structured data.

text: What is Node.js?
options: A CSS preprocessor tool
A browser testing framework
A JavaScript server runtime
A database query language
explanation: Node.js is a JavaScript runtime for executing server-side code.

text: What is a route in backend development?
options: A URL pattern mapped to a handler
A database table relationship link
A network cable connecting servers
A CSS path for animation movement
explanation: A route maps a URL pattern to a specific handler function.

text: What does CRUD stand for?
options: Compile, Run, Upload, Deploy
Cache, Route, Update, Debug
Create, Read, Update, Delete
Connect, Render, Update, Drop
explanation: CRUD stands for Create, Read, Update, Delete — basic data operations.

text: What is middleware in backend development?
options: The physical hardware between client and server machines in the network
Software that processes requests between receiving and sending response
The CSS styles applied between two animation keyframes in a transition
A database layer that sits between the raw data files and the queries
explanation: Middleware processes requests between receipt and response in the request pipeline.

text: What is Express.js?
options: A database query builder
A CSS animation library
A frontend testing suite
A Node.js web framework
explanation: Express.js is a minimal web framework for Node.js.

text: What format is commonly used for API responses?
options: HTML format
XML format
CSV format
JSON format
explanation: JSON is the most common format for API responses.

text: What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL databases?
options: NoSQL databases cannot handle relational data while SQL handles everything
SQL is only for small datasets while NoSQL is only for large datasets used
SQL databases are always faster than NoSQL for every possible query type
SQL databases use tables with schemas while NoSQL uses flexible data models
explanation: SQL uses structured tables with schemas; NoSQL uses flexible models like documents or key-value.

text: What is the purpose of an ORM in backend development?
options: To map database records to objects in code without writing raw SQL queries
To manage real-time messaging between connected WebSocket client users
To optimize CSS rendering in server-side rendered pages for performance
To orchestrate multiple microservices into a single deployment pipeline
explanation: An ORM maps database tables to objects, letting you interact with data using code instead of SQL.

text: How does JWT authentication work?
options: The client sends username and password with every single request it makes
The server generates a new unique URL for each authenticated user session
The server issues a signed token containing claims that the client stores
The server stores session data in a database and sends a session cookie
explanation: JWT issues a signed token with encoded claims that the client sends with requests.

text: What is the purpose of environment variables in backend applications?
options: To define CSS custom properties that change based on the user environment
To determine the operating system type for conditional CSS style loading
To store configuration values that change between deployment environments
To set the browser viewport dimensions for responsive testing of layouts
explanation: Environment variables store configuration like API keys that vary between environments.

text: What is rate limiting and why is it important?
options: Controlling the speed at which database queries execute on the server side
Setting a maximum file upload size for requests sent to the server endpoint
Limiting the CSS animation frame rate to improve browser performance speed
Restricting the number of API requests a client can make in a time period
explanation: Rate limiting restricts the number of requests a client can make to prevent abuse.

text: How does connection pooling improve database performance?
options: It compresses database query results before sending them to the application
It distributes database queries equally across all available server hardware
It reuses existing database connections instead of creating new ones each time
It pools CSS connections to share stylesheets across multiple page views
explanation: Connection pooling reuses existing database connections, reducing connection overhead.

text: What is the purpose of input validation on the server side?
options: To verify that client-side JavaScript is properly minified before caching
To ensure incoming data meets expected formats and prevents malicious input
To check that image files are in the correct resolution for display on page
To validate that CSS selectors match existing HTML elements in the document
explanation: Server-side validation ensures data integrity and prevents injection attacks.

text: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
options: Authentication verifies identity while authorization checks access permissions
Authentication checks permissions while authorization verifies identity first
Authentication is for APIs only while authorization is for web pages exclusively
Authentication uses tokens while authorization exclusively uses session cookies
explanation: Authentication verifies who you are; authorization determines what you can access.

text: What is GraphQL and how does it differ from REST?
options: GraphQL requires WebSocket connections while REST uses standard HTTP methods
GraphQL lets clients request exactly the data they need with a single query
GraphQL only supports read operations while REST supports full CRUD actions
GraphQL is a database language while REST is an API architecture style used
explanation: GraphQL lets clients specify exactly what data they need, reducing over-fetching.

text: What is the purpose of logging in backend applications?
options: To measure the length of CSS animations and report their duration in logs
To create visual log-shaped decorations on the user interface of the website
To record application events and errors for monitoring and debugging needs
To generate automatic documentation from source code comment annotations
explanation: Logging records events, errors, and metrics for monitoring, debugging, and auditing.

text: How does database sharding improve scalability for high-traffic applications?
options: It compresses database tables to fit more records into less storage space
It creates a complete copy of the database on every single server available
It caches all database queries in memory eliminating disk read operations
It distributes data across multiple databases reducing load on each individual
explanation: Sharding distributes data across multiple databases, reducing load and improving scalability.

text: What is the saga pattern and when is it used in distributed systems?
options: A database indexing strategy for optimizing full-text search across multiple tables
A pattern for managing distributed transactions across microservices with compensations
A UI design pattern for creating step-by-step wizard forms in web applications
A testing pattern for writing narrative-style test cases in behavior-driven development
explanation: The saga pattern manages distributed transactions with compensating actions for failures.

text: How does event sourcing differ from traditional CRUD data storage?
options: Event sourcing stores state changes as events while CRUD stores current state
Event sourcing is synchronous while CRUD operations are always asynchronous
Event sourcing deletes old data while CRUD keeps complete history of changes
Event sourcing only works with NoSQL while CRUD requires relational databases
explanation: Event sourcing stores every state change as an event, enabling replay and audit trails.

text: What is the CQRS pattern and what problem does it solve?
options: It compresses query results on the server before sending them to clients
It combines all read and write operations into a single optimized data model
It creates a queue for all server requests to process them sequentially only
It separates read and write models for independent optimization and scaling
explanation: CQRS separates read and write models, allowing independent optimization and scaling.

text: How does a message queue improve backend architecture reliability?
options: It queues CSS style calculations to prevent layout thrashing in the browser
It stores database query results in a queue for faster repeated access times
It decouples services by buffering messages for asynchronous processing safely
It creates a queue of user interface updates for batch rendering on client
explanation: Message queues decouple services, buffer requests, and enable reliable async processing.

text: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling?
options: Horizontal only works for databases while vertical only works for web servers
Horizontal adds more servers while vertical adds more resources to one server
Horizontal adds resources to one server while vertical adds more server nodes
Horizontal scaling is free while vertical scaling always requires paid licenses
explanation: Horizontal scaling adds more servers; vertical scaling adds resources to a single server.

text: How does the strangler fig pattern help migrate monoliths to microservices?
options: It immediately replaces the entire monolith with microservices in one deployment
It gradually replaces monolith functionality by routing to new services incrementally
It copies the monolith code into separate repositories without any code changes
It strangles the monolith by reducing its allocated server resources over time
explanation: The strangler fig pattern gradually replaces monolith features by routing traffic to new services.

text: What is the purpose of a dead letter queue in message processing?
options: To create a backup of all messages sent during system maintenance downtime
To queue messages from deactivated user accounts for archival purposes only
To permanently delete messages that have been successfully processed already
To store messages that fail processing for later inspection and retry handling
explanation: Dead letter queues capture messages that fail processing for investigation and retry.

text: How does the outbox pattern ensure reliable event publishing in microservices?
options: It stores events in a database table atomically with data then publishes them
It creates an outbox email notification for every event published by service
It sends events directly to the message broker before committing the database
It buffers events in browser local storage until network connectivity returns
explanation: The outbox pattern stores events with data in the same transaction, then publishes them reliably.

text: What is the bulkhead pattern in distributed systems?
options: A pattern that isolates failures by partitioning resources into separate pools
A pattern for compressing bulk API responses to reduce network bandwidth use
A pattern for creating bulk database insert operations for high throughput
A pattern for generating bulk SSL certificates for multiple domain endpoints
explanation: The bulkhead pattern isolates system components so failure in one doesn't cascade to others.

text: What is server-side rendering?
options: Compiling JavaScript into machine code on the server for faster speed
Rendering CSS animations on the server before sending them to client
Processing database queries on the client side instead of the server
Generating HTML on the server before sending it to the browser client
explanation: SSR generates HTML on the server and sends the complete page to the browser.

text: What is PHP primarily used for?
options: Mobile app development
Desktop GUI applications
Server-side web scripting
Database engine creation
explanation: PHP is a server-side scripting language designed for web development.

text: What is a session in web development?
options: A JavaScript testing framework function
A CSS animation sequence that plays once
A way to store user data across requests
A type of database join operation query
explanation: A session stores user data across multiple HTTP requests on the server.

text: What is a cookie?
options: A small data piece stored in the browser
A CSS property for styling form inputs
A server configuration file for caching
A database table for user preferences
explanation: A cookie is a small piece of data stored in the browser by the server.

text: What does MVC stand for?
options: Managed-Virtual-Cache
Main-View-Component
Model-View-Controller
Module-Variable-Class
explanation: MVC stands for Model-View-Controller, a software architecture pattern.

text: What is a template engine?
options: A tool that generates CSS from design files automatically
A tool that combines templates with data to produce HTML output
A tool that creates database schemas from visual diagram models
A tool that compiles TypeScript into standard JavaScript code
explanation: A template engine combines templates with data to generate HTML pages.

text: What is Django?
options: A database management system
A CSS preprocessor language
A JavaScript runtime environment
A Python-based web framework
explanation: Django is a high-level Python web framework for rapid development.

text: What is Ruby on Rails?
options: A database visualization platform
A Ruby-based web framework tool
A CSS grid framework for layouts
A JavaScript package manager app
explanation: Ruby on Rails is a web framework written in Ruby following MVC conventions.

text: What is a request-response cycle?
options: The process of compiling source code and deploying it to a production box
The cycle of backing up and restoring database records on a fixed schedule
The time it takes CSS to render on screen after being parsed by browser
A client sends a request and the server returns a corresponding response
explanation: The request-response cycle involves a client sending a request and receiving a server response.

text: What does a web framework provide?
options: Only visual design tools for creating page mockups and wireframes easily
Hardware drivers for connecting web servers to network switch devices
Pre-built tools and structure for building web applications efficiently
Browser extensions that enhance the user experience of any website
explanation: A web framework provides tools, libraries, and structure for building web apps.

text: How does server-side caching improve application performance?
options: It caches user passwords on the server to speed up authentication checks
It stores JavaScript bundle files on CDN edge nodes for faster delivery
It caches CSS files in the browser to avoid downloading them every visit
It stores computed results in memory to avoid repeated expensive operations
explanation: Server-side caching stores results in memory, avoiding repeated expensive computations.

text: What is the difference between stateful and stateless server architectures?
options: Stateful servers retain client data between requests while stateless do not
Stateful servers are always faster because they remember all previous data
Stateless servers cannot use databases while stateful servers require them
Stateful is for REST APIs only while stateless is for GraphQL APIs exclusively
explanation: Stateful servers retain client data between requests; stateless treat each request independently.

text: How does server-side form validation complement client-side validation?
options: Server validation runs first and client validation is applied after server
Server validation replaces client validation entirely making it unnecessary
Server validation ensures security since client validation can be bypassed
Server validation is only needed for file uploads and not for text inputs
explanation: Server-side validation is essential because client-side validation can be bypassed.

text: What is the purpose of an application server versus a web server?
options: Application servers are for mobile apps while web servers are for websites only
Web servers are deprecated and application servers have replaced them entirely
Web servers handle HTTP requests while app servers execute business logic also
Application servers only serve static files while web servers handle all logic
explanation: Web servers handle HTTP; application servers also execute business logic and dynamic content.

text: How do server-side redirects differ from client-side redirects?
options: Server redirects happen via HTTP status codes while client uses JavaScript
Client redirects are more secure because they happen within the browser
Server redirects are slower because they require a full page reload always
Server redirects only work with POST requests while client works with GET
explanation: Server-side redirects use HTTP 3xx status codes; client-side uses JavaScript or meta tags.

text: What is the purpose of a task queue in server-side applications?
options: To handle time-consuming tasks asynchronously outside the request cycle
To queue CSS animations for smooth rendering on the server-side components
To organize database migration tasks in the order they should be executed
To schedule automated browser tests to run sequentially on a CI pipeline
explanation: Task queues process long-running tasks asynchronously, keeping request handlers responsive.

text: How does server-side pagination work for large datasets?
options: It loads all records into memory then splits them into pages on the server
It caches the entire dataset in the browser and paginates using JavaScript
It fetches only a subset of records per request using offset and limit values
It creates separate database tables for each page of results automatically
explanation: Server-side pagination uses limit and offset to return only a subset of records per request.

text: What is the role of a reverse proxy like Nginx in server deployments?
options: It provides a graphical user interface for managing database tables easily
It converts server-side code into client-side JavaScript for the browser
It reverses the order of HTTP headers to confuse potential attackers today
It sits in front of servers handling load balancing, SSL, and static files
explanation: Nginx as reverse proxy handles load balancing, SSL termination, and static file serving.

text: What is the purpose of database migrations in server-side development?
options: To move the database server to a different physical location or data center
To manage incremental changes to database schema in a version-controlled way
To migrate data from SQL databases to NoSQL databases for better performance
To create daily backup copies of the database for disaster recovery purposes
explanation: Migrations manage versioned, incremental changes to the database schema.

text: How do background jobs differ from synchronous request handling?
options: Background jobs process tasks outside the request cycle without blocking it
Background jobs run on the client side while synchronous handlers run on server
Background jobs run immediately within the request cycle blocking the response
Background jobs only handle database operations and not any other task types
explanation: Background jobs process tasks outside the request-response cycle, avoiding blocking.

text: How does the N+1 query problem occur and how can it be resolved?
options: It occurs when a query returns N+1 duplicate records from the database table
It occurs when loading related data triggers a query per item instead of batching
It occurs when N+1 users simultaneously access the same server resource endpoint
It occurs when the database connection pool has exactly N+1 connections open
explanation: N+1 occurs when each item triggers a separate query; solved by eager loading or batching.

text: What is the purpose of a write-ahead log in database systems?
options: To record changes before applying them ensuring durability and crash recovery
To write CSS styles ahead of rendering to prevent layout shift on the page
To prewrite API documentation before implementing the actual endpoint logic
To log all user write operations for billing and usage tracking purposes only
explanation: WAL records changes before applying them, ensuring data durability and crash recovery.

text: How does connection multiplexing work in HTTP/2 server implementations?
options: It multiplies the server bandwidth by splitting traffic across many networks
It duplicates every request to multiple servers for redundancy and failover
It creates multiple separate TCP connections for each HTTP request sent out
It sends multiple requests and responses over a single TCP connection stream
explanation: HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple request-response streams over a single TCP connection.

text: What is the leader election pattern in distributed server systems?
options: A pattern for selecting which CSS framework the development team should adopt
A pattern where users vote for their preferred server through a web interface
A pattern where the fastest server automatically becomes the primary database
A pattern where one node is chosen to coordinate tasks among distributed nodes
explanation: Leader election selects one node as coordinator for tasks in distributed systems.

text: How does server-side request forgery (SSRF) attack work and how is it prevented?
options: SSRF sends forged DNS responses to redirect traffic to malicious web servers
SSRF exploits SQL injection to forge database records in production tables
SSRF tricks a server into making requests to unintended internal resources
SSRF forges client-side cookies to impersonate legitimate authenticated users
explanation: SSRF tricks servers into making requests to internal resources; prevented by URL validation.

text: What is the purpose of graceful shutdown in server applications?
options: To finish in-progress requests and release resources before stopping the server
To immediately terminate all active connections without completing any requests
To reduce server CPU speed gradually to lower power consumption over time
To send shutdown notifications to all connected users via email before stop
explanation: Graceful shutdown completes in-progress requests and releases resources before stopping.

text: How does the sidecar pattern work in server-side architecture?
options: It deploys a helper process alongside the main service for cross-cutting tasks
It creates a secondary copy of the application for A/B testing user features
It adds a sidebar navigation component to every page of the web application
It places the database alongside the web server on the same physical machine
explanation: The sidecar pattern deploys a helper process alongside the main service for tasks like logging.

text: What is back-pressure in server systems and how should it be handled?
options: It is the physical pressure on server hardware from high temperature levels
It is a database optimization that compresses data under high storage pressure
It is a mechanism to slow producers when consumers cannot keep up with load
It is the CSS pressure applied to buttons to create depth visual effects
explanation: Back-pressure slows producers when consumers can't keep up, preventing system overload.

text: How does the actor model approach concurrency differently than shared memory?
options: Actors require operating system level support and cannot run on standard hardware
Actors communicate via message passing with isolated state avoiding shared memory
Actors use a single thread and cannot process multiple messages simultaneously
Actors share a global memory space and use locks for synchronization of access
explanation: The actor model uses message passing between isolated actors, avoiding shared memory issues.

text: What is the purpose of distributed tracing in microservice architectures?
options: To track requests across multiple services for debugging and performance analysis
To monitor network cable connections between physical server rack units in DC
To trace CSS rendering performance across multiple browser rendering engines
To trace database query execution plans across multiple table join operations
explanation: Distributed tracing tracks requests across services for end-to-end debugging and analysis.

text: What is SQL?
options: A server configuration format
A JavaScript testing framework
A styling language for web pages
A language for managing databases
explanation: SQL (Structured Query Language) is used to manage and query relational databases.

text: What is a primary key in a database?
options: The main index for text search
The first column in every table
The password for database access
A unique identifier for each row
explanation: A primary key uniquely identifies each row in a database table.

text: What does SELECT do in SQL?
options: Creates new database tables
Deletes specified records
Updates existing row values
Retrieves data from tables
explanation: SELECT retrieves data from one or more database tables.

text: What is MongoDB?
options: A CSS styling framework
A JavaScript build tool
A relational SQL database
A NoSQL document database
explanation: MongoDB is a NoSQL document database that stores data in JSON-like documents.

text: What is a table in a relational database?
options: A JavaScript data structure for key-value pairs used in code
A CSS property that creates grid-like layouts on web page elements
A collection of related data organized in rows and columns format
A server configuration file that maps URLs to handler functions
explanation: A table organizes data into rows and columns in a relational database.

text: What does INSERT do in SQL?
options: Removes rows from a table
Adds new rows to a table
Searches for text patterns
Modifies existing row data
explanation: INSERT adds new rows of data to a database table.

text: What is a foreign key?
options: A temporary key generated during database backup process
A field that references a primary key in another table
A key used to encrypt database files for security
A key that allows foreign users to access the database
explanation: A foreign key references a primary key in another table, linking related data.

text: What is MySQL?
options: A relational SQL database
A CSS preprocessor tool
A JavaScript framework
A NoSQL document store
explanation: MySQL is an open-source relational database management system.

text: What does UPDATE do in SQL?
options: Modifies existing data in the database table rows
Deletes the entire database from the server storage
Creates a new table in the database schema file
Retrieves all records matching a specified condition
explanation: UPDATE modifies existing data in database table rows.

text: What is PostgreSQL?
options: A command-line tool for managing server deployment pipelines
An advanced open-source relational database management system
A frontend CSS framework for web layout design styling
A JavaScript library for building user interface components
explanation: PostgreSQL is an advanced open-source relational database system.

text: What is database normalization and why is it important?
options: It normalizes CSS values to consistent units across all stylesheet files
It organizes data to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity in tables
It converts all database text fields to normalized Unicode encoding formats
It equalizes query execution times so all queries run at the same speed
explanation: Normalization organizes data to minimize redundancy and maintain data integrity.

text: How do database indexes improve query performance?
options: They create visual index pages for the database administrator interface
They automatically optimize SQL queries by rewriting them more efficiently
They create data structures that allow the database to find rows faster
They compress database tables to reduce the overall storage requirements
explanation: Indexes create data structures (like B-trees) that speed up data retrieval.

text: What is the difference between INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN?
options: INNER JOIN returns matching rows while LEFT JOIN includes unmatched left rows
INNER JOIN is faster but less accurate while LEFT JOIN is slower but precise
INNER JOIN returns all rows while LEFT JOIN returns only matching ones
INNER JOIN is for two tables while LEFT JOIN works with three or more tables
explanation: INNER JOIN returns only matching rows; LEFT JOIN includes all left table rows.

text: What is a database transaction and what properties does it have?
options: A financial transaction record stored in the database for accounting use
A scheduled task that runs database maintenance operations periodically
A log entry recording each time a user accesses the database connection
A unit of work with ACID properties ensuring reliability and consistency
explanation: A transaction is a unit of work with ACID properties: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability.

text: How does connection pooling benefit database-driven web applications?
options: It distributes connections equally across all tables in the database schema
It pools multiple databases into a single virtual database for unified access
It creates a pool of pre-compiled SQL queries for faster execution speed
It maintains reusable connections reducing overhead of creating new ones each time
explanation: Connection pooling reuses database connections, reducing connection creation overhead.

text: What is the purpose of database views?
options: To create virtual tables from queries for simplified data access and security
To display database tables in a graphical user interface for administrators
To store compressed backups of database tables for disaster recovery use
To provide real-time visualization charts of database performance metrics
explanation: Views are virtual tables created from queries, simplifying access and providing security.

text: What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL databases?
options: SQL databases are free while NoSQL databases always require paid commercial licenses
SQL uses structured schemas and tables while NoSQL uses flexible data models
SQL databases cannot scale while NoSQL databases scale infinitely with ease
SQL is only for small applications while NoSQL is only for enterprise projects
explanation: SQL uses structured schemas; NoSQL uses flexible models like documents, key-value, or graphs.

text: What is an ORM and how does it simplify database interaction?
options: It maps database tables to code objects enabling queries without raw SQL usage
It organizes REST methods into a standardized naming convention for APIs
It manages database backups by scheduling them at regular time intervals daily
It monitors database performance and reports optimization recommendations to devs
explanation: An ORM maps database tables to objects, allowing database interaction through code.

text: What is the purpose of stored procedures in SQL databases?
options: To save precompiled SQL code on the server for reuse and performance gains
To store JavaScript procedures that run in the browser before page loading
To store CSS procedures for server-side rendering of styled web page content
To archive old database records in compressed format for long-term storage
explanation: Stored procedures are precompiled SQL code stored on the server for reuse and performance.

text: How does Redis differ from traditional relational databases?
options: Redis only supports read operations while relational databases support writes
Redis is an in-memory key-value store optimized for speed and caching needs
Redis requires strict schemas while relational databases are schema-flexible
Redis stores all data on disk while relational databases use in-memory only
explanation: Redis is an in-memory key-value store optimized for fast data access and caching.

text: How does database replication improve availability and what are its consistency challenges?
options: Replication creates copies across servers but may cause stale reads temporarily
Replication guarantees immediate consistency across all replicas without any delay
Replication eliminates the need for backups since data exists on multiple servers
Replication only works with NoSQL databases and not with relational SQL databases
explanation: Replication copies data across servers for availability but may cause temporary inconsistency.

text: What is the write-ahead log and how does it ensure data durability?
options: It logs all SELECT queries for audit purposes before returning results to clients
It writes database documentation ahead of implementation for planning purposes
It records changes to a log before applying them enabling crash recovery of data
It pre-generates auto-increment IDs ahead of insert operations for performance
explanation: WAL records changes to a log before writing to data files, enabling crash recovery.

text: How does database connection pool sizing affect application performance?
options: Larger pools always improve performance with no diminishing returns whatsoever
Connection pools should always match the exact number of application server threads
Too few connections cause queuing while too many waste resources and add contention
Pool size has no impact on performance and is purely a configuration preference
explanation: Undersized pools cause queuing; oversized pools waste resources and increase contention.

text: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking?
options: Optimistic is for read operations only while pessimistic is for writes exclusively
Optimistic locking is faster but pessimistic locking uses less memory on the server
Optimistic assumes no conflicts and checks at commit while pessimistic locks upfront
Optimistic locks the resource immediately while pessimistic waits until commit time
explanation: Optimistic locking checks for conflicts at commit; pessimistic locks resources upfront.

text: How do graph databases differ from relational databases for relationship-heavy data?
options: Graph databases require data to be normalized into third normal form before insertion
Graph databases cannot store any properties on nodes and only support edge connections
Graph databases store data in tables but use graphical user interfaces for queries
Graph databases model relationships as first-class citizens enabling efficient traversal
explanation: Graph databases treat relationships as first-class entities, enabling efficient traversal.

text: What is the purpose of a materialized view and when should it be used?
options: It stores precomputed query results physically for fast access on complex queries
It visualizes database schema as a material design diagram for documentation use
It generates a physical backup file of the database view definitions and metadata
It creates a virtual view that recomputes on every access for fresh data always
explanation: Materialized views store precomputed results, trading storage for faster complex query access.

text: How does database partitioning differ from sharding?
options: Partitioning distributes data across servers while sharding splits within one database
Partitioning and sharding are identical concepts with no practical difference at all
Partitioning is for NoSQL only while sharding works exclusively with SQL databases
Partitioning splits data within one database while sharding distributes across servers
explanation: Partitioning splits data within one database; sharding distributes across multiple servers.

text: What is the CAP theorem's impact on database selection for distributed systems?
options: CAP only applies to NoSQL databases and has no relevance to SQL database systems
CAP requires all three properties to be met for any distributed database to function
CAP states you can only guarantee two of consistency, availability, partition tolerance
CAP states distributed systems must choose between consistency and partition tolerance
explanation: CAP theorem states you can only guarantee two of three: consistency, availability, partition tolerance.

text: How does a time-series database optimize for temporal data compared to relational databases?
options: It stores all timestamps as plain text strings for human-readable log file output
It requires data to be inserted in reverse chronological order for index efficiency
It uses time-based partitioning and compression optimized for append-heavy workloads
It only supports queries for the most recent hour of data and archives everything else
explanation: Time-series databases use time-based partitioning and compression for efficient temporal queries.

text: What is the change data capture pattern and how does it enable event-driven architectures?
options: It captures network packet changes for monitoring database connection health status
It captures CSS property changes and generates transition animations automatically
It captures UI changes in the browser DOM and sends them to the server for storage
It captures database changes as events enabling downstream systems to react to them
explanation: CDC captures database changes as events, enabling downstream systems to react asynchronously.

text: What is HTTPS?
options: A CSS styling standard
HTTP with TLS encryption
A faster version of HTML
A database access protocol
explanation: HTTPS is HTTP with TLS encryption for secure data transmission.

text: What is a password hash?
options: A one-way encrypted form of password
A method for generating passwords
A visible password shown on screen
A type of password recovery email
explanation: A password hash is a one-way transformation that securely stores passwords.

text: What is XSS?
options: A database query optimizer
A server-side language tool
Cross-site scripting attack
A CSS framework for styling
explanation: XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) injects malicious scripts into web pages.

text: What is a firewall?
options: A JavaScript error handling method
A CSS property for styling borders
A database backup recovery process
A system that filters network traffic
explanation: A firewall monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic.

text: What does SSL stand for?
options: System Security Level
Server Side Language
Secure Sockets Layer
Standard Style Library
explanation: SSL stands for Secure Sockets Layer, a protocol for encrypted communication.

text: What is two-factor authentication?
options: Logging in from two different devices at the same time for redundancy
Using two different browsers to log in to the same account simultaneously
Creating two separate passwords for a single user account on the site
Requiring two forms of identity verification for accessing an account
explanation: 2FA requires two forms of verification, adding an extra security layer.

text: What is a CAPTCHA used for?
options: To style form input fields
To verify a user is human
To encrypt form submissions
To speed up page loading
explanation: CAPTCHA verifies that a user is human, preventing automated bot access.

text: What is malware?
options: A CSS animation framework
A type of web server software
A database storage format
Software designed to cause harm
explanation: Malware is software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorized access.

text: What is phishing?
options: A process for optimizing database queries for faster execution time
A performance testing method for measuring page load speed metrics
A technique for compressing images to reduce file size on servers
A fraudulent attempt to obtain sensitive information by deception
explanation: Phishing tricks users into revealing sensitive information through fake communications.

text: What is encryption?
options: Creating backup copies of important files on the server
Deleting sensitive data from the database permanently
Compressing files to reduce their storage size on disk
Converting data into an unreadable format for protection
explanation: Encryption converts data into an unreadable format that requires a key to decrypt.

text: How does CSRF attack work and how can it be prevented?
options: CSRF intercepts network traffic between client and server to modify data
CSRF brute-forces user passwords by trying common combinations repeatedly
CSRF exploits stored XSS to steal cookies from authenticated user sessions
CSRF tricks authenticated users into making unintended requests using tokens
explanation: CSRF tricks users into making unintended requests; prevented with anti-CSRF tokens.

text: What is SQL injection and how is it prevented?
options: SQL injection inserts malicious SQL through input fields prevented by parameterization
SQL injection overloads the database server with excessive query requests at once
SQL injection exploits weak passwords to gain direct database admin access rights
SQL injection inserts CSS into database queries to alter page styling output
explanation: SQL injection inserts malicious SQL via inputs; prevented by parameterized queries.

text: What is the purpose of Content Security Policy headers?
options: To restrict which sources of content the browser is allowed to load safely
To define the content type of the HTTP response body sent to the client
To set the content encoding for compressing response data before transfer
To specify the content language for automatic translation of web page text
explanation: CSP headers restrict which sources can load scripts, styles, and other resources.

text: How does the SameSite cookie attribute improve security?
options: It encrypts cookie values so they cannot be read by client-side JavaScript
It forces cookies to expire after a single use preventing session replay attacks
It restricts cookies to same-site requests reducing CSRF attack vulnerability
It limits cookie storage to a maximum of one kilobyte for security purposes
explanation: SameSite restricts cookies to same-site requests, mitigating CSRF attacks.

text: What is the principle of least privilege in web security?
options: Giving administrators the highest level of access to all system resources
Providing equal access levels to all users regardless of their actual role
Granting only the minimum permissions necessary to perform required tasks
Restricting all users from accessing any system resources by default only
explanation: Least privilege grants only the minimum permissions needed to perform a task.

text: How does CORS work and why is it important?
options: CORS controls which origins can access resources preventing unauthorized access
CORS caches origin responses to reduce the number of server requests needed
CORS encrypts all cross-origin requests to protect data during transmission
CORS compresses resources to reduce bandwidth and improve page load speed
explanation: CORS controls cross-origin resource access through HTTP headers.

text: What is the purpose of input sanitization?
options: To clean user input by removing or escaping potentially harmful characters
To format input data for visually appealing display in the user interface
To validate that input matches a specific data type before database storage
To compress input data to reduce the payload size of form submission data
explanation: Input sanitization removes or escapes harmful characters to prevent injection attacks.

text: What is a brute force attack?
options: An attack that tries all possible combinations to guess passwords or keys
An attack that floods the server with traffic to make it unavailable to users
An attack that intercepts data in transit between the client and the server
An attack that exploits a known vulnerability in the server operating system
explanation: A brute force attack systematically tries all possible combinations to crack passwords.

text: How does rate limiting help prevent security attacks?
options: It limits the size of HTTP response bodies to prevent data exfiltration attacks
It limits the CSS animation frame rate to prevent browser resource exhaustion
It restricts the number of database columns that can be queried simultaneously
It restricts request frequency from a single source to prevent abuse and attacks
explanation: Rate limiting restricts request frequency, preventing brute force and DDoS attacks.

text: What is the purpose of security headers like X-Frame-Options?
options: To specify the maximum number of iframes allowed on a single web page view
To set the visual frame and border styling for the web page layout design
To define the frame rate for CSS animations and JavaScript timer intervals
To prevent clickjacking by controlling whether a page can be framed by others
explanation: X-Frame-Options prevents clickjacking by controlling page framing.

text: How does a timing attack exploit web applications and how is it mitigated?
options: It measures response time differences to infer secret data using constant-time comparison
It exploits expired TLS certificates during the brief window before renewal occurs
It sends requests at precisely timed intervals to bypass rate limiting protections
It attacks during specific time zones when server administrators are offline
explanation: Timing attacks measure response time variations; mitigated with constant-time comparisons.

text: What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption in web security?
options: Symmetric uses two keys while asymmetric uses a single shared secret key only
Symmetric uses one shared key while asymmetric uses a public-private key pair
Symmetric is more secure than asymmetric in all possible use case scenarios today
Symmetric is for encrypting files while asymmetric is for encrypting databases
explanation: Symmetric uses one shared key; asymmetric uses a public-private key pair.

text: How does a JWT token compromise affect application security and what mitigations exist?
options: JWT compromise only affects the single request where the token was intercepted
JWT tokens cannot be compromised because they are encrypted with AES-256 always
JWT compromise requires the attacker to also know the user password to exploit
JWT compromise grants access until expiration mitigated by short TTL and rotation
explanation: Compromised JWTs grant access until expiration; mitigated by short TTLs and token rotation.

text: What is the purpose of a Web Application Firewall and how does it differ from a network firewall?
options: WAF operates at the network layer while network firewall operates at application layer
WAF only protects static websites while network firewall protects dynamic applications
WAF replaces the need for HTTPS encryption while network firewall enhances encryption
WAF protects against application layer attacks while network firewall handles lower layers
explanation: WAF protects against application-layer attacks (XSS, SQLi); network firewalls handle lower layers.

text: How does certificate pinning enhance TLS security and what are its drawbacks?
options: It pins SSL certificates to specific IP addresses for geographic restriction purposes
It pins certificates in DNS records requiring DNSSEC for validation of every connection
It binds a host to specific certificates preventing man-in-middle with CA compromise risk
It permanently pins a certificate preventing any future certificate rotation or updates
explanation: Certificate pinning binds hosts to specific certificates but makes rotation difficult.

text: What is a supply chain attack in web development and how can it be mitigated?
options: A vulnerability in the CDN supply chain that serves corrupted static asset files
A compromised dependency that injects malicious code mitigated by lockfiles and audits
An attack on the physical supply chain of server hardware during manufacturing
An attack where the attacker buys the domain name of a deprecated npm package
explanation: Supply chain attacks compromise dependencies; mitigated by lockfiles, audits, and SRI.

text: How does Subresource Integrity protect against CDN compromises?
options: It checks that CDN servers have valid SSL certificates before loading any resources
It encrypts all resources loaded from CDNs to prevent eavesdropping during transfer
It creates a backup copy of CDN resources on the origin server for failover support
It verifies resource integrity using cryptographic hashes ensuring files are untampered
explanation: SRI uses cryptographic hashes to verify that CDN-served resources haven't been tampered with.

text: What is the OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow and why is PKCE important?
options: OAuth uses basic auth and PKCE adds an extra password layer for security measure
Auth code flow exchanges code for tokens and PKCE prevents authorization code interception
OAuth only works with social login providers and PKCE extends it to email passwords
Auth code flow is deprecated and PKCE is the entirely new protocol that replaced it
explanation: Authorization code flow exchanges codes for tokens; PKCE prevents code interception attacks.

text: How does a zero-day vulnerability differ from a known vulnerability in web security?
options: Zero-day is a vulnerability discovered on the first day the software was released
Zero-day is an unknown exploit with no patch while known vulnerabilities have fixes
Zero-day means the vulnerability has zero impact while known ones have high impact
Zero-day vulnerabilities can only affect server software and not client applications
explanation: Zero-day vulnerabilities have no available patch; known vulnerabilities have published fixes.

text: What is the purpose of security response headers like Permissions-Policy?
options: To set file system permissions for static assets served by the web server only
To control which browser features and APIs a document is allowed to use safely
To define which team members have permission to deploy code to production servers
To manage database user permissions through HTTP headers sent with each request
explanation: Permissions-Policy controls which browser features and APIs the document can use.

text: What is page load time?
options: Time to write the page code
Time to deploy the page code
Time for a page to fully display
Time for DNS to resolve domain
explanation: Page load time is the duration from navigation start to the page being fully displayed.

text: What does minification do?
options: Encrypts the source code files
Adds comments to the code
Compiles code to machine code
Removes unnecessary characters
explanation: Minification removes whitespace, comments, and shortens names to reduce file size.

text: What is caching?
options: Encrypting data in the database
Storing data for faster future access
Deleting old files from server
Compiling code before deployment
explanation: Caching stores data temporarily for faster access on subsequent requests.

text: What does image compression do?
options: Converts images to HTML format
Adds metadata to image files
Increases image resolution quality
Reduces image file size for web
explanation: Image compression reduces file size while maintaining acceptable visual quality.

text: What is lazy loading?
options: Slowing down the server to reduce power usage
Loading resources only on the server side cache
Deferring resource loading until they are needed
Loading all resources immediately on page start
explanation: Lazy loading defers loading resources until they're needed, improving initial load.

text: What does a CDN help with?
options: Writing server code
Database management
Code compilation speed
Faster content delivery
explanation: A CDN delivers content from servers geographically closer to users.

text: What is browser caching?
options: Encrypting browser data for privacy and security use
Compiling web pages into native apps for offline use
Deleting browser history to free up disk space now
Storing web resources locally in the browser for reuse
explanation: Browser caching stores resources locally to avoid re-downloading on repeat visits.

text: What does the defer attribute do on a script tag?
options: It loads the script after HTML parsing is complete first
It delays the script execution by a fixed time in seconds
It defers CSS loading until after JavaScript execution
It prevents the script from running in production builds
explanation: defer loads the script after HTML parsing is complete.

text: What is bandwidth?
options: The maximum file size a server can store on disk
The number of pages a website has in its sitemap
The visual width of a web page layout in pixels
The data transfer capacity of a network connection
explanation: Bandwidth is the maximum data transfer rate of a network connection.

text: What is a performance bottleneck?
options: A CSS property that controls animation speed on a page
A feature that limits the overall performance of a system
A tool used to measure the speed of a web application
A server configuration for limiting maximum user connections
explanation: A bottleneck is a component that limits overall system performance.

text: What are Core Web Vitals and why are they important?
options: They are Google metrics measuring loading, interactivity, and visual stability
They are CSS properties that control the visual appearance of web page text
They are server health metrics measuring CPU and memory usage of web servers
They are JavaScript functions that validate form data before submission occurs
explanation: Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) measure user experience and affect search rankings.

text: How does code splitting improve web application performance?
options: It splits the database into multiple tables for faster query execution speed
It divides server code into microservices for independent deployment of each
It splits CSS into multiple files that load sequentially one after another
It breaks code into smaller chunks loaded on demand reducing initial bundle size
explanation: Code splitting breaks code into chunks loaded on demand, reducing initial load size.

text: What is the critical rendering path?
options: The sequence of steps the browser takes to convert HTML, CSS, JS to pixels
The path data takes through the network from server to client browser device
The path a user follows through a website to complete a conversion goal task
The route HTTP requests take through load balancers to reach the origin server
explanation: The critical rendering path is the sequence the browser follows to render a page.

text: How does tree shaking reduce bundle size?
options: It removes duplicate database records to reduce storage and query overhead
It prunes old log entries from the server to free up storage disk space use
It eliminates dead code and unused exports from the final JavaScript bundle
It removes unused CSS classes from stylesheets during the build compilation
explanation: Tree shaking removes unused code exports from the final bundle during build.

text: What is the purpose of preloading and prefetching resources?
options: To load critical resources early or fetch future resources in idle time ahead
To download and install browser plugins required by the web page for function
To pre-validate form data on the server before the user submits the form
To preview web pages before navigating to them in the browser tab interface
explanation: Preloading fetches critical resources early; prefetching loads likely-needed future resources.

text: How does HTTP caching work with Cache-Control headers?
options: Cache-Control headers define which HTTP methods are allowed for each resource
Cache-Control headers control the maximum number of concurrent HTTP connections
Cache-Control headers specify how and how long browsers and CDNs cache resources
Cache-Control headers encrypt cached data to prevent unauthorized access today
explanation: Cache-Control headers specify caching directives like max-age and no-cache.

text: What is render-blocking and how can it be avoided?
options: Database locks that block writes avoided by using optimistic concurrency control patterns
Elements that block user scrolling avoided by removing fixed position CSS properties
Resources that block initial render avoided by async scripts and critical CSS inlining
Server processes that block response avoided by using asynchronous database queries
explanation: Render-blocking resources delay rendering; avoided by async scripts and inlining critical CSS.

text: How does image format selection affect web performance?
options: SVG is the best format for photographs while JPEG is best for vector graphics use
PNG is always the smallest format and should be used for all web image content
Modern formats like WebP and AVIF offer better compression than JPEG and PNG today
All image formats perform identically with no difference in file size or quality
explanation: WebP and AVIF offer better compression than JPEG/PNG, reducing file size.

text: What is the purpose of a service worker for performance optimization?
options: To compile JavaScript code into machine code for faster execution on the client
To cache resources and serve them offline improving load times on repeat visits
To run CSS animations in a separate thread for smoother visual performance output
To distribute server workload across multiple threads for parallel request handling
explanation: Service workers cache resources and serve them from cache, improving repeat visit performance.

text: How does DNS prefetching improve page load performance?
options: It caches the entire web page at the DNS level for instant loading on next visit
It compresses DNS queries to reduce the bandwidth used during name resolution
It prefetches all images on a page before the user scrolls down to view them
It resolves domain names to IP addresses early reducing DNS lookup latency overall
explanation: DNS prefetching resolves domain names early, reducing DNS lookup latency.

text: How does the PRPL pattern optimize web application loading performance?
options: It Parses HTML, Resolves dependencies, Processes CSS, and Loads JavaScript
It Prioritizes above-fold, Reduces payload, Prefetches links, Lowers latency
It Pre-renders, Re-renders, Pre-loads, and Lazy-loads all page components
It Pushes critical resources, Renders route, Pre-caches, and Lazy-loads rest
explanation: PRPL: Push critical resources, Render initial route, Pre-cache remaining, Lazy-load rest.

text: What is the impact of long tasks on web performance and how are they identified?
options: Long tasks are HTTP requests taking over five seconds to receive a server response
Long tasks are CSS animations exceeding one second that cause janky scrolling
Tasks that write large amounts of data to localStorage causing storage quota errors
Tasks exceeding 50ms block the main thread and are identified via Long Tasks API
explanation: Tasks over 50ms block the main thread; identified via the Long Tasks API and Performance Observer.

text: How does resource prioritization work in the browser and how can developers influence it?
options: Browsers assign priority based on resource type and developers use fetchpriority hints
Browsers load all resources with equal priority and developers cannot change this
Resource priority is determined solely by the order resources appear in the HTML file
Developers must manually specify download order for every single resource on page
explanation: Browsers prioritize by resource type; developers use fetchpriority and preload hints.

text: What is the purpose of the Performance Observer API?
options: To observe network performance and automatically switch to lower quality resources
To asynchronously observe performance entries like marks, measures, and paint timing
To observe and report CSS rendering performance for animation smoothness checks
To monitor server CPU and memory performance from the client browser JavaScript
explanation: Performance Observer asynchronously watches for performance entries like paint timing.

text: How does Brotli compression compare to Gzip for web content delivery?
options: Brotli only works with HTML files while Gzip supports all content types served
Brotli and Gzip produce identical compression ratios for all web content types
Brotli achieves higher compression ratios than Gzip especially for static content
Brotli requires client-side decompression libraries while Gzip is built into browsers
explanation: Brotli achieves better compression ratios than Gzip, especially for static text content.

text: What is layout thrashing and how can it be prevented?
options: Layout thrashing occurs when too many CSS classes are applied to a single element
It occurs when multiple CSS files conflict with each other causing rendering delays
It occurs when repeated reads and writes to the DOM force synchronous layout recalculations
Layout thrashing happens when the server sends too many layout instructions in response
explanation: Layout thrashing happens when alternating DOM reads/writes force synchronous layouts.

text: How does the speculation rules API improve navigation performance?
options: It allows prerendering of pages the user is likely to navigate to using rules set
It guesses user input and pre-fills form fields to reduce time to completion
It predicts server load and automatically scales resources before traffic spikes
It speculates which CSS styles will be needed and pre-compiles them for speed
explanation: Speculation rules API prerendrers likely navigation targets for instant page transitions.

text: What is the difference between First Contentful Paint and Largest Contentful Paint?
options: FCP is a server metric while LCP is a client metric and they measure different things
FCP measures when first content renders while LCP measures largest content element render
FCP measures first byte received while LCP measures last byte received from server
FCP only measures text rendering while LCP only measures image rendering on the page
explanation: FCP measures first content render; LCP measures when the largest content element renders.

text: How does edge computing improve web application performance?
options: It runs computation at network edge locations closer to users reducing latency seen
It prioritizes content at the top edge of the page for above-the-fold rendering
It sharpens image edges using advanced algorithms for better visual quality display
It processes data at the edge of the database table for faster query result times
explanation: Edge computing runs code at network edge locations close to users, reducing latency.

text: What is the Interaction to Next Paint metric and why did it replace First Input Delay?
options: INP measures overall responsiveness across all interactions not just the first input event
INP measures network latency to the next server while FID measured to the current server
INP only measures touch interactions while FID measured all types of user input events
INP measures the time between two consecutive paint operations on the canvas element
explanation: INP measures responsiveness across all interactions, providing a fuller picture than FID's first-only.

text: What is unit testing?
options: Testing network connectivity and speed
Testing individual components in isolation
Testing the user interface visual layout
Testing the entire application end to end
explanation: Unit testing tests individual components or functions in isolation.

text: What is a bug in software?
options: A planned software enhancement
A CSS styling improvement idea
An error or defect in the code
A feature request from the user
explanation: A bug is an error or defect in software that causes incorrect behavior.

text: What is the purpose of the browser console?
options: To design database table structures
To view logs, errors, and run JavaScript
To create new web pages from templates
To manage web server configurations
explanation: The browser console shows logs, errors, and allows running JavaScript.

text: What does a linter do?
options: It removes lint from clothing images
It generates test cases from source
It compresses files for deployment
It checks code for style and errors
explanation: A linter analyzes code for stylistic issues and potential errors.

text: What is debugging?
options: Deploying code to production
Finding and fixing code errors
Writing documentation for code
Adding new features to software
explanation: Debugging is the process of finding and fixing errors in code.

text: What is a test case?
options: A special case statement in JavaScript switch expressions
A container for storing test data in the database table
A set of conditions to verify expected software behavior
A type of briefcase used by quality assurance engineers
explanation: A test case defines conditions to verify that software behaves as expected.

text: What tool is commonly used for JavaScript testing?
options: Nginx server
MySQL server
Photoshop
Jest framework
explanation: Jest is a popular JavaScript testing framework.

text: What is a breakpoint in debugging?
options: A network point where data packets are split for transfer
A point in code where execution pauses for inspection work
A CSS property that breaks text into multiple lines on page
A screen width where the responsive layout changes its form
explanation: A breakpoint pauses code execution at a specific line for debugging.

text: What is integration testing?
options: Testing individual functions completely isolated from others
Testing the application under extreme load conditions only
Testing how different modules or components work together
Testing CSS integration with HTML elements on the web page
explanation: Integration testing verifies that different modules work correctly together.

text: What is a test suite?
options: A single test case for one function
A collection of related test cases
A CSS framework for test pages
A hotel suite for QA team members
explanation: A test suite is a collection of related test cases grouped together.

text: What is the difference between unit testing and end-to-end testing?
options: Unit tests are for backend code only while end-to-end tests are for frontend code only
Unit tests verify individual components while E2E tests verify complete user workflows
Unit tests run in production while end-to-end tests run only in development environments
Unit tests are automated while end-to-end tests can only be performed manually today
explanation: Unit tests verify isolated components; E2E tests verify complete user workflows.

text: What is test-driven development and how does it work?
options: Tests are driven by user feedback collected through surveys after each deployment
Tests are written after the feature is complete to verify correctness of the code
Tests are written before the code and the code is written to make them pass first
Tests are automatically generated from documentation without developer involvement
explanation: TDD writes tests first, then writes code to pass them, then refactors.

text: What is mocking in testing and why is it useful?
options: Creating fake visual mockups of web page designs for client presentation review
Mocking the server response time to test how the frontend handles slow networks
Creating duplicate databases for testing queries without affecting production data
Replacing real dependencies with simulated objects for isolated unit testing work
explanation: Mocking replaces real dependencies with controlled simulations for isolated testing.

text: How does code coverage measurement help in testing?
options: It calculates the physical storage space required to store all test result files
It counts the number of developers who have reviewed the code before deployment
It measures the percentage of code executed during tests identifying untested areas
It measures how much of the codebase is covered by documentation comments today
explanation: Code coverage measures what percentage of code is executed by tests.

text: What is snapshot testing and when is it useful?
options: Recording network request snapshots for offline testing of API integrations work
Creating snapshots of database state before and after running migration scripts
Taking screenshots of web pages and comparing them manually for visual changes
Capturing component output and comparing against stored reference for regressions
explanation: Snapshot testing captures component output and compares it to stored references.

text: What is the purpose of continuous integration in testing?
options: To continuously monitor production servers and restart them when tests fail today
To continuously integrate new CSS styles into the existing stylesheet files daily
To automatically run tests on every code change ensuring quality before merging
To integrate third-party testing tools into the development environment manually
explanation: CI automatically runs tests on every code change, catching issues before merging.

text: How does Cypress differ from Selenium for web testing?
options: Cypress runs in the browser with native access while Selenium uses remote drivers
Cypress only supports unit testing while Selenium supports all types of testing
Cypress requires Python while Selenium only works with Java programming language
Cypress is a server-side testing tool while Selenium tests only client-side code
explanation: Cypress runs inside the browser with native access; Selenium uses remote WebDriver protocol.

text: What is regression testing?
options: Testing new features that are being developed for the first time in the sprint
Reducing the number of test cases by removing redundant or obsolete ones today
Verifying that existing functionality still works after code changes are made
Testing the application by reverting to a previous version of the codebase
explanation: Regression testing ensures existing functionality isn't broken by new changes.

text: What is the testing pyramid and what does it recommend?
options: Many UI tests at top, fewer integration in middle, fewest unit tests at bottom
Only end-to-end tests are needed because they cover all lower levels automatically
Equal numbers of unit, integration, and end-to-end tests across all three levels
Many unit tests at the base, fewer integration in the middle, fewest E2E on top
explanation: The testing pyramid recommends many unit tests, fewer integration, and fewest E2E tests.

text: What is the purpose of accessibility testing?
options: To verify that the website is usable by people with various disabilities needs
To verify that the website source code is accessible through view-source option
To test whether the website can be accessed from different geographic regions
To test how fast the website loads on different network connection speed levels
explanation: Accessibility testing ensures websites are usable by people with disabilities.

text: How does visual regression testing work and what tools support it?
options: It manually reviews visual design changes in a design review meeting with stakeholders
It tests CSS regression properties by comparing computed styles programmatically
It runs CSS animations in reverse to test visual consistency during undo operations
It captures and compares screenshots pixel-by-pixel using tools like Percy or Chromatic
explanation: Visual regression testing compares screenshots pixel-by-pixel using tools like Percy.

text: What is property-based testing and how does it differ from example-based testing?
options: Property-based only tests object properties while example-based tests entire components
Property-based uses specific examples while example-based uses random test input data
Property-based requires no assertions while example-based requires explicit assertions
Property-based generates random inputs to verify invariants rather than specific examples
explanation: Property-based testing generates random inputs to verify properties hold for all cases.

text: How does contract testing ensure compatibility between microservices?
options: It verifies that service interactions conform to agreed-upon API contracts defined
It verifies that all microservices use the same programming language and framework
It ensures all services are deployed in the same data center for low latency access
It tests the legal contracts associated with third-party service license agreements
explanation: Contract testing verifies that services adhere to agreed API contracts at their boundaries.

text: What is mutation testing and how does it evaluate test suite quality?
options: It introduces small code changes to verify that tests catch defects effectively now
It tests how the application handles mutated or corrupted database records safely
It mutates the test code itself to verify it can be easily maintained over time
It tests the application with different user input mutations for edge case coverage
explanation: Mutation testing introduces code changes (mutants) to verify tests detect them.

text: How does chaos engineering apply to web application testing?
options: It intentionally introduces failures to test system resilience and recovery ability
It generates random database schemas to test ORM compatibility across configurations
It tests the application with randomly generated CSS styles for visual robustness
It creates chaotic user interface layouts to test responsive design breakpoints
explanation: Chaos engineering intentionally injects failures to test system resilience.

text: What is the difference between synthetic and real user monitoring?
options: Synthetic measures server metrics while RUM measures only client-side rendering speed
Synthetic uses scripted tests in controlled environments while RUM tracks actual users
Synthetic only works in development while RUM only works in staging environments used
Synthetic monitors real users while RUM uses automated scripts for testing performance
explanation: Synthetic uses scripted tests; RUM tracks actual user interactions in production.

text: How does test parallelization improve CI/CD pipeline performance?
options: It parallelizes code compilation and testing in the same process for efficiency gains
It runs all tests sequentially on multiple servers for consistent result comparison
It creates parallel copies of the database for each test to run in complete isolation
It runs independent tests simultaneously across workers reducing total execution time
explanation: Test parallelization runs independent tests simultaneously, reducing CI/CD pipeline time.

text: What is the role of test fixtures and factories in maintaining test data?
options: Fixtures are physical hardware devices used for hardware-in-the-loop testing only
Fixtures store CSS styling for test result report pages displayed in the browser
Fixtures and factories create consistent reusable test data reducing setup duplication
Factories generate random production data for populating staging environments daily
explanation: Fixtures and factories create consistent, reusable test data, reducing setup duplication.

text: How does fuzz testing help discover security vulnerabilities?
options: It applies fuzzy logic to determine which test cases should be prioritized first
It feeds random or malformed input to find crashes and unexpected behavior patterns
It tests the application with fuzzy or blurred images to check image processing
It tests fuzzy string matching algorithms for accuracy in search functionality
explanation: Fuzz testing feeds random/malformed inputs to discover crashes and vulnerabilities.

text: What is the testing trophy pattern and how does it differ from the testing pyramid?
options: The trophy inverts the pyramid putting the most end-to-end tests at the base level
The trophy eliminates unit tests entirely and focuses only on end-to-end test coverage
The trophy has more integration tests emphasizing confidence per test written effort
The trophy adds a new layer of performance tests that the pyramid does not include
explanation: The testing trophy emphasizes integration tests for higher confidence per test.

text: What is deployment in web development?
options: Writing the initial source code for a new feature in the application
Designing the user interface mockups in a visual design tool app
Making a website available on a server for users to access online
Testing the application for bugs before releasing it to the public
explanation: Deployment is the process of making a website available on a server.

text: What is a domain name?
options: A server-side scripting language
A database management system tool
A human-readable web address name
A CSS layout framework for grids
explanation: A domain name is a human-readable address that maps to a server IP.

text: What does FTP stand for?
options: File Transfer Protocol
Front Template Page
Full Type Platform
Fast Text Processing
explanation: FTP stands for File Transfer Protocol for transferring files.

text: What is shared hosting?
options: Hosting where you own the entire physical server exclusively for your site
Hosting where multiple websites share a single server and its resources
Hosting where files are shared across multiple CDN edge server locations
Hosting where developers share code through version control repositories
explanation: Shared hosting hosts multiple websites on a single server sharing resources.

text: What is a SSL certificate?
options: A certificate showing a website passed all accessibility compliance tests
A certification for web developers from an accredited training institution
A license required to operate a web hosting business in certain countries
A digital certificate that enables HTTPS encryption for a website domain
explanation: An SSL certificate enables HTTPS by authenticating the site and encrypting data.

text: What is GitHub Pages?
options: A backend API framework tool
A static site hosting service
A CSS component library app
A database hosting service
explanation: GitHub Pages is a free static site hosting service from GitHub.

text: What is a VPS?
options: Virtual Private Server
Variable Processing System
Visual Page Styling
Virtual Protocol Service
explanation: VPS stands for Virtual Private Server, a virtualized hosting environment.

text: What does DNS do?
options: Compiles JavaScript for production use
Translates domain names to IP addresses
Styles web page elements with colors
Creates database tables automatically
explanation: DNS translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses.

text: What is cloud hosting?
options: Hosting in a weather station
Hosting through email services
Hosting files on a local computer
Hosting on virtual cloud servers
explanation: Cloud hosting uses virtual servers in the cloud for scalable hosting.

text: What is Netlify used for?
options: Hosting and deploying web applications
Writing server-side Python programs
Managing SQL databases for web apps
Creating mobile applications for iOS
explanation: Netlify is a platform for hosting and deploying web applications.

text: What is the difference between continuous integration and continuous deployment?
options: CI is for frontend code only while CD handles backend deployment pipeline exclusively
CI deploys code to production while CD runs tests and merges code into the main branch
