CI requires manual approval while CD runs automatically without any human intervention
CI merges code and runs tests while CD automatically deploys passing code to production
explanation: CI automates merging and testing; CD automates deployment of passing code to production.

text: What is a Docker container and how does it help deployment?
options: A CSS container element that wraps other elements for layout and styling purposes
A physical container that stores server hardware in a data center facility rack
A lightweight isolated environment that packages an app with all its dependencies
A database container that stores related tables together for organized data access
explanation: Docker containers package applications with dependencies for consistent deployment.

text: What is the purpose of a reverse proxy in deployment?
options: To reverse engineer the deployment process for rollback to a previous version
To sit before application servers handling load balancing, SSL, and caching tasks
To create a proxy copy of the production database for development testing use
To reverse the order of CSS rules applied to elements for specificity overrides
explanation: A reverse proxy handles load balancing, SSL termination, and caching before app servers.

text: What is a deployment pipeline?
options: A pipeline that transfers database records from staging to production environments
A physical network cable that connects development machines to production servers
An automated process that builds, tests, and deploys code through defined stages
A logging pipeline that streams application logs to a monitoring dashboard view
explanation: A deployment pipeline automates the build, test, and deploy process through stages.

text: How does blue-green deployment reduce downtime?
options: It colors the deployment logs blue for success and green for errors encountered
It deploys to both environments simultaneously and picks the faster one to serve
It maintains two identical environments switching traffic to reduce deployment risk
It uses blue and green colored servers to visually distinguish environments easily
explanation: Blue-green deployment maintains two environments, switching traffic for zero-downtime deploys.

text: What is infrastructure as code and why is it important?
options: Creating infrastructure diagrams using code for documentation purposes exclusively
Building physical server infrastructure using 3D printing from code specifications
Writing CSS that defines the visual infrastructure layout of web page elements used
Managing infrastructure through code files enabling version control and repeatability
explanation: IaC manages infrastructure through code, enabling version control and reproducibility.

text: What is the purpose of environment variables in deployment?
options: To define the physical environment like temperature where servers are housed today
To set environment-specific font sizes for development versus production display
To store configuration that varies between environments without code changes needed
To set CSS custom properties that change based on the user browser environment
explanation: Environment variables store deployment-specific configuration without hardcoding.

text: What is a container registry?
options: A registry of physical shipping containers used for server hardware delivery tasks
A repository for storing and distributing container images like Docker Hub service
A system that registers new CSS container queries for responsive component design
A database that registers all running containers on a single host machine system
explanation: A container registry stores and distributes container images for deployment.

text: How does canary deployment work?
options: It uses canary-colored warning messages to alert users about upcoming changes ahead
It gradually rolls out changes to a small subset of users before full deployment
It deploys to a canary server that only team members can access for final testing
It deploys the entire application to all users simultaneously with no phased rollout
explanation: Canary deployment gradually rolls out changes to a small user subset before full release.

text: What is the purpose of health checks in deployed applications?
options: To monitor the physical health of server hardware like CPU temperature and disk state
To verify that application components are running correctly and able to serve traffic
To check the health of developers working on the application project regularly
To validate that HTML passes W3C health standards for accessibility compliance test
explanation: Health checks verify that application components are running correctly.

text: How does GitOps differ from traditional CI/CD approaches?
options: GitOps eliminates the need for any version control system in the deployment process
GitOps only works with GitHub and cannot be used with GitLab or Bitbucket platforms
GitOps requires manual approval for every deployment while CI/CD is fully automated
GitOps uses Git as the single source of truth for declarative infrastructure and apps
explanation: GitOps uses Git as the single source of truth for infrastructure and application state.

text: What is the purpose of a service mesh in container orchestration?
options: It manages service-to-service communication with observability, security, and routing
It generates a mesh network of CDN nodes for distributed static asset delivery use
It creates a visual mesh overlay of all CSS grid layouts on the deployed web page
It meshes multiple databases together into a single unified query interface for apps
explanation: A service mesh manages inter-service communication with observability and security.

text: How does immutable infrastructure improve deployment reliability?
options: It replaces servers entirely instead of updating them ensuring consistent clean state
It freezes all application features and prevents any new deployments from occurring
It makes database records immutable so they cannot be updated or deleted by anyone
It prevents any developer from modifying the infrastructure code in the repository
explanation: Immutable infrastructure replaces servers rather than updating them, ensuring consistency.

text: What is the purpose of a deployment rollback strategy?
options: To create a backup of the deployment pipeline configuration for disaster recovery
To gradually roll forward by deploying incrementally to larger user group segments
To roll back CSS changes to a previous design version when stakeholders disagree
To quickly revert to a previous working version when a deployment causes issues
explanation: Rollback strategies enable quick reversion to a previous working version on issues.

text: How does Kubernetes handle self-healing of deployed applications?
options: It automatically rewrites application code to fix bugs detected in production use
It heals network connections between services by automatically rerouting all traffic
It monitors pod health and restarts or replaces failed pods to maintain desired state
It repairs corrupted database tables by restoring from the most recent backup file
explanation: Kubernetes monitors pod health and automatically restarts or replaces failed pods.

text: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical pod autoscaling in Kubernetes?
options: Horizontal only scales CPU while vertical only scales memory allocation for each pod
Horizontal adjusts resources while vertical adds replicas for load distribution work
Horizontal adds more pod replicas while vertical adjusts pod resource limits upward
Horizontal requires manual triggers while vertical scales automatically based on load
explanation: HPA adds/removes pod replicas; VPA adjusts CPU/memory resources of individual pods.

text: How does a progressive delivery strategy improve deployment safety?
options: It progressively increases the server CPU speed during deployment for faster builds
It delivers CSS and JavaScript progressively using chunked transfer encoding only
It deploys all changes at once but progressively enables monitoring after deployment
It gradually exposes changes to users with feature flags, canaries, and observability
explanation: Progressive delivery gradually exposes changes using feature flags and canary releases.

text: What is the purpose of chaos engineering in production deployments?
options: To generate chaotic test data that stress-tests database storage capacity under load
To randomly assign deployment tasks to team members for knowledge sharing purposes
To intentionally inject failures to verify system resilience and recovery mechanisms
To create chaotic deployment schedules that test team response time capabilities
explanation: Chaos engineering injects failures to verify system resilience in production.

text: How does multi-region deployment improve application availability?
options: It deploys across geographic regions providing failover and reduced latency globally
It runs multiple instances of the database on the same server for read performance
It deploys the application to multiple folders within a single server for redundancy
It creates multiple copies of the source code repository in different version controls
explanation: Multi-region deployment provides geographic redundancy, failover, and reduced latency.

text: What is the twelve-factor app methodology and how does it influence deployment?
options: It mandates twelve team members minimum for any cloud deployment project management
It requires twelve separate deployment stages before code can reach production servers
It limits applications to twelve files maximum for simplified deployment management
It defines twelve principles for building scalable, maintainable cloud-native applications
explanation: The twelve-factor methodology defines principles for building scalable cloud-native apps.

text: What is a REST API?
options: A database query language spec
A CSS framework for styling
A JavaScript testing framework
An API following REST principles
explanation: A REST API follows REST architectural principles using HTTP methods.

text: What HTTP method retrieves data?
options: DELETE method
PUT method
POST method
GET method
explanation: GET retrieves data from a server.

text: What is JSON used for in APIs?
options: Exchanging data between client and server systems
Styling web page elements with colors and fonts
Compiling JavaScript code for production deployment
Creating database tables and defining their schemas
explanation: JSON is the standard format for exchanging data in APIs.

text: What does an API endpoint represent?
options: A JavaScript variable type name
A CSS selector targeting elements
A database table column value
A specific URL for a resource
explanation: An API endpoint is a specific URL where a resource can be accessed.

text: What HTTP method creates a new resource?
options: GET method
DELETE method
PUT method
POST method
explanation: POST creates a new resource on the server.

text: What is an API key?
options: A database encryption algorithm name
A CSS property for keyboard events
A token for authenticating API requests
A physical key for server room access
explanation: An API key is a token used to authenticate requests to an API.

text: What does HTTP status 200 mean?
options: OK / Success
Unauthorized
Not found
Server error
explanation: HTTP 200 means the request was successful.

text: What is a request header?
options: A database table column header
The first line of HTML on a page
A CSS header styling property
Metadata sent with an HTTP request
explanation: Request headers contain metadata about the HTTP request.

text: What does CORS stand for?
options: Cache Optimized Resource Store
Cross-Origin Resource Sharing
Central Origin Response System
Client Object Request Service
explanation: CORS stands for Cross-Origin Resource Sharing.

text: What is a webhook?
options: A CSS animation hook for elements
An automated HTTP callback notification
A JavaScript debugging hook method
A hook for hanging server cables
explanation: A webhook is an automated HTTP callback that sends data when an event occurs.

text: What is the difference between PUT and PATCH HTTP methods?
options: PUT is idempotent while PATCH is not and cannot be safely retried on failure
PUT replaces the entire resource while PATCH partially updates specific fields
PUT creates a new resource while PATCH deletes an existing resource from server
PUT is for files only while PATCH works with JSON data responses exclusively
explanation: PUT replaces the entire resource; PATCH partially updates specific fields.

text: How does API versioning help maintain backward compatibility?
options: It tracks how many times each API endpoint has been called for billing usage
It creates version backups of the API database for disaster recovery purposes
It allows multiple API versions to coexist so old clients continue to function
It versions the CSS stylesheets used in API documentation pages for consistency
explanation: API versioning lets multiple versions coexist, preventing breaking changes for clients.

text: What is the purpose of API rate limiting?
options: To restrict API responses to a fixed number of records per database query
To control request frequency preventing abuse and ensuring fair resource usage
To limit the number of API endpoints that can be created in a single project
To limit the CSS animation frame rate for API loading spinner components
explanation: Rate limiting controls request frequency to prevent abuse and ensure fair usage.

text: How does pagination work in REST APIs?
options: It splits API request payloads across multiple HTTP connections for parallelism
It creates separate HTML pages for each API response sent to the client browser
It automatically paginates database tables into fixed-size partitions for storage
It returns data in smaller chunks using offset, limit, or cursor-based approaches
explanation: API pagination returns data in chunks using offset/limit or cursor-based approaches.

text: What is the purpose of API documentation tools like Swagger?
options: To automatically generate CSS styles for API response rendering in browsers
To provide interactive documentation for exploring and testing API endpoints
To generate client-side JavaScript code from server-side API handler functions
To create database documentation from SQL schema definitions automatically
explanation: Swagger/OpenAPI provides interactive documentation for API exploration and testing.

text: How do WebSockets differ from REST APIs for real-time communication?
options: WebSockets require API keys while REST APIs use session cookies for authentication
WebSockets only support text data while REST APIs support all content type formats
WebSockets maintain persistent bidirectional connections unlike REST request-response
WebSockets use HTTP request-response while REST uses persistent connections only
explanation: WebSockets provide persistent bidirectional communication; REST uses request-response.

text: What is the purpose of HATEOAS in REST API design?
options: To hash API tokens using advanced encryption standards for security purposes
To include hypermedia links in responses enabling clients to discover actions
To hate on APIs that don't follow REST principles by logging warning messages
To handle asynchronous task execution and observe API server health metrics
explanation: HATEOAS includes hypermedia links in responses so clients can discover available actions.

text: What is an API gateway and what functions does it serve?
options: A single entry point that handles routing, authentication, and rate limiting tasks
A tool that generates API code from visual gateway diagrams designed by teams
A physical gateway device that connects API servers to the internet backbone
A database gateway that translates API calls into SQL queries automatically used
explanation: An API gateway provides a single entry point handling routing, auth, and rate limiting.

text: How does OAuth 2.0 work for API authorization?
options: It requires users to share their passwords directly with third-party applications
It delegates authorization using access tokens without sharing user credentials
It only works for social media APIs and cannot be used for other API services
It encrypts all API responses using a shared secret key between both parties
explanation: OAuth 2.0 delegates authorization via access tokens without sharing user credentials.

text: What is the purpose of idempotency in API design?
options: To ensure that making the same request multiple times produces the same result
To identify duplicate API endpoints and remove them from the server routing table
To verify that API request parameters match the expected data types precisely
To ensure API responses always contain identical formatting and structure layout
explanation: Idempotency ensures repeated identical requests produce the same result without side effects.

text: How does GraphQL handle the over-fetching and under-fetching problems of REST?
options: GraphQL automatically paginates responses to prevent over-fetching of large datasets
GraphQL requires multiple round trips but each returns exactly one field per request
GraphQL lets clients specify exactly which fields they need in a single query request
GraphQL caches all possible response combinations to eliminate unnecessary fetching
explanation: GraphQL lets clients request exactly the fields needed, solving over/under-fetching.

text: What are the tradeoffs between REST, GraphQL, and gRPC for API design?
options: gRPC only works between frontend and backend while REST is only for backend services
REST is always the best choice and GraphQL and gRPC offer no advantages over it
REST is simple and cacheable, GraphQL is flexible for clients, gRPC is fast for services
GraphQL is the fastest protocol while REST and gRPC are slower but more user-friendly
explanation: REST is simple/cacheable, GraphQL offers client flexibility, gRPC excels at service-to-service.

text: How does the BFF (Backend for Frontend) pattern optimize API consumption?
options: It creates dedicated backend services tailored to specific frontend client requirements
It creates a beautiful frontend framework that replaces the need for API calls entirely
It bundles all frontend files and serves them from the backend for faster initial loads
It buffers frontend failures and retries API calls automatically without user awareness
explanation: BFF creates tailored backend services for specific frontend needs, optimizing data delivery.

text: What is the purpose of API circuit breakers and how do they improve reliability?
options: They stop calling failing APIs after threshold breaches preventing cascade failures
They create circular API routes for redundant request handling and load balancing
They break API responses into smaller circuits for parallel processing on the client
They physically disconnect API servers from the network during maintenance windows
explanation: Circuit breakers stop calling failing services after threshold, preventing cascade failures.

text: How does event-driven API design differ from request-response patterns?
options: Event-driven APIs push events to subscribers asynchronously instead of synchronous calls
Event-driven APIs only support read operations and cannot handle data write operations
Event-driven APIs require clients to poll the server continuously for new events data
Event-driven APIs are limited to WebSocket transport and cannot use HTTP protocol at all
explanation: Event-driven APIs push events to subscribers asynchronously, decoupling producers and consumers.

text: What is the purpose of API schema validation and how does it prevent issues?
options: It validates CSS schemas applied to API response rendering in the browser viewport
It validates request and response structures against defined schemas catching errors early
It validates server configuration files to ensure API routing is correctly configured
It validates database schemas before running API queries to prevent SQL syntax errors
explanation: Schema validation checks requests/responses against defined structures, catching errors early.

text: How does API versioning through content negotiation work?
options: Clients specify the desired API version in the URL path like /api/v1/resource endpoint
Clients specify the desired version through Accept headers rather than URL path changes
Servers randomly assign API versions to clients based on their geographic location data
Clients download different API versions and choose which one to install on their device
explanation: Content negotiation uses Accept headers to specify API version without URL path changes.

text: What is the purpose of the saga pattern in managing distributed API transactions?
options: It tracks the complete saga or history of all API calls for audit compliance purposes
It creates epic narrative documentation for complex API integration workflows only
It generates sequential API call stories for automated testing of integration scenarios
It coordinates long-running distributed transactions with compensating actions for failures
explanation: The saga pattern coordinates distributed transactions with compensating actions for failures.

text: How does server-sent events differ from WebSockets for real-time API communication?
options: SSE requires a dedicated server while WebSockets work on any standard HTTP web server
SSE only supports binary data while WebSockets only support plain text message formats
SSE is bidirectional over HTTP while WebSockets only support server-to-client messages
SSE provides one-way server-to-client streaming over HTTP while WebSockets are bidirectional
explanation: SSE provides one-way server-to-client streaming over HTTP; WebSockets are full-duplex.

text: What is the purpose of API observability and what does it encompass?
options: It observes API source code changes and generates release notes documentation only
It encompasses logging, metrics, and tracing for understanding API behavior and health
It observes competitor API features and suggests improvements for your own API design
It monitors the visual appearance of API documentation pages for design consistency
explanation: API observability encompasses logging, metrics, and distributed tracing for understanding API health.

text: What is a Progressive Web App?
options: A web app that requires progressive download of all assets before loading
A web app that only works on desktop computers and not on mobile devices
A web app with native-like features such as offline support and installability
A web app that progressively removes features as more users access the site
explanation: A PWA offers native-like features including offline support and installability.

text: What is TypeScript?
options: A CSS preprocessor for styling
A database query language spec
A server hosting platform tool
A typed superset of JavaScript
explanation: TypeScript is a typed superset of JavaScript that compiles to plain JavaScript.

text: What is a static site generator?
options: A tool that generates dynamic server-rendered pages on every request received
A tool that creates static CSS styles without any responsive design support
A tool that builds HTML pages at build time from templates and data sources
A tool that generates static IP addresses for web server hosting purposes
explanation: A static site generator builds HTML pages at build time from templates and content.

text: What is a headless CMS?
options: A CMS with no user interface for content editors or administrators to use
A CMS that only supports headless browser testing and not content editing
A CMS that runs without a server and stores content in browser storage
A CMS that provides content via APIs without a fixed presentation layer
explanation: A headless CMS delivers content via APIs, decoupled from the presentation layer.

text: What is serverless computing?
options: Computing without any servers involved in the entire application stack
Computing that only works when the server is completely turned off today
Running code in cloud functions without managing server infrastructure
A deployment model where code runs directly in the user browser client
explanation: Serverless runs code in cloud functions without managing server infrastructure.

text: What is WebAssembly?
options: A JavaScript framework for building web applications with assembly patterns
A binary format for running compiled code in the browser at near-native speed
A new CSS layout system for assembling web page components together
A server-side assembly language for writing high-performance API endpoints
explanation: WebAssembly is a binary format for running compiled code at near-native speed in browsers.

text: What is a monorepo?
options: A server configuration where all services run on one physical machine only
A single repository containing multiple related projects or packages together
A database that stores all data in a single table without any relationships
A single repository containing only one small microservice application code
explanation: A monorepo is a single repository containing multiple related projects or packages.

text: What is Jamstack?
options: Java, Angular, and MongoDB for building enterprise web applications stack
JavaScript, APIs, and Markup for building modern web applications stack
jQuery, Apache, and MariaDB for building traditional web application stack
JSON, AJAX, and MySQL for building data-driven web applications stack
explanation: Jamstack uses JavaScript, APIs, and Markup for modern web development.

text: What is a design system?
options: A system for automatically generating web page designs from text descriptions
A tool for designing database schemas using visual drag-and-drop interfaces
A CSS framework that generates design tokens from Figma files automatically
A collection of reusable components and guidelines for consistent user interfaces
explanation: A design system is a collection of reusable components and guidelines for consistent UI.

text: What are web components?
options: Server-side components that generate HTML on the server before sending
Components built exclusively using React or Vue frontend framework libraries
CSS animation components that add visual effects to web page elements
Custom reusable HTML elements using native browser APIs and standards
explanation: Web components are custom reusable elements built with native browser standards.

text: How does server-side rendering differ from static site generation?
options: SSR generates static files while SSG renders pages dynamically per request
SSR only works with React while SSG works with all frontend frameworks used
SSR and SSG are identical concepts with no practical differences between them
SSR renders pages on each request while SSG pre-builds pages at build time
explanation: SSR renders on each request; SSG pre-builds pages at build time.

text: What is the purpose of a build tool like Vite or Webpack?
options: To bundle, transform, and optimize source code for production deployment use
To build physical server infrastructure from configuration code specifications
To build database schemas from visual entity relationship diagram models only
To construct CSS layouts from wireframe sketches using AI image recognition
explanation: Build tools bundle, transform, and optimize source code for production.

text: What is the purpose of feature flags in modern web development?
options: To add visual flag icons to features that are new or recently updated for users
To mark CSS features that are not supported in older browsers for fallback use
To enable or disable features dynamically without deploying new code versions
To flag code that contains bugs for prioritized fixing by the development team
explanation: Feature flags enable toggling features on/off without redeploying code.

text: How does edge computing benefit modern web applications?
options: It sharpens the visual edges of images displayed on the web page for clarity
It caches database queries at the edge of the database cluster for faster reads
It processes requests at network edge locations closer to users reducing latency
It adds decorative edge borders to web page components for visual enhancement
explanation: Edge computing processes requests at locations closer to users, reducing latency.

text: What is the purpose of a design token in design systems?
options: A physical token given to designers to access the design tool application suite
A database token that authorizes access to the design system API documentation
A cryptocurrency token used to purchase premium design assets and templates
A named value storing design decisions like colors and spacing for consistency
explanation: Design tokens store design decisions as named values for cross-platform consistency.

text: How do monorepos benefit large-scale web development teams?
options: They automatically generate documentation for all projects in the repository
They force all projects to use the same programming language and framework only
They enable code sharing, atomic changes, and unified tooling across projects
They eliminate the need for any version control branching strategies entirely
explanation: Monorepos enable code sharing, atomic changes, and unified tooling across projects.

text: What is the difference between a library and a framework?
options: Libraries are free and open source while frameworks always require paid licenses
A library is larger than a framework and includes more features out of the box
A library is called by your code while a framework calls your code via inversion
Libraries only work in browsers while frameworks work on both server and client
explanation: A library is called by your code; a framework calls your code through inversion of control.

text: What is the purpose of a CI/CD pipeline in modern web development?
options: To create interactive and creative designs for web page user interface layouts
To continuously inspect and clean database records for data quality maintenance
To continuously integrate CSS changes with HTML changes for consistent styling
To automate building, testing, and deploying code changes reliably and quickly
explanation: CI/CD pipelines automate building, testing, and deploying code changes.

text: How does incremental static regeneration work?
options: It regenerates static pages in the background after a revalidation period expires
It incrementally adds new CSS styles to static pages without rebuilding them all
It regenerates all static pages from scratch on every single user request received
It generates static pages incrementally one at a time during the build process
explanation: ISR regenerates static pages in the background after a specified revalidation period.

text: What is the purpose of a component library like Shadcn or Material UI?
options: To store compiled JavaScript components in a shared library for runtime loading
To create a library catalog of all API endpoints for developer documentation use
To provide pre-built accessible UI components for consistent application styling
To compile custom HTML elements into web components for cross-framework sharing
explanation: Component libraries provide pre-built, accessible UI components for consistent styling.

text: How does partial hydration improve performance compared to full hydration?
options: Partial hydration only hydrates interactive components reducing JavaScript sent to client
Partial hydration pre-renders CSS while full hydration renders it at runtime on client
Partial hydration downloads less HTML while full hydration downloads the complete page
Partial hydration splits the database queries while full hydration runs them all at once
explanation: Partial hydration only hydrates interactive components, reducing JavaScript payload.

text: What is the purpose of React Server Components in the modern web ecosystem?
options: They are server-side templates that generate static HTML without any React features used
They create server-to-server communication channels for microservice architectures only
They render on the server reducing bundle size while keeping client interactivity where needed
They replace all client components entirely and run everything on the server only
explanation: RSC render on the server, reducing bundle size while allowing client interactivity where needed.

text: How does the module federation pattern enable micro-frontend architectures?
options: It creates federated databases where each micro-frontend has its own data storage silo
It federates CSS modules across multiple applications for shared styling consistency
It allows applications to share and load modules from other builds at runtime dynamically
It merges all micro-frontend codebases into a single monolithic bundle for deployment
explanation: Module federation allows apps to share and load modules from other builds at runtime.

text: What is the purpose of observability in modern web applications?
options: To observe CSS rendering performance and generate visual reports for stakeholders
To observe user behavior through screen recording for UX research purposes only
To monitor competitor websites and observe their feature changes for benchmarking
To provide insight into system behavior through logs, metrics, and distributed tracing
explanation: Observability combines logs, metrics, and tracing for insight into system behavior.

text: How does the streaming architecture pattern benefit modern web applications?
options: It streams video content to users using specialized video encoding protocols only
It processes and delivers data incrementally reducing time to first meaningful content
It creates a stream of database backups continuously for disaster recovery purposes
It streams CSS styles progressively so the page styling appears gradually over time
explanation: Streaming delivers data incrementally, reducing time to first meaningful content.

text: What is the difference between build-time and runtime in modern web frameworks?
options: Build-time only applies to CSS while runtime only applies to JavaScript code execution
Build-time requires Node.js while runtime requires a completely different language runtime
Build-time is slower but more secure while runtime is faster but has security vulnerabilities
Build-time generates optimized output ahead of deployment while runtime executes on request
explanation: Build-time generates optimized output before deployment; runtime executes on each request.

text: How does the AI-assisted development trend impact modern web practices?
options: AI development tools are limited to backend development and cannot assist with frontend
AI completely replaces the need for human developers in all web development tasks today
AI tools assist with code generation, review, and debugging while humans provide oversight
AI only works for generating CSS styles and cannot help with JavaScript or HTML coding
explanation: AI tools assist developers with code generation, review, and debugging with human oversight.

text: What is the concept of edge-first architecture in modern web development?
options: Designing buildings with edge-shaped architecture for modern tech company headquarters
Developing applications that only work on Microsoft Edge browser for optimal performance
Designing applications to run primarily at network edge for minimal latency and global scale
Prioritizing the visual edges and borders of UI elements for a modern design aesthetic
explanation: Edge-first architecture runs application logic at the network edge for minimal latency.

text: How does the concept of composability influence modern web architecture?
options: It composes CSS animations by chaining multiple keyframe definitions in sequence only
It composes music and sound effects for web applications using the Web Audio API
It creates composite database indexes by combining multiple columns for query speed
It builds systems from interchangeable independent modules that can be freely combined
explanation: Composability builds systems from interchangeable modules that can be freely combined.

text: What are the tradeoffs of adopting a serverless architecture for web applications?
options: Serverless only works for static websites and cannot handle any dynamic content at all
Serverless eliminates all costs since you never pay for idle server time or any resources
Serverless has no tradeoffs and is always the best choice for every web application type
Serverless reduces ops overhead but introduces cold starts and vendor lock-in challenges
explanation: Serverless reduces operations overhead but introduces cold starts and potential vendor lock-in.

text: What is a domain name in web development?
options: A human-readable address for a website
A programming language for servers
A type of database management system
A tool for editing HTML files
explanation: A domain name is a human-readable address like example.com that maps to a server's IP address.

text: Which of the following is an example of a markup language?
options: Python
Java
HTML
explanation: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is a markup language used to structure content on the web.

text: What is the purpose of JavaScript in web development?
options: To structure web page content
To define database schemas
To add interactivity and dynamic behavior
To manage server hardware
explanation: JavaScript is used to add interactivity, dynamic content, and behavior to web pages.

text: What is the purpose of an SSL certificate on a website?
options: To speed up JavaScript execution
To encrypt data transmitted between the browser and the server
To store user passwords
To compress CSS files
explanation: An SSL certificate enables HTTPS encryption, securing data transmitted between the user's browser and the web server.

text: What is a search engine?
options: A tool that finds and displays web pages based on user queries
A server that stores website files
A programming language for building websites
A device that connects computers to the internet
explanation: A search engine is software that indexes web pages and returns results based on user search queries.

text: Which file extension is commonly used for CSS files?
options: .html
.js
.css
.php
explanation: CSS files use the .css extension to store stylesheet rules for web pages.

text: What is the World Wide Web?
options: A system of interlinked hypertext documents accessed via the internet
A type of programming language
A hardware component inside computers
A local network connecting two computers
explanation: The World Wide Web is a system of interlinked hypertext documents and resources accessed through the internet using browsers.

text: What does a text editor do in web development?
options: Compiles code into machine language
Allows developers to write and edit code
Hosts websites on the internet
Manages database records
explanation: A text editor is a tool that allows developers to write, edit, and save code files.

text: What is an internet browser's primary function?
options: To compile programming languages
To render and display web pages
To store website databases
To write server-side code
explanation: A browser's primary function is to retrieve, render, and display web pages for users.

text: What is the difference between the internet and the World Wide Web?
options: They are the same thing
The internet is the network infrastructure while the web is a service that runs on it
The web is a type of hardware device
The internet only supports email
explanation: The internet is the global network infrastructure, while the World Wide Web is a service built on top of it for accessing hyperlinked documents.

text: What is the role of a web framework in development?
options: It replaces the need for HTML entirely
It provides pre-built tools and structure to speed up development
It is only used for mobile app development
It manages internet service provider connections
explanation: A web framework provides pre-built components, conventions, and tools that help developers build web applications faster and more consistently.

text: What is the purpose of developer tools in modern browsers?
options: To create new websites automatically
To inspect, debug, and profile web pages in real time
To replace the need for a code editor
To manage web hosting accounts
explanation: Browser developer tools allow developers to inspect HTML/CSS, debug JavaScript, monitor network requests, and profile performance.

text: What is a web application compared to a traditional website?
options: A web application is always built with Python
A web application provides interactive functionality beyond static content
A web application cannot use HTML
A web application runs without a browser
explanation: A web application provides interactive functionality like forms, user accounts, and data manipulation, going beyond static content display.

text: What is the MEAN stack?
options: MongoDB, Express, Angular, Node.js
MySQL, Ember, Apache, Nginx
Markdown, ESLint, AWS, Netlify
MongoDB, Electron, Ansible, NPM
explanation: MEAN stands for MongoDB, Express.js, Angular, and Node.js, forming a full-stack JavaScript development environment.

text: What is cross-browser compatibility?
options: A tool for compressing images
Ensuring a website works correctly across different web browsers
A method for encrypting web traffic
A framework for building mobile apps
explanation: Cross-browser compatibility means ensuring a website functions and appears consistently across different browsers like Chrome, Firefox, and Safari.

text: What is the purpose of a favicon on a website?
options: To improve server performance
To display a small icon in the browser tab representing the site
To encrypt user data
To validate form inputs
explanation: A favicon is a small icon displayed in the browser tab, bookmarks bar, and other UI elements to visually represent a website.

text: What does the term 'deployment' mean in web development?
options: Writing the initial HTML code
Making a website available on a live server for public access
Installing a text editor
Creating a database backup
explanation: Deployment is the process of publishing a website or application to a production server so it is accessible to users on the internet.

text: What is a progressive web application?
options: A desktop-only application
A web app that uses modern APIs to deliver native-like experiences
A website that only works in Chrome
A server-side rendering technique
explanation: A PWA uses modern web APIs to deliver app-like features such as offline access, push notifications, and home screen installation.

text: What is the purpose of semantic versioning in web projects?
options: To style web pages
To communicate the nature of changes in software releases using major.minor.patch format
To compress JavaScript files
To manage DNS records
explanation: Semantic versioning (SemVer) uses a major.minor.patch format to communicate whether changes are breaking, feature additions, or bug fixes.

text: What is the difference between a library and a framework in web development?
options: They are identical concepts
A library provides specific functions you call, while a framework controls the overall flow of your application
A framework is always smaller than a library
Libraries only work on the server side
explanation: A library offers reusable functions you call as needed, while a framework provides the overall structure and calls your code (inversion of control).

text: What is the main trade-off when choosing between a monolithic architecture and a microservices architecture for a web application?
options: Monoliths are always slower than microservices
Microservices add operational complexity but allow independent scaling and deployment of services
Monoliths cannot use databases
Microservices require a single programming language
explanation: Microservices enable independent scaling and deployment but introduce operational complexity in networking, deployment, and data consistency.

text: How does WebAssembly complement JavaScript in modern web development?
options: It completely replaces JavaScript in all browsers
It allows near-native performance for compute-intensive tasks while JavaScript handles DOM interaction
It is only used for styling web pages
It is a server-side-only technology
explanation: WebAssembly provides near-native execution speed for compute-heavy tasks like image processing or games, while JavaScript remains ideal for DOM manipulation and UI logic.

text: What problem does the micro-frontend architecture solve in large-scale web development?
options: It reduces CSS file size
It allows independent teams to develop, deploy, and maintain separate parts of a frontend application
It eliminates the need for a backend
It only works with Angular applications
explanation: Micro-frontends allow different teams to independently develop, test, and deploy distinct sections of a web frontend, improving scalability in large organizations.

text: What is the significance of the critical rendering path in web performance?
options: It determines how the server compiles JavaScript
It is the sequence of steps the browser takes to convert HTML, CSS, and JS into rendered pixels on screen
It only affects mobile devices
It refers to the deployment pipeline
explanation: The critical rendering path is the sequence of steps (DOM construction, CSSOM construction, render tree, layout, paint) the browser follows to render a page.

text: What is the purpose of edge computing in the context of modern web applications?
options: To increase the physical size of data centers
To run computations closer to the end user, reducing latency and improving responsiveness
To replace all client-side JavaScript
To eliminate the need for caching
explanation: Edge computing processes requests at servers geographically closer to users, reducing round-trip latency and improving application responsiveness.

text: How does the concept of progressive enhancement differ from graceful degradation?
options: They are the same strategy
Progressive enhancement builds from a baseline experience upward, while graceful degradation starts with full features and falls back
Progressive enhancement only applies to CSS
Graceful degradation does not work in modern browsers
explanation: Progressive enhancement starts with a basic functional experience and layers on advanced features, while graceful degradation starts with the full experience and handles failures.

text: What is the role of a build tool like Webpack or Vite in modern web development?
options: It hosts websites on the internet
It bundles, transforms, and optimizes source files for production deployment
It is used exclusively for database management
It replaces the need for version control
explanation: Build tools bundle modules, transpile modern syntax, optimize assets, and enable features like code splitting and tree shaking for production-ready output.

text: Why is accessibility (a11y) considered a fundamental aspect of professional web development?
options: It only improves search engine rankings
It ensures web content is usable by people with disabilities and often improves usability for all users
It is only required by European law
It slows down website performance
explanation: Accessibility ensures people with disabilities can use web content, and accessible practices often improve usability, SEO, and overall user experience for everyone.

text: What is the difference between Server-Sent Events and WebSockets for real-time communication?
options: They are identical protocols
SSE is unidirectional from server to client over HTTP, while WebSockets provide full-duplex bidirectional communication
SSE is faster than WebSockets in all cases
WebSockets cannot send text data
explanation: SSE provides one-way server-to-client streaming over HTTP, while WebSockets establish a persistent full-duplex connection for bidirectional real-time communication.

text: What is the impact of third-party scripts on web application performance and security?
options: Third-party scripts have no impact on performance
They can increase page load time, introduce security vulnerabilities, and affect Core Web Vitals
They always improve website speed
They are automatically sandboxed by browsers
explanation: Third-party scripts can block rendering, increase bundle size, introduce XSS risks, and negatively impact Core Web Vitals if not carefully managed.

text: What is a server in web architecture?
options: A computer that provides resources or services to other computers
A type of web browser
A CSS framework
A programming language
explanation: A server is a computer or software that provides resources, data, or services to clients over a network.

text: What is a URL used for?
options: To style web pages
To locate and access a specific resource on the web
To compile JavaScript
To manage database tables
explanation: A URL (Uniform Resource Locator) specifies the address of a resource on the web, including the protocol, domain, and path.

text: What does the status code 500 indicate in HTTP?
options: Successful request
Page not found
Internal server error
Redirect to another page
explanation: HTTP status code 500 indicates an internal server error, meaning something went wrong on the server side.

text: What is a request in the client-server model?
options: Data sent from the client to the server asking for a resource or action
A CSS rule applied to a web page
A JavaScript function call
A database table definition
explanation: A request is a message sent from a client to a server asking for data, a resource, or to perform an action.

text: What is a response in the client-server model?
options: A CSS animation effect
Data sent from the server back to the client after processing a request
A type of JavaScript variable
A method of creating HTML elements
explanation: A response is the data or message the server sends back to the client after processing its request.

text: What protocol does HTTPS use for encryption?
options: FTP
TLS/SSL
SMTP
DNS
explanation: HTTPS uses TLS (Transport Layer Security) or its predecessor SSL to encrypt communication between client and server.

text: What is the default port for HTTPS?
explanation: The default port for HTTPS is 443, while HTTP uses port 80.

text: What does the HTTP DELETE method do?
options: Creates a new resource
Retrieves data from the server
Removes a specified resource
Updates part of a resource
explanation: The HTTP DELETE method requests the removal of a specified resource from the server.

text: What is a domain name system (DNS) record?
options: An entry in a DNS database that maps a domain to information like an IP address
A JavaScript variable declaration
A CSS property for fonts
An HTML form element
explanation: A DNS record is an entry that provides mapping between domain names and various types of data, most commonly IP addresses.

text: What is the purpose of an HTTP header?
options: To display visible content on the page
To provide metadata about the request or response
To create database connections
To compile source code
explanation: HTTP headers carry metadata such as content type, caching directives, authentication tokens, and other information about the request or response.

text: What is the purpose of a load balancer in web architecture?
options: To compress images on the server
To distribute incoming network traffic across multiple servers
To write JavaScript code
To manage DNS records
explanation: A load balancer distributes incoming requests across multiple servers to ensure no single server is overwhelmed and to improve reliability.

text: What is the difference between a forward proxy and a reverse proxy?
options: They are the same thing
A forward proxy acts on behalf of clients while a reverse proxy acts on behalf of servers
Forward proxies are faster
Reverse proxies are only used for caching
explanation: A forward proxy sits between clients and the internet, acting on behalf of clients, while a reverse proxy sits in front of servers, handling incoming requests on behalf of backend servers.

text: What is the role of caching in web architecture?
options: To permanently delete old data
To store frequently accessed data closer to the user to reduce latency and server load
To encrypt data in transit
To create new database tables
explanation: Caching stores copies of frequently accessed data at various levels (browser, CDN, server) to reduce response times and server load.

text: What is the role of DNS in web architecture?
options: To style web pages
To translate human-readable domain names into IP addresses that computers use to locate servers
To compress HTTP responses
To manage database connections
explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) translates domain names like example.com into IP addresses, enabling browsers to locate the correct server for a website.

text: What is the purpose of the HTTP PATCH method?
options: To delete a resource entirely
To retrieve all resources
To apply partial modifications to a resource
To create a new resource from scratch
explanation: The HTTP PATCH method is used to apply partial modifications to a resource, unlike PUT which replaces the entire resource.

text: What is a content delivery network (CDN)?
options: A tool for writing HTML code
A geographically distributed network of servers that delivers content from the nearest location to the user
A type of programming language
A database management system
explanation: A CDN is a network of servers distributed across locations that caches and serves content from the server nearest to the user, reducing latency.

text: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous communication in web architecture?
options: They are identical approaches
Synchronous blocks until a response is received while asynchronous allows other operations to continue
Synchronous is always faster
Asynchronous cannot be used with HTTP
explanation: Synchronous communication blocks the caller until a response arrives, while asynchronous communication allows processing to continue without waiting.

text: What does a status code in the 2xx range indicate?
options: Client error
Server error
Successful request
Redirect
explanation: HTTP 2xx status codes indicate that the request was successfully received, understood, and processed by the server.

text: What is the role of an API gateway in a distributed system?
options: To store static files
To act as a single entry point that routes requests to appropriate backend services
To write CSS styles
To manage version control
explanation: An API gateway acts as a single entry point for clients, routing requests to the correct backend services and handling cross-cutting concerns like authentication and rate limiting.

text: What is the purpose of the HTTP Cache-Control header?
options: To define the page title
To specify caching directives for both requests and responses
To set the content language
To redirect users to a different page
explanation: The Cache-Control header specifies caching policies such as max-age, no-cache, and no-store that control how responses are cached by browsers and intermediaries.

text: What is the split-brain problem in distributed web systems?
options: A CSS rendering issue
When network partitions cause parts of a distributed system to operate independently with conflicting states
A JavaScript memory leak pattern
A browser compatibility issue
explanation: Split-brain occurs when network partitions cause nodes in a distributed system to diverge, each believing it is the authoritative source, potentially leading to data conflicts.

text: How does the QUIC protocol improve web performance compared to TCP?
options: QUIC is slower but more secure than TCP
QUIC eliminates head-of-line blocking and reduces connection establishment latency by combining transport and encryption handshakes
QUIC only works on local networks
QUIC replaces HTTP entirely
explanation: QUIC uses UDP to eliminate TCP's head-of-line blocking and merges transport and TLS handshakes into a single round trip, reducing connection setup time.

text: What is the purpose of the saga pattern in distributed architectures?
options: To optimize CSS rendering
To manage distributed transactions by breaking them into a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions
To compress network traffic
To handle DNS resolution
explanation: The saga pattern manages distributed transactions by executing a sequence of local transactions, with compensating transactions to undo changes if a step fails.

text: What is the difference between strong consistency and eventual consistency in distributed systems?
options: They are the same model
Strong consistency guarantees all nodes see the latest data immediately while eventual consistency allows temporary divergence
Strong consistency is always faster
Eventual consistency prevents data replication
explanation: Strong consistency ensures every read returns the most recent write, while eventual consistency allows temporary stale reads but guarantees convergence over time.

text: What is the purpose of connection pooling in web architecture?
options: To create new database connections for every request
To maintain a pool of reusable connections to reduce the overhead of establishing new connections
To encrypt all network traffic
To manage CSS class names
explanation: Connection pooling maintains a set of pre-established connections that can be reused, avoiding the overhead of creating and tearing down connections for each request.

text: How does the event-driven architecture pattern benefit web applications?
options: It makes all operations synchronous
It decouples services through asynchronous event publishing and consumption, improving scalability and resilience
It eliminates the need for databases
It only works with a single server
explanation: Event-driven architecture decouples producers and consumers through events, enabling independent scaling, loose coupling, and better resilience to failures.

text: What is the role of a service mesh in modern web architecture?
options: To create HTML templates
To manage service-to-service communication, providing observability, security, and traffic control
To replace the frontend framework
To store static files
explanation: A service mesh handles service-to-service communication with features like mTLS, load balancing, circuit breaking, and distributed tracing without modifying application code.

text: What problem does the Command Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS) pattern address?
options: CSS specificity conflicts
It separates read and write models to optimize each independently for different performance and scaling requirements
It manages DNS records
It compresses HTTP responses
explanation: CQRS separates the read (query) and write (command) models of an application, allowing each to be optimized, scaled, and evolved independently.

text: What is the purpose of request idempotency in web architecture?
options: To make all requests faster
To ensure that making the same request multiple times produces the same result without unintended side effects
To encrypt request payloads
To compress response bodies
explanation: Idempotency ensures that repeated identical requests produce the same result, which is crucial for safely retrying failed operations in distributed systems.

text: How does the sidecar proxy pattern work in distributed web architectures?
options: It replaces the main application entirely
It deploys a helper process alongside each service instance to handle cross-cutting concerns like networking and observability
It is a CSS layout technique
It manages browser cookies
explanation: The sidecar pattern deploys a proxy alongside each service to handle networking, security, and observability, allowing the service to focus on business logic.

text: What does the <!DOCTYPE html> declaration do?
options: It creates a new paragraph
It declares the document type as HTML5
It adds a comment to the page
It links a CSS stylesheet
explanation: The <!DOCTYPE html> declaration tells the browser that the document is an HTML5 document.

text: Which HTML tag is used for creating a numbered list?
options: <ul>
<ol>
<li>
<dl>
explanation: The <ol> (ordered list) tag creates a numbered list in HTML.

text: What is the purpose of the <hr> tag in HTML?
options: To create a heading
To insert a horizontal rule (line)
To make text italic
To add a hyperlink
explanation: The <hr> tag creates a horizontal rule, which appears as a line used to separate content.

text: Which HTML tag is used to display an image?
options: <picture>
<photo>
<image>
explanation: The tag is used to embed an image in an HTML page.

text: What does the <em> tag do in HTML?
options: Creates a line break
Emphasizes text, typically displayed in italics
Makes text bold
Adds a hyperlink
explanation: The <em> tag indicates emphasized text, which browsers typically render in italics.

text: Which attribute specifies the source of an image in the tag?
options: href
link
src
url
explanation: The src attribute specifies the URL path to the image file to be displayed.

text: What is the purpose of the <footer> tag in HTML5?
options: To create a navigation menu
To define the footer section of a document or section
To insert a form
To add a heading
explanation: The <footer> tag defines the footer of a document or section, typically containing copyright, contact, or navigation information.

text: Which HTML element is used to define a table header cell?
options: <td>
<th>
<tr>
<thead>
explanation: The <th> tag defines a header cell in an HTML table, which is typically bold and centered by default.

text: What does the <header> tag represent in HTML5?
options: The main content area
Introductory content or a group of navigational aids
A form input field
A table column
explanation: The <header> tag represents introductory content, typically containing headings, navigation, or a site logo.

text: Which tag is used to create a text input field in a form?
options: <textarea>
<input>
<select>
<button>
explanation: The <input> tag is used to create various form controls including text input fields.

text: What is the difference between the <section> and <article> elements?
options: They are identical elements
A section groups related content while an article represents self-contained, independently distributable content
Section is for navigation only
Article cannot contain headings
explanation: A <section> groups thematically related content, while an <article> represents self-contained content that could be independently syndicated.

text: What is the purpose of the autocomplete attribute on form inputs?
options: To validate the input value
To allow browsers to suggest previously entered values for the field
To make the input read-only
To add placeholder text
explanation: The autocomplete attribute controls whether the browser can suggest previously entered values, improving form-filling efficiency.

text: What does the <datalist> element do in HTML5?
options: Creates a data table
Provides a predefined list of suggestions for an input element
Defines a database connection
Generates a numbered list
explanation: The <datalist> element provides a list of predefined options that appear as autocomplete suggestions for an associated input element.

text: What is the difference between the <script> tag placed in <head> versus at the end of <body>?
options: There is no difference
Scripts in <head> may block HTML parsing while scripts before </body> allow the page to render first
Scripts in <head> execute slower
Scripts in <body> cannot access the DOM
explanation: Scripts in <head> can block page rendering during download and execution, while scripts at the end of <body> allow the HTML to parse and render before executing.

text: What is the purpose of the <main> element in HTML5?
options: To create a sidebar
To represent the dominant content of the body, unique to the document
To define a footer
To embed external content
explanation: The <main> element represents the primary content of the document, which should be unique and not duplicated across pages.

text: What does the pattern attribute do on an input element?
options: It sets the input's visual style
It specifies a regular expression that the input value must match for validation
It creates a placeholder text
It disables the input field
explanation: The pattern attribute specifies a regular expression that the input's value is checked against during form submission for client-side validation.

text: What is the difference between the <link> and <a> tags?
options: They are interchangeable
<link> defines relationships between the document and external resources while <a> creates clickable hyperlinks
<link> is used for images
<a> is only for email links
explanation: The <link> tag defines relationships with external resources like stylesheets, while <a> creates navigable hyperlinks within the page content.

text: What is the role of the <noscript> element in HTML?
options: To remove JavaScript from the page
To provide alternative content for users whose browsers do not support or have disabled JavaScript
To add comments to code
To optimize images
explanation: The <noscript> element defines content that is displayed when JavaScript is not available or is disabled in the user's browser.

text: What does the loading attribute on images and iframes control?
options: The image resolution
Whether the resource is loaded immediately or deferred until it nears the viewport
The image format
The image border style
explanation: The loading attribute supports 'eager' (load immediately) and 'lazy' (defer loading until the element is near the viewport), improving performance.

text: What is the purpose of the <output> element in HTML5 forms?
options: To create a text input
To display the result of a calculation or user action
To submit the form
To define form labels
explanation: The <output> element represents the result of a calculation or user action, often used with JavaScript to display computed values.

text: How does the <portal> element differ from an <iframe> for embedding content?
options: They are identical elements
Portals allow seamless navigation to the embedded content and can be activated to become the top-level page
Portals cannot load external URLs
Iframes are deprecated in HTML5
explanation: The <portal> element embeds content like an iframe but enables seamless navigation, allowing the embedded page to be activated as the top-level browsing context.

text: What is the purpose of the Declarative Shadow DOM in HTML?
options: To add CSS animations
To allow server-side rendering of shadow DOM content using HTML-only syntax without JavaScript
To manage form validation
To create responsive images
explanation: Declarative Shadow DOM allows shadow roots to be expressed in HTML markup, enabling server-side rendering of web components without requiring JavaScript for initial rendering.

text: How does the blocking attribute on script and style elements affect rendering?
options: It has no effect on rendering
It explicitly indicates which rendering operations should be blocked until the resource is processed
It deletes the element from the DOM
It compresses the resource
explanation: The blocking attribute explicitly declares that certain rendering operations (like 'render') should be blocked until the linked resource has been fetched and processed.

text: What is the purpose of the rel='modulepreload' link type?
options: To load CSS modules
To preload JavaScript modules and their dependencies for faster execution when needed
To create module-scoped styles
To define database modules
explanation: The rel='modulepreload' link type preloads ES module scripts and their dependency trees, ensuring they are available instantly when imported.

text: How does the is attribute enable customized built-in elements?
options: It sets the element's ID
It extends a built-in HTML element with custom behavior by associating it with a custom element class
It changes the element's display property
It removes default browser styling
explanation: The 'is' attribute allows a built-in element like <button is='my-button'> to be upgraded with custom element behavior while retaining its native semantics and accessibility.

text: What is the purpose of the ElementInternals API in custom elements?
options: To style elements with CSS
To allow custom elements to participate in forms, manage accessibility states, and interact with browser internals
To create database connections
To handle routing
explanation: ElementInternals allows custom elements to participate in form submission, set ARIA states, manage form validation, and interact with the browser's accessibility tree.

text: How does the importmap script type improve module loading in HTML?
options: It imports CSS modules
It allows developers to control module specifier resolution, mapping bare specifiers to URLs without a build step
It creates image maps
It generates sitemaps
explanation: Import maps let developers define mappings from bare module specifiers to URLs, enabling imports like 'import x from "lodash"' without requiring a bundler.

text: What is the purpose of the Sanitizer API in the context of HTML?
options: To remove all JavaScript from a page
To safely parse and sanitize untrusted HTML strings before inserting them into the DOM, preventing XSS attacks
To compress HTML files
To validate form inputs
explanation: The Sanitizer API provides a built-in browser mechanism to safely sanitize untrusted HTML, removing dangerous elements and attributes to prevent XSS attacks.

text: What role does the Navigation API play in modern single-page applications?
options: It manages CSS navigation menus
It provides a standardized way to intercept and handle navigation events, replacing History API workarounds
It creates breadcrumb components
It manages database migrations
explanation: The Navigation API provides a modern, standardized interface for intercepting navigations, managing history entries, and handling transitions in SPAs.

text: How does the Speculation Rules API improve page navigation performance?
options: It guesses which CSS to apply
It allows developers to specify rules for prefetching or prerendering future navigations based on link patterns
It creates animated page transitions
It manages service workers
explanation: The Speculation Rules API lets developers define JSON-based rules that tell the browser which pages to prefetch or prerender, dramatically reducing navigation latency.

text: Which CSS property controls the size of text?
options: text-size
font-weight
font-size
text-height
explanation: The font-size property controls the size of text in CSS.

text: What does the display: block property do?
options: Hides the element
Makes the element take up the full width available
Makes the element inline
Removes the element from the page
explanation: The display: block property makes an element a block-level element, taking up the full available width and starting on a new line.

text: How do you apply CSS styles directly to an HTML element?
options: Using an external stylesheet
Using the style attribute on the element
Using a JavaScript file
Using a server-side script
explanation: Inline styles are applied directly to an HTML element using the style attribute, e.g., style='color: red;'.

text: Which property changes the background image of an element?
options: bg-image
background-image
image-bg
back-img
explanation: The background-image property sets one or more background images for an element.

text: What does the border property do in CSS?
options: Adds space inside the element
Creates a line around the element
Changes the text color
Sets the element's width
explanation: The border property adds a visible border line around an element, with customizable width, style, and color.

text: Which CSS property makes text italic?
options: text-decoration
font-weight
font-style
text-transform
explanation: The font-style property is used to make text italic by setting its value to 'italic'.

text: What does the color property in CSS affect?
options: The background color of an element
The text color of an element
The border color
The shadow color
explanation: The color property sets the foreground color of an element's text content.

text: How do you link an external CSS file to an HTML document?
options: Using the <style> tag
Using the <link> tag in the <head> section
Using the <script> tag
Using the <css> tag
explanation: An external CSS file is linked using <link rel='stylesheet' href='style.css'> in the <head> section of the HTML document.

text: Which CSS property sets the height of an element?
options: size
height
tall
length
explanation: The height property sets the height of an element's content area.

text: What is a CSS class selector?
options: A selector that targets elements by their tag name
A selector that targets elements by a class name using a dot prefix
A selector that targets elements by their ID
A selector that targets all elements
explanation: A class selector uses a dot (.) followed by the class name to target all elements with that class, e.g., .my-class { }.

text: What is the difference between relative and absolute units in CSS?
options: There is no difference
Relative units like em and % scale based on context while absolute units like px have a fixed size
Absolute units are always larger
Relative units only work in flexbox
explanation: Relative units (em, rem, %) scale relative to parent elements or viewport, while absolute units (px, pt) maintain a fixed measurement regardless of context.

text: What does the position: fixed property do?
options: Positions the element relative to its parent
Positions the element relative to the browser viewport, staying in place during scrolling
Removes the element from the document flow
Makes the element invisible
explanation: position: fixed positions an element relative to the viewport, so it stays in the same position even when the page is scrolled.

text: What is the purpose of the CSS opacity property?
options: To set the element's border width
To control the transparency level of an element
To change the font weight
To set the z-index
explanation: The opacity property sets the transparency of an element, with values from 0 (fully transparent) to 1 (fully opaque).

text: How does the CSS cascade determine which styles are applied?
options: Styles are applied randomly
Styles are resolved based on specificity, source order, and importance
Only the last stylesheet wins
Only inline styles are applied
explanation: The cascade resolves style conflicts using origin and importance, specificity of selectors, and source order to determine which rules take precedence.

text: What does the text-decoration property control?
options: The font size of text
Decorative lines on text such as underline, overline, or line-through
The text alignment
The letter spacing
explanation: The text-decoration property adds or removes decorative lines on text, including underline, overline, line-through, and their styles.

text: What is the purpose of the CSS transform property?
options: To change an element's color
To apply 2D or 3D transformations like rotate, scale, skew, or translate to an element
To set the element's position
To add a border
explanation: The transform property applies visual transformations such as rotation, scaling, translation, and skewing without affecting the document layout.

text: What is the difference between position: sticky and position: fixed?
options: They behave identically
Sticky toggles between relative and fixed based on scroll position while fixed always stays relative to the viewport
Sticky only works on images
Fixed does not work in modern browsers
explanation: position: sticky behaves as relative until a scroll threshold is reached, then becomes fixed, while position: fixed is always relative to the viewport.

text: What does the CSS line-height property control?
options: The width of a line
The vertical space between lines of text
The number of lines visible
The horizontal spacing of characters
explanation: The line-height property sets the vertical distance between lines of text, affecting readability and spacing.

text: How do CSS pseudo-elements like ::before and ::after work?
options: They create new HTML elements in the DOM
They insert generated content before or after an element's actual content using CSS alone
They modify JavaScript events
They add attributes to HTML tags
explanation: The ::before and ::after pseudo-elements insert generated content styled by CSS before or after an element's content without adding elements to the HTML.

text: What is the purpose of the CSS outline property?
options: To set the background color
To draw a line outside the element's border without affecting layout
To add inner padding
To change the font style
explanation: The outline property draws a line outside the element's border. Unlike border, it does not take up space or affect the element's layout dimensions.

text: How does the CSS paint API allow custom rendering of element backgrounds?
options: It uses JavaScript to generate images dynamically as CSS backgrounds through registered paint worklets
It only changes text colors
It is a server-side rendering technique
It replaces all CSS with JavaScript
explanation: The CSS Paint API (part of Houdini) lets developers register paint worklets that generate images programmatically, usable as CSS background-image values.

text: What is the difference between logical and physical properties in CSS?
options: They are identical
Logical properties like inline-start and block-end adapt to writing direction and text flow, while physical properties like left and top do not
Logical properties only work in Firefox
Physical properties are deprecated
explanation: Logical properties (margin-inline-start, padding-block-end) adapt to writing mode and direction, making layouts internationalization-friendly, unlike fixed physical properties.

text: How does the CSS typed object model (Typed OM) improve performance over traditional CSSOM access?
options: It has no performance benefit
It provides typed JavaScript objects for CSS values, reducing string parsing overhead and enabling more efficient style manipulation
It replaces CSS entirely
It only works with inline styles
explanation: Typed OM represents CSS values as typed JavaScript objects instead of strings, eliminating repeated parsing and providing better performance for style manipulation.

text: What is the purpose of the CSS @scope rule?
options: To create global styles
To limit the reach of CSS selectors to a specific subtree of the DOM, preventing style leakage
To define animation keyframes
To import external fonts
explanation: The @scope rule scopes CSS selectors to a defined DOM subtree, with optional lower boundaries, preventing styles from leaking outside the intended area.

text: How does the CSS view transitions API work for page transitions?
options: It uses iframes for transitions
It captures screenshots of old and new states, then animates between them using CSS animations on pseudo-elements
It requires WebGL
It only works with React
explanation: The View Transitions API captures the old state, applies DOM changes, captures the new state, and creates animatable pseudo-elements representing both states for smooth transitions.

text: What is the purpose of CSS anchor positioning?
options: To create navigation links
