explanation: Design tokens represent atomic design decisions (colors, spacing, fonts) as named variables, ensuring consistent styling across platforms, products, and tools.

text: How does monorepo tooling like Turborepo improve development workflows?
options: It creates CSS monorepos
It manages multiple packages in one repository with intelligent caching, parallel execution, and dependency-aware task orchestration
It only manages databases
It replaces Git
explanation: Turborepo and similar tools optimize monorepo workflows by caching build outputs, running tasks in parallel, and only rebuilding packages affected by changes.

text: How do web components achieve framework-agnostic reusability?
options: They only work inside React applications
They use browser-native APIs like Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, and HTML Templates to create encapsulated components usable in any framework
They require a special bundler plugin
They depend on a shared CSS library
explanation: Web Components use browser-native standards including Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, and HTML Templates to create encapsulated, reusable UI components that work across any JavaScript framework or vanilla HTML.

text: What is the concept of incremental adoption in modern web frameworks?
options: Installing the entire framework at once
Gradually integrating a framework into an existing application page by page instead of rewriting everything
Adopting only the CSS parts of a framework
Using multiple frameworks simultaneously
explanation: Incremental adoption allows developers to gradually integrate a modern framework into an existing codebase, migrating routes or components one at a time rather than doing a complete rewrite.

text: What is the purpose of TypeScript in modern web development?
options: To replace JavaScript entirely
To add static type checking to JavaScript, catching type errors at compile time and improving developer experience
To type CSS styles
To type database queries
explanation: TypeScript adds optional static type checking to JavaScript, catching type-related bugs at compile time and providing better IDE support, refactoring, and documentation.

text: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic UI updates?
options: Optimistic updates are slower but more accurate
Optimistic updates assume success and update the UI immediately before server confirmation, while pessimistic updates wait for the server response
Pessimistic updates never send data to servers
Optimistic updates only work with REST APIs
explanation: Optimistic UI updates immediately reflect changes in the interface assuming the server will succeed, providing a snappier user experience. Pessimistic updates wait for server confirmation before updating the UI.

text: What is the purpose of Bun as a JavaScript runtime?
options: To bundle CSS files
To provide a fast all-in-one JavaScript runtime, bundler, test runner, and package manager as an alternative to Node.js
To create database bundles
To manage HTML bundles
explanation: Bun is a fast JavaScript runtime that also serves as a bundler, test runner, and package manager, designed as a drop-in Node.js replacement with significantly better performance.

text: What is the purpose of Web Components standards?
options: Standard CSS components
A set of browser APIs (Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, HTML Templates) for creating reusable, encapsulated UI components
Standard database components
Standard server components
explanation: Web Components combine Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, and HTML Templates to create framework-agnostic, encapsulated, reusable UI components that work in any browser.

text: What is the purpose of a build tool like Webpack in modern development?
options: To build CSS from scratch
To bundle, transform, and optimize JavaScript modules and assets for production deployment
To build database schemas
To build HTML templates
explanation: Webpack bundles JavaScript modules and other assets, applying loaders and plugins for transformation, optimization, code splitting, and production builds.

text: What is the purpose of containerization in modern web development?
options: To contain CSS styles
To package applications with all dependencies into isolated containers that run consistently across any environment
To contain database records
To contain HTML elements
explanation: Containerization packages applications with their complete runtime environment into isolated containers, ensuring consistent behavior across development, testing, and production.

text: How does the concept of partial hydration improve performance in modern frameworks?
options: It partially loads CSS
It hydrates only interactive components on a page while leaving static content as plain HTML, reducing JavaScript payload
It partially loads databases
It replaces full page hydration entirely
explanation: Partial hydration selectively adds JavaScript interactivity only to components that need it, leaving the rest as static HTML, dramatically reducing client-side JavaScript.

text: What is the purpose of the edge-first architecture pattern in modern web applications?
options: It adds CSS edge effects
It runs application logic at CDN edge locations by default, falling back to origin servers only when necessary, minimizing latency globally
It only processes edge cases
It replaces all server-side logic
explanation: Edge-first architecture defaults to running computation at CDN edge nodes near users, reducing latency for personalized content and API calls while falling back to origin when needed.

text: How does the signals pattern in modern frameworks differ from React's state model?
options: They are identical patterns
Signals provide fine-grained reactivity by tracking dependencies at the value level, updating only subscribers without re-rendering entire component trees
Signals are slower
React's model is more granular
explanation: Signals track dependencies at individual value access points and notify only direct subscribers, bypassing the component tree re-rendering model that React's state triggers.

text: What is the purpose of the Baseline web platform compatibility initiative?
options: To create CSS baselines
To define a clear set of web features that are interoperable across major browsers, helping developers know what they can safely use
To baseline database performance
To measure JavaScript execution baselines
explanation: Baseline identifies web features that are consistently implemented across all major browsers, giving developers confidence about which APIs and features they can use without polyfills.

text: How does the composability pattern influence modern web architecture?
options: It composes CSS animations
It enables building applications by combining independent, interchangeable modules and services rather than monolithic implementations
It only applies to React components
It replaces all existing patterns
explanation: Composability designs systems from independent, interchangeable modules (composable commerce, headless services) that can be assembled and replaced without affecting the whole system.

text: What is the purpose of the View Transitions API for multi-page applications?
options: To create CSS views
It enables smooth animated transitions between pages in traditional multi-page applications, previously only achievable in SPAs
It only works in SPAs
It replaces CSS animations
explanation: The View Transitions API enables smooth, customizable visual transitions between page navigations in MPAs, bringing SPA-like transition experiences to traditional server-rendered sites.

text: How does AI-assisted development change modern web development workflows?
options: It replaces all developers
AI tools assist with code generation, debugging, review, and documentation while developers provide direction, review outputs, and handle complex decisions
AI writes all CSS
AI manages all databases
explanation: AI-assisted development augments developers with code generation, intelligent autocomplete, bug detection, and documentation while humans provide context, review, and architectural decisions.

text: What is the purpose of the Interop project for web standards?
options: To create interoperable CSS
It is a cross-browser collaboration where major browser vendors commit to improving compatibility on specific web features each year
It only affects JavaScript
It manages database interoperability
explanation: Interop is an annual collaboration between browser vendors (Chrome, Firefox, Safari) to prioritize and improve cross-browser compatibility for specific web platform features.

text: How does the concept of zero-JavaScript pages improve performance in modern frameworks like Astro?
options: It removes all JavaScript from the internet
Pages ship no client-side JavaScript by default, rendering everything as static HTML and only adding JavaScript for explicitly interactive components
It prevents JavaScript from running
It only works for simple pages
explanation: Zero-JS pages render entirely as static HTML with no client JavaScript by default. Interactive components explicitly opt-in to hydration, achieving optimal performance for content-heavy sites.

text: What are the key tradeoffs of adopting a serverless architecture for web applications?
options: There are no tradeoffs
Serverless offers automatic scaling and reduced ops but introduces cold starts, vendor lock-in, execution limits, and challenges with stateful workloads
Serverless is always better
Serverless cannot scale
explanation: Serverless provides auto-scaling and zero server management but introduces cold start latency, vendor lock-in, execution time limits, and difficulties with long-running or stateful processes.

text: What does WWW stand for?
options: Wide World Web
World Wide Web
Web World Wide
World Web Wide
explanation: WWW stands for World Wide Web, a system of interlinked hypertext documents accessed via the internet.

text: Which organization is responsible for maintaining web standards?
options: IEEE
ISO
W3C
IETF
explanation: The World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) develops and maintains web standards such as HTML and CSS.

text: What is the primary role of a web browser?
options: To host websites
To render and display web pages
To store databases
To compile server code
explanation: A web browser interprets HTML, CSS, and JavaScript to render and display web pages to the user.

text: Which side of the client-server model runs in the user's browser?
options: Server-side
Database-side
Client-side
Network-side
explanation: Client-side code runs in the user's web browser, handling the user interface and interactions.

text: A static website displays the same content for every user. What does a dynamic website do?
options: Displays only text
Generates content based on user interaction or data
Does not use HTML
Cannot use CSS
explanation: Dynamic websites generate content on the fly based on user input, database queries, or other logic.

text: Who is credited with inventing the World Wide Web?
options: Vint Cerf
Tim Berners-Lee
Bill Gates
Linus Torvalds
explanation: Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web in 1989 while working at CERN.

text: What does a web server do?
options: Displays web pages to users
Stores and serves web content to clients
Designs website layouts
Manages user passwords only
explanation: A web server stores web content and responds to client requests by serving the appropriate files.

text: What is a URL?
options: A programming language
A type of database
A unique address for a web resource
A web browser plugin
explanation: A URL (Uniform Resource Locator) is the address used to access a specific resource on the web.

text: Which language is used to structure content on a web page?
options: CSS
JavaScript
Python
HTML
explanation: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) provides the structure and content of web pages.

text: Which of the following is an example of a web browser?
options: Apache
Nginx
Google Chrome
Node.js
explanation: Google Chrome is a web browser. Apache and Nginx are web servers, and Node.js is a runtime environment.

text: What is the difference between the internet and the World Wide Web?
options: They are the same thing
The internet is a network infrastructure; the web is a service running on it
The web is hardware; the internet is software
The internet only supports email
explanation: The internet is the global network of interconnected computers, while the web is an application layer service that uses it.

text: Which protocol is the foundation of data communication on the World Wide Web?
options: FTP
SMTP
HTTP
TCP
explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the primary protocol used for transferring web pages on the World Wide Web.

text: What does server-side rendering mean?
options: The browser compiles the page
The server generates the full HTML before sending it to the client
CSS is processed on the server
JavaScript is disabled on the server
explanation: Server-side rendering means the server processes and generates the complete HTML page before sending it to the browser.

text: What is a web standard?
options: A proprietary browser feature
A formal specification that defines how web technologies should work
A JavaScript library
A type of web hosting
explanation: Web standards are formal specifications published by standards bodies like W3C to ensure interoperability across browsers.

text: What is the role of a rendering engine in a browser?
options: To compile JavaScript
To parse and display HTML and CSS content
To manage network requests
To store cookies
explanation: The rendering engine parses HTML and CSS to construct the visual representation of a web page on screen.

text: Which of the following best describes a client-server architecture?
options: Both client and server run on the same machine only
The client requests services, and the server provides them
The server always initiates communication
No network is involved
explanation: In client-server architecture, clients send requests and servers respond with the requested resources or data.

text: What year was the first website published?
explanation: The first website was published in 1991 by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN, describing the World Wide Web project.

text: What is the purpose of the WHATWG?
options: To develop a new operating system
To maintain and evolve the HTML Living Standard
To replace the W3C entirely
To manage DNS servers
explanation: WHATWG (Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group) maintains the HTML Living Standard.

text: Which of these is a characteristic of a dynamic website?
options: Uses only plain HTML files
Content changes based on database queries or user input
Cannot use JavaScript
Pages are pre-built and never change
explanation: Dynamic websites generate pages on the fly using server-side logic, databases, or client-side scripting.

text: What is the Document Object Model (DOM)?
options: A CSS framework
A tree-like representation of an HTML document in memory
A server-side language
A type of database
explanation: The DOM is a programming interface that represents an HTML document as a tree structure, allowing scripts to modify content.

text: What is the critical rendering path in a web browser?
options: The network route between client and server
The sequence of steps the browser takes to convert HTML, CSS, and JS into rendered pixels
The order in which CSS files are loaded
The JavaScript execution order
explanation: The critical rendering path is the sequence of steps (DOM construction, CSSOM, render tree, layout, paint) the browser follows to display a page.

text: Which browser engine does Google Chrome use?
options: Gecko
WebKit
Blink
Trident
explanation: Google Chrome uses the Blink rendering engine, which was forked from WebKit in 2013.

text: What is the purpose of the Web Accessibility Initiative (WAI)?
options: To make websites faster
To develop strategies and guidelines for making the web accessible to people with disabilities
To encrypt web traffic
To standardize JavaScript
explanation: WAI, a W3C initiative, develops guidelines like WCAG to ensure web content is accessible to people with disabilities.

text: What distinguishes a progressive web app (PWA) from a traditional website?
options: PWAs cannot use HTML
PWAs can work offline, send push notifications, and be installed on devices
PWAs require a special browser
PWAs do not use JavaScript
explanation: PWAs use service workers and manifests to provide offline capability, push notifications, and installability.

text: What is the same-origin policy in web browsers?
options: A policy that blocks all third-party scripts
A security mechanism that restricts how documents or scripts from one origin can interact with resources from another
A CSS rendering rule
A rule about file naming conventions
explanation: The same-origin policy restricts cross-origin interactions to prevent malicious sites from accessing data on other domains.

text: What is the difference between client-side rendering (CSR) and server-side rendering (SSR)?
options: CSR uses Python; SSR uses JavaScript
In CSR the browser builds the page using JavaScript; in SSR the server sends fully rendered HTML
There is no difference
SSR does not support CSS
explanation: CSR renders pages in the browser via JavaScript, while SSR generates complete HTML on the server before sending it to the client.

text: Which W3C specification defines how browsers should handle parsing errors in HTML?
options: CSS3 specification
HTML5 parsing algorithm
ECMAScript specification
DOM Level 2
explanation: The HTML5 specification defines a detailed parsing algorithm that specifies how browsers should handle malformed HTML.

text: What is a headless browser?
options: A browser without a bookmark bar
A browser that operates without a graphical user interface
A browser that cannot run JavaScript
A browser only for mobile devices
explanation: A headless browser runs without a visible UI and is commonly used for automated testing and web scraping.

text: What does the term 'web 2.0' primarily refer to?
options: A new version of HTTP
The shift towards user-generated content, interactivity, and social media on the web
A specific browser version
A W3C standard for CSS
explanation: Web 2.0 describes the evolution of the web towards dynamic, interactive platforms with user-generated content and social features.

text: In which layer of the OSI model does HTTP operate?
options: Transport layer
Network layer
Application layer
Data link layer
explanation: HTTP operates at the Application layer (Layer 7) of the OSI model, where user-facing protocols function.

text: What does HTTP stand for?
options: HyperText Transfer Protocol
High Transfer Text Protocol
HyperText Transmission Process
Hyper Transfer Text Protocol
explanation: HTTP stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol, used for transmitting web pages over the internet.

text: What HTTP status code indicates a successful request?
explanation: HTTP status code 200 means OK, indicating the request was successful.

text: What does DNS stand for?
options: Dynamic Name Server
Domain Name System
Data Network Service
Direct Name System
explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses.

text: What HTTP method is used to retrieve data from a server?
options: POST
PUT
DELETE
GET
explanation: The GET method requests data from a specified resource without modifying it.

text: What is the purpose of HTTPS?
options: To speed up page loading
To encrypt communication between the browser and server
To compress HTML files
To cache web pages
explanation: HTTPS uses TLS/SSL encryption to secure data transmitted between the client and server.

text: What are cookies in web development?
options: Server-side scripts
Small pieces of data stored on the client by the browser
Database records
CSS properties
explanation: Cookies are small text files stored in the browser to remember user information between requests.

text: What does the HTTP status code 404 indicate?
options: Server error
Unauthorized access
Resource not found
Redirect
explanation: Status code 404 means the requested resource could not be found on the server.

text: Which HTTP method is used to submit data to a server?
options: GET
HEAD
POST
OPTIONS
explanation: The POST method sends data to the server, typically used for form submissions and creating resources.

text: What is a CDN?
options: Central Data Network
Content Delivery Network
Cloud Database Node
Client Domain Name
explanation: A CDN (Content Delivery Network) distributes content across geographically dispersed servers to improve performance.

text: In the request-response cycle, who initiates the request?
options: The server
The database
The client
The router
explanation: The client (typically a web browser) initiates the request, and the server responds with the requested resource.

text: What is the difference between HTTP and HTTPS?
options: HTTPS is faster than HTTP
HTTPS encrypts data using TLS/SSL while HTTP does not
HTTP supports images but HTTPS does not
There is no difference
explanation: HTTPS adds a layer of encryption via TLS/SSL to protect data in transit, unlike plain HTTP.

text: Which HTTP status code range indicates a client error?
options: 1xx
2xx
3xx
4xx
explanation: 4xx status codes indicate client errors, such as 400 Bad Request or 404 Not Found.

text: What does REST stand for?
options: Remote Execution Standard Technology
Representational State Transfer
Request Evaluation Server Tool
Resource Entry System Type
explanation: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing networked applications using standard HTTP methods.

text: What is the purpose of the HTTP 'Content-Type' header?
options: To specify the length of the response
To indicate the media type of the resource being sent
To authenticate the user
To set caching rules
explanation: The Content-Type header tells the client what type of data is being returned, such as text/html or application/json.

text: What is a session in web development?
options: A CSS class
A server-side mechanism to store user state across multiple requests
A JavaScript function
A type of HTTP method
explanation: A session stores user-specific data on the server to maintain state across multiple HTTP requests.

text: What does the HTTP status code 301 indicate?
options: OK
Bad Request
Moved Permanently
Internal Server Error
explanation: Status code 301 indicates the resource has been permanently moved to a new URL.

text: What is the role of a load balancer?
options: To compress web pages
To distribute incoming network traffic across multiple servers
To encrypt data
To parse HTML
explanation: A load balancer distributes incoming requests across multiple servers to ensure no single server is overwhelmed.

text: Which HTTP method is idempotent and used to update a resource entirely?
options: POST
PATCH
PUT
DELETE
explanation: PUT is idempotent and replaces the entire resource at the specified URL with the request payload.

text: What does DNS resolution do?
options: Compresses web files
Converts domain names into IP addresses
Encrypts HTTP traffic
Validates HTML syntax
explanation: DNS resolution translates a human-readable domain name into the corresponding IP address the browser uses to connect.

text: What is the difference between cookies and local storage?
options: Cookies are larger than local storage
Cookies are sent with every HTTP request; local storage is not
Local storage is server-side; cookies are client-side
There is no difference
explanation: Cookies are automatically sent with every HTTP request to the server, whereas local storage data stays in the browser.

text: What is CORS and why is it needed?
options: A CSS framework for responsive design
A mechanism that allows servers to specify which origins can access their resources
A JavaScript compiler
A type of database query
explanation: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a security mechanism that uses HTTP headers to allow controlled cross-origin requests.

text: What is the difference between HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2?
options: HTTP/2 only supports text
HTTP/2 introduces multiplexing, header compression, and server push
HTTP/1.1 is encrypted; HTTP/2 is not
There is no difference
explanation: HTTP/2 improves performance through binary framing, multiplexed streams, header compression (HPACK), and server push.

text: What is a reverse proxy?
options: A client-side caching tool
A server that sits in front of backend servers and forwards client requests to them
A JavaScript testing framework
A browser extension
explanation: A reverse proxy receives client requests and forwards them to backend servers, providing load balancing, caching, and security.

text: What HTTP header is used to prevent clickjacking attacks?
options: Content-Type
Authorization
X-Frame-Options
Accept-Language
explanation: X-Frame-Options tells the browser whether the page can be displayed in an iframe, preventing clickjacking.

text: In REST, what does it mean for the architecture to be stateless?
options: The server stores session data
Each request must contain all information needed; the server retains no client state between requests
The client caches all data
Stateless means no authentication is used
explanation: Statelessness means each request is self-contained; the server does not store any session state between requests.

text: What is the purpose of the ETag HTTP header?
options: To specify the content type
To provide a cache validator that allows efficient conditional requests
To set cookie values
To redirect the request
explanation: ETag provides a unique identifier for a resource version, enabling conditional requests to avoid unnecessary data transfers.

text: What is HTTP pipelining?
options: Encrypting multiple HTTP requests
Sending multiple HTTP requests on a single TCP connection without waiting for responses
Compressing HTTP headers
A load balancing technique
explanation: HTTP pipelining allows multiple requests to be sent on a single connection before receiving responses, reducing latency.

text: What does the HTTP status code 429 indicate?
options: Not Found
Forbidden
Too Many Requests
Service Unavailable
explanation: Status code 429 indicates the user has sent too many requests in a given timeframe (rate limiting).

text: What is the difference between the PATCH and PUT HTTP methods?
options: PATCH creates a resource; PUT deletes it
PUT replaces the entire resource; PATCH applies partial modifications
They are identical
PATCH is not a valid HTTP method
explanation: PUT replaces the entire resource at the URL, while PATCH applies partial updates to the existing resource.

text: What is the purpose of the Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header?
options: To compress response data
To define approved sources of content, mitigating XSS and data injection attacks
To set cookie expiration dates
To enable CORS
explanation: CSP allows servers to specify which content sources are trusted, helping prevent XSS and other code injection attacks.

text: Which HTML tag is used to create a hyperlink?
options: <link>
<href>
<url>
explanation: The <a> (anchor) tag is used to create hyperlinks to other pages or resources.

text: What does the <title> tag define?
options: The main heading of the page
The title displayed in the browser tab
A tooltip for an element
The page footer
explanation: The <title> tag sets the title shown in the browser's title bar or tab.

text: Which tag is used to insert an image in HTML?
options: <picture>
<image>
<src>
explanation: The tag is used to embed images in an HTML page using the src attribute.

text: What is the correct HTML tag for the largest heading?
options: <heading>
<head>
explanation: The <h1> tag represents the highest level (largest) heading, with <h6> being the smallest.

text: Which HTML element is used for an unordered list?
options: <ol>
<ul>
<li>
<list>
explanation: The <ul> tag creates an unordered (bulleted) list, with each item wrapped in <li> tags.

text: Which attribute specifies the URL of a linked page in an <a> tag?
options: src
link
href
url
explanation: The href attribute of the <a> tag specifies the URL of the page the link points to.

text:
What does the
tag do?
options: Creates a bold text
Inserts a line break
Creates a border
Makes text bigger
explanation:
The
tag inserts a single line break in the content.

text: Which HTML element defines the body of an HTML document?
options: <content>
<main>
<body>
<html>
explanation: The <body> element contains all the visible content of an HTML page.

text: What type of input creates a text field in an HTML form?
options: <input type="text">
<input type="field">
<textfield>
<input type="string">
explanation: The <input type="text"> creates a single-line text input field in a form.

text: Which tag is used to define a table row in HTML?
options: <td>
<th>
<tr>
<table>
explanation: The <tr> tag defines a row within an HTML table.

text: What is semantic HTML?
options: HTML that uses only divs
HTML that uses meaningful tags to describe the content's purpose
HTML with inline styles
HTML without any attributes
explanation: Semantic HTML uses tags like <article>, <nav>, and <section> that clearly describe their content and meaning.

text: Which HTML5 element is used for navigation links?
options: <navigate>
<links>
<nav>
<menu>
explanation: The <nav> element represents a section of navigation links.

text: What is the purpose of the 'alt' attribute on an tag?
options: To set the image size
To provide alternative text for screen readers and when the image fails to load
To add a tooltip
To link to another image
explanation: The alt attribute provides alternative text that describes the image for accessibility and when the image cannot be displayed.

text: What does the <meta charset="UTF-8"> tag specify?
options: The page language
The character encoding used by the document
The page title
The viewport width
explanation: The meta charset tag declares the character encoding (UTF-8) so the browser correctly renders text characters.

text: Which HTML element is used to embed a video?
options: <media>
<movie>
<video>
<embed>
explanation: The <video> element is used to embed video content directly in an HTML page.

text: What is the purpose of the <fieldset> element in forms?
options: To create a text input
To group related form controls together
To submit the form
To add validation
explanation: The <fieldset> element groups related form elements and often includes a <legend> to provide a caption.

text: What does the 'required' attribute do on a form input?
options: Makes the field read-only
Prevents the form from being submitted if the field is empty
Hides the field
Sets a default value
explanation: The required attribute ensures the form cannot be submitted until the user provides a value for that field.

text: Which attribute makes an HTML element uniquely identifiable?
options: class
name
id
type
explanation: The id attribute assigns a unique identifier to an element, which must be unique within the page.

text: What is the difference between <section> and <div>?
options: There is no difference
<section> is semantic and represents a thematic grouping; <div> is a generic container
<div> is semantic; <section> is not
<section> cannot contain other elements
explanation: The <section> element is semantic and represents a thematic grouping of content, while <div> is a non-semantic generic container.

text: Which HTML element is used to define a self-contained piece of content?
options: <div>
<span>
<article>
<section>
explanation: The <article> element represents a self-contained piece of content that could be independently distributed, like a blog post.

text: What is the purpose of ARIA roles in HTML?
options: To add animations
To provide semantic information to assistive technologies about elements' roles and states
To improve page loading speed
To validate form inputs
explanation: ARIA roles enhance accessibility by conveying the purpose and state of UI elements to screen readers and assistive technologies.

text: What does the 'defer' attribute do on a <script> tag?
options: Prevents the script from running
Downloads the script in parallel and executes it after HTML parsing is complete
Loads the script synchronously
Moves the script to the head section
explanation: The defer attribute tells the browser to download the script asynchronously and execute it only after the HTML document has been fully parsed.

text: What is the difference between the 'async' and 'defer' attributes on a <script> tag?
options: They are identical
async executes as soon as downloaded; defer waits until HTML parsing completes
defer loads faster than async
async blocks HTML parsing; defer does not load the script
explanation: Both download scripts asynchronously, but async executes immediately after download while defer waits until parsing finishes.

text: What is the <template> element used for?
options: To define a reusable page layout
To hold client-side content that is not rendered when the page loads but can be instantiated via JavaScript
To create a CSS template
To define server-side includes
explanation: The <template> element holds HTML content that is not rendered on load but can be cloned and inserted into the DOM via JavaScript.

text: What is the purpose of the 'aria-live' attribute?
options: To animate elements
To announce dynamic content changes to screen readers
To set element visibility
To define live video streams
explanation: The aria-live attribute tells assistive technologies to announce updates to the element's content, useful for dynamic regions.

text: What does the element provide that alone does not?
options: Animation support
The ability to specify multiple source images for different screen sizes and formats
Video playback
3D rendering
explanation: The <picture> element allows art direction and format selection by providing multiple <source> elements for responsive images.

text: What is the purpose of the 'sandbox' attribute on an <iframe>?
options: To add a border to the iframe
To apply security restrictions like disabling scripts and form submissions within the iframe
To make the iframe responsive
To cache the iframe content
explanation: The sandbox attribute applies extra restrictions to iframe content, blocking scripts, forms, and popups unless explicitly allowed.

text: What is the difference between the <strong> and <b> elements?
options: They are identical in every way
<strong> conveys semantic importance; <b> only provides visual boldness without semantic meaning
<b> is semantic; <strong> is not
<strong> is deprecated
explanation: The <strong> element indicates strong importance semantically, while <b> only applies bold styling without conveying meaning.

text: What is the purpose of the <datalist> element?
options: To create a database connection
To provide a list of predefined options for an input element's autocomplete
To define a data table
To store JSON data
explanation: The <datalist> element provides a dropdown list of predefined suggestions for an <input> element.

text: What is the 'content model' concept in HTML5?
options: A CSS layout model
A set of rules defining what content an element can contain and where it can be used
A JavaScript design pattern
A server-side templating system
explanation: Content models define rules for which elements can be nested inside others, categorized as flow, phrasing, interactive, etc.

text: What does CSS stand for?
options: Computer Style Sheets
Creative Style System
Cascading Style Sheets
Colorful Style Syntax
explanation: CSS stands for Cascading Style Sheets, used to style and layout web pages.

text: Which CSS property changes the text color of an element?
options: text-color
font-color
color
foreground
explanation: The color property sets the text color of an element.

text: Which CSS property is used to change the background color?
options: bgcolor
background-color
color-background
bg-color
explanation: The background-color property sets the background color of an element.

text: How do you select an element with the id 'header' in CSS?
options: .header
#header
header
*header
explanation: The # selector targets an element by its id attribute.

text: How do you select all elements with the class 'btn' in CSS?
options: #btn
.btn
btn
*btn
explanation: The . (dot) selector targets all elements with the specified class name.

text: Which CSS property controls the size of text?
options: text-size
font-size
text-style
font-weight
explanation: The font-size property sets the size of the text.

text: Which CSS property adds space inside an element between its content and border?
options: margin
padding
spacing
border-spacing
explanation: Padding creates space between an element's content and its border.

text: Which CSS property adds space outside an element's border?
options: padding
border
margin
gap
explanation: Margin creates space outside an element's border, separating it from other elements.

text: What is the default value of the CSS 'position' property?
options: relative
absolute
fixed
static
explanation: The default value of position is static, meaning the element follows the normal document flow.

text: Which CSS display value hides an element completely?
options: hidden
none
invisible
collapse
explanation: Setting display: none removes the element from the document flow entirely, hiding it and freeing its space.

text: What are the four areas of the CSS box model from inside to outside?
options: Content, border, padding, margin
Content, padding, border, margin
Margin, border, padding, content
Padding, content, margin, border
explanation: The CSS box model layers from innermost to outermost are: content, padding, border, and margin.

text: What does 'display: flex' do to an element?
options: Makes it invisible
Turns it into a flex container, enabling flexbox layout for its children
Makes it float left
Sets it to block display
explanation: Setting display: flex makes the element a flex container, allowing its children to be laid out using flexbox properties.

text: What is CSS specificity?
options: The order in which CSS files are loaded
A system that determines which CSS rule is applied when multiple rules target the same element
The number of CSS properties used
The speed of CSS rendering
explanation: Specificity is a scoring system that resolves conflicts when multiple CSS rules target the same element.

text: Which selector has the highest specificity?
options: Element selector (p)
Class selector (.intro)
ID selector (#header)
Universal selector (*)
explanation: ID selectors have higher specificity than class and element selectors. Only inline styles and !important override them.

text: What does 'position: absolute' do?
options: Positions relative to the viewport
Removes the element from normal flow and positions it relative to its nearest positioned ancestor
Keeps the element in the normal flow
Makes the element sticky
explanation: Absolute positioning removes the element from the normal flow and positions it relative to the nearest ancestor with a non-static position.

text: What does the CSS 'inherit' value do?
options: Resets the property to its default
Makes the property take the computed value from its parent element
Removes the property entirely
Sets the property to auto
explanation: The inherit value forces a property to take the same computed value as its parent element.

text: What is the difference between 'display: inline' and 'display: inline-block'?
options: They are identical
inline-block respects width and height properties; inline does not
inline respects width; inline-block does not
inline-block cannot have padding
explanation: Inline elements ignore width and height, while inline-block elements respect these properties while still flowing inline.

text: Which flexbox property aligns items along the cross axis?
options: justify-content
align-items
flex-direction
flex-wrap
explanation: The align-items property aligns flex items along the cross axis (perpendicular to the main axis).

text: What does 'box-sizing: border-box' do?
options: Adds extra padding
Makes width and height include padding and border in the element's total size
Removes the border
Sets the box shadow
explanation: With border-box, the element's width and height include content, padding, and border, making sizing more predictable.

text: What does the CSS cascade determine?
options: The font size of elements
Which CSS rule wins when multiple rules apply to the same element, based on origin, specificity, and order
The loading order of CSS files
The animation speed
explanation: The cascade algorithm resolves conflicts by considering rule origin, specificity, and source order to determine which styles apply.

text: What is a CSS pseudo-element and how does it differ from a pseudo-class?
options: They are the same thing
Pseudo-elements (::before) style specific parts of an element; pseudo-classes (:hover) style elements based on state
Pseudo-classes style parts of elements; pseudo-elements style states
Neither exists in modern CSS
explanation: Pseudo-elements target sub-parts of an element (like ::before), while pseudo-classes target element states (like :hover).

text: What is the stacking context in CSS?
options: The order of CSS file imports
A three-dimensional conceptualization of HTML elements along the z-axis, determining how overlapping elements are rendered
A flexbox alignment property
The order of CSS selectors
explanation: A stacking context determines the z-axis rendering order of elements. It is created by properties like z-index with position, opacity, or transform.

text: How does the 'em' unit differ from the 'rem' unit in CSS?
options: They are identical
em is relative to the parent element's font-size; rem is relative to the root element's font-size
rem is relative to the parent; em is relative to the root
Neither is a valid CSS unit
explanation: The em unit is relative to the font-size of its parent, while rem is always relative to the root (<html>) element's font-size.

text: What happens when two CSS rules with equal specificity target the same element?
options: Both are ignored
The rule that appears later in the source order wins
The first rule wins
A browser error occurs
explanation: When specificity is equal, the CSS cascade applies the rule that appears last in the source order.

text: What does the CSS 'will-change' property do?
options: Forces an element to change color
Hints to the browser which properties will change, allowing it to optimize rendering in advance
Triggers an animation
Changes the element's display type
explanation: The will-change property informs the browser of expected changes, allowing it to set up optimizations like promoting the element to its own layer.

text: What is the difference between 'visibility: hidden' and 'display: none'?
options: They are identical
visibility: hidden hides the element but it still occupies space; display: none removes it from the layout entirely
display: none preserves space; visibility: hidden removes it
Both remove the element from the DOM
explanation: visibility: hidden makes the element invisible but it still takes up space, while display: none removes it from the layout flow completely.

text: What is margin collapsing in CSS?
options: When margins cause elements to overlap
When vertical margins of adjacent block-level elements combine into a single margin equal to the larger of the two
When margins are set to zero
When margins override padding
explanation: Margin collapsing occurs when the top and bottom margins of adjacent block elements merge into one margin, taking the larger value.

text: What does the CSS 'clamp()' function do?
options: Clips text overflow
Constrains a value between a minimum, preferred, and maximum value
Clamps an animation
Restricts element visibility
explanation: The clamp(min, preferred, max) function sets a value that is clamped between a defined minimum and maximum.

text: What is the purpose of the CSS 'contain' property?
options: To set overflow behavior
To indicate that an element and its contents are independent from the rest of the document, enabling rendering optimizations
To create a container query
To clip element borders
explanation: The contain property lets the browser isolate subtrees for optimization by indicating layout, size, or paint containment.

text: In CSS specificity calculation, what are the three levels from highest to lowest?
options: Classes, IDs, elements
Elements, classes, IDs
IDs, classes/attributes/pseudo-classes, elements/pseudo-elements
Pseudo-elements, pseudo-classes, elements
explanation: CSS specificity is calculated as (ID selectors, class/attribute/pseudo-class selectors, element/pseudo-element selectors).

text: What CSS property is used to create a grid layout?
options: display: block
display: flex
display: grid
display: table
explanation: Setting display: grid on a container enables CSS Grid layout for arranging child elements in rows and columns.

text: What is responsive web design?
options: Design that only works on desktops
Design that adapts to different screen sizes and devices
Design that uses only images
Design that requires no CSS
explanation: Responsive design uses flexible layouts, media queries, and fluid images to adapt the UI to different screen sizes.

text: What is a media query in CSS?
options: A JavaScript function
A rule that applies styles based on conditions like screen width
A way to import images
A method to query databases
explanation: Media queries apply CSS rules conditionally based on device characteristics like viewport width or orientation.

text: How do you define a CSS variable (custom property)?
options: $variable: value
--variable: value
var(variable)
@variable: value
explanation: CSS custom properties are defined with a double-dash prefix (--my-color: blue) and accessed via var(--my-color).

text: Which CSS property creates a smooth transition effect?
options: animation
transform
transition
translate
explanation: The transition property smoothly animates property changes over a specified duration.

text: What does BEM stand for in CSS methodology?
options: Basic Element Model
Block Element Modifier
Browser Enhanced Markup
Base Entity Method
explanation: BEM (Block Element Modifier) is a CSS naming convention for creating reusable and maintainable component-based styles.

text: Which CSS property is used to rotate, scale, or move an element?
options: transition
animation
transform
position
explanation: The transform property applies transformations like rotate(), scale(), and translate() to an element.

text: What is SASS?
options: A JavaScript framework
A CSS preprocessor that adds features like variables and nesting
A web server
A database system
explanation: SASS (Syntactically Awesome Stylesheets) is a CSS preprocessor that extends CSS with variables, nesting, mixins, and more.

text: What does the CSS 'grid-template-columns' property define?
options: The number of grid rows
The column structure of a grid layout
The gap between grid items
The grid alignment
explanation: The grid-template-columns property defines the number and size of columns in a CSS grid container.

text: Which CSS property adds space between grid or flex items?
options: margin
padding
gap
spacing
explanation: The gap property sets the spacing between rows and columns in grid and flexbox layouts.

text: What is the 'fr' unit in CSS Grid?
options: A fixed pixel unit
A fractional unit that represents a portion of the available space in the grid container
A font-relative unit
A frame rate unit
explanation: The fr unit distributes available space proportionally among grid tracks, similar to flex-grow in flexbox.

text: How do you access a CSS custom property in a stylesheet?
options: $(--my-var)
var(--my-var)
@my-var
get(--my-var)
explanation: The var() function retrieves the value of a CSS custom property, e.g., color: var(--primary-color).

text: What is the difference between CSS transitions and CSS animations?
options: They are identical
Transitions require a trigger and go from state A to B; animations can run automatically with multiple keyframes
Animations require a trigger; transitions do not
Transitions support keyframes; animations do not
explanation: Transitions need a trigger (like :hover) and animate between two states, while animations use @keyframes and can run independently with multiple steps.

text: What is the purpose of a CSS preprocessor like LESS?
options: To reduce file size
To extend CSS with programming features like variables, mixins, and functions before compiling to standard CSS
To replace CSS entirely
To add JavaScript to stylesheets
explanation: LESS is a CSS preprocessor that adds features like variables, mixins, and functions, and compiles down to regular CSS.

text: What does 'grid-area' allow you to do?
options: Set the background of a grid
Assign a grid item to a named area or specify its row and column placement
Add gap between items
Define the number of columns
explanation: The grid-area property assigns a grid item to a named template area or defines its placement using row/column start and end lines.

text: What is CSS-in-JS?
options: Writing CSS inside HTML files
An approach where CSS is authored using JavaScript, often scoped to components
A CSS validation tool
A way to minify CSS
explanation: CSS-in-JS is a pattern where styles are written in JavaScript files, enabling scoped styles and dynamic styling based on props.

text: What does the CSS 'min()' function do?
options: Finds the minimum of a dataset
Returns the smallest value from a comma-separated list of expressions
Sets the minimum font size
Minimizes the element
explanation: The min() function picks the smallest computed value from a list of expressions, useful for responsive sizing.

text: In a BEM naming convention, how would you name a modifier for a button that is disabled?
options: button_disabled
button--disabled
button.disabled
button:disabled
explanation: In BEM, modifiers use double dashes: block--modifier, so a disabled button is button--disabled.

text: What is the mobile-first approach in responsive design?
options: Building desktop layout first and scaling down
Designing for mobile screens first and using media queries to add styles for larger screens
Only supporting mobile devices
Using mobile-specific HTML tags
explanation: Mobile-first means writing base styles for mobile and using min-width media queries to progressively enhance for larger screens.

text: What is the purpose of the '@keyframes' rule in CSS?
options: To import external stylesheets
To define the steps of a CSS animation sequence
To create media queries
To declare CSS variables
explanation: The @keyframes rule defines the sequence of styles an element transitions through during a CSS animation.

text: What are CSS container queries?
options: Queries that check the server's container
Rules that apply styles based on the size of a parent container rather than the viewport
A Docker-related CSS feature
Queries for grid containers only
explanation: Container queries allow styles to be applied based on a parent container's size, enabling truly component-level responsive design.

text: What is the CSS 'subgrid' value?
options: A grid inside a flexbox
A value for grid-template-rows/columns that allows a nested grid to inherit its parent grid's track sizing
A smaller version of the grid
A grid layout for mobile only
explanation: Subgrid allows a nested grid item to adopt its parent grid's row or column tracks, maintaining alignment across nested grids.

text: How do CSS logical properties differ from physical properties?
options: They are identical
Logical properties use flow-relative terms (inline-start, block-end) instead of physical directions (left, top), adapting to writing modes
Physical properties are newer than logical properties
Logical properties only work in flexbox
explanation: Logical properties like margin-inline-start adapt to different writing modes and text directions, unlike physical properties like margin-left.

text: What is the purpose of the CSS ':has()' pseudo-class?
options: To check if CSS is supported
To select an element based on whether it contains a specific descendant or matches a condition
To check if an element has attributes
To detect browser type
explanation: The :has() pseudo-class selects elements that contain descendants matching the given selector, acting as a parent selector.

text: What problem does CSS specificity wars refer to?
options: Browser compatibility issues
The escalation of increasingly specific selectors or !important to override styles, leading to unmaintainable code
Conflicts between CSS Grid and Flexbox
Performance issues with animations
explanation: Specificity wars occur when developers keep adding more specific selectors or !important to override each other, making CSS hard to maintain.

text: What is the CSS 'aspect-ratio' property used for?
options: Setting font proportions
Defining a preferred width-to-height ratio for an element's box
Creating circular elements
Adjusting line height
explanation: The aspect-ratio property sets a preferred ratio between width and height, ensuring the element maintains proportions when resized.

text: What is the CSS cascade layer feature (@layer)?
options: A way to create visual layers with z-index
A mechanism to explicitly control the priority order of CSS rules without relying on specificity or source order
A media query for layered devices
A method for lazy-loading CSS
explanation: Cascade layers (@layer) let developers control the ordering of rule sets so that lower layers lose to higher layers regardless of specificity.

text: What is the difference between 'auto-fill' and 'auto-fit' in CSS Grid's repeat() function?
options: They are identical
auto-fill creates as many tracks as fit even if empty; auto-fit collapses empty tracks to zero
auto-fit creates more tracks; auto-fill creates fewer
auto-fill is for rows; auto-fit is for columns
explanation: auto-fill keeps empty tracks at their defined size, while auto-fit collapses empty tracks to zero, allowing content to stretch.

text: How does 'isolation: isolate' affect an element?
options: It hides the element
It creates a new stacking context, preventing the element's content from blending with elements outside it
It removes the element from the DOM
It disables pointer events
explanation: Setting isolation: isolate creates a new stacking context, ensuring that mix-blend-mode on children does not affect elements outside.

text: What is the purpose of the CSS 'scroll-snap' properties?
options: To disable scrolling
To create controlled scroll positions where the viewport snaps to defined points
To animate the scrollbar
To hide overflow content
explanation: Scroll-snap properties (scroll-snap-type, scroll-snap-align) create snapping behavior so the scroll stops at defined positions.

text: Which keyword is used to declare a constant variable in JavaScript?
options: var
let
const
constant
explanation: The const keyword declares a variable whose value cannot be reassigned after initialization.

text: What is the result of typeof null in JavaScript?
options: 'null'
'undefined'
'object'
'boolean'
explanation: Due to a historical bug in JavaScript, typeof null returns 'object' instead of 'null'.

text: Which method is used to select an HTML element by its ID in JavaScript?
options: document.querySelector()
document.getElement()
document.getElementById()
document.findById()
explanation: document.getElementById() returns the DOM element with the specified ID attribute.

text: What does the '===' operator do in JavaScript?
options: Assigns a value
Checks equality without type comparison
Checks equality with type comparison (strict equality)
Checks if a value is truthy
explanation: The strict equality operator (===) checks both value and type, unlike == which performs type coercion.

text: Which data type represents text in JavaScript?
options: Number
Boolean
String
Object
explanation: The String data type is used to represent and manipulate sequences of characters (text).

text: What does console.log() do?
options: Displays an alert box
Writes output to the browser console
Creates a new HTML element
Saves data to a file
explanation: console.log() outputs messages or values to the browser's developer console for debugging.

text: Which loop iterates a specific number of times?
options: while loop
do-while loop
for loop
for-in loop
explanation: The for loop is commonly used when the number of iterations is known, using an initializer, condition, and increment.

text: How do you write a single-line comment in JavaScript?
options: <!-- comment -->
/* comment */
// comment
# comment
explanation: Single-line comments in JavaScript start with // and extend to the end of the line.

text: What is an event in JavaScript?
options: A CSS property
An action or occurrence detected by the browser, like a click or keypress
A type of variable
A server request
explanation: Events are actions like clicks, keypresses, or page loads that JavaScript can detect and respond to.

text: What value does an uninitialized variable have in JavaScript?
options: null
undefined
NaN
explanation: Variables declared but not assigned a value have the default value of undefined.

text: What is hoisting in JavaScript?
options: Moving elements up in the DOM
The behavior where variable and function declarations are moved to the top of their scope during compilation
A sorting algorithm
A CSS positioning technique
explanation: Hoisting moves declarations to the top of their scope before execution, so var and function declarations can be used before their line of code.

text: What is the difference between 'let' and 'var'?
options: They are identical
let is block-scoped and not hoisted to the top of its scope; var is function-scoped and hoisted
var is block-scoped; let is function-scoped
let can only hold strings
explanation: let is block-scoped and enters a 'temporal dead zone' before declaration, while var is function-scoped and hoisted with undefined.

text: What is the difference between null and undefined in JavaScript?
options: They are the same
undefined means a variable has been declared but not assigned; null is an intentional assignment of no value
null is automatic; undefined is intentional
undefined is a number; null is a string
explanation: undefined is the default value of uninitialized variables, while null is explicitly assigned to represent the absence of value.

text: What does the addEventListener() method do?
options: Creates a new DOM element
Attaches an event handler function to an element
Removes an event listener
Triggers an event manually
explanation: addEventListener() attaches a function to an element that is called when the specified event occurs.

text: What is the scope of a variable declared with 'var' inside a function?
options: Block scope
Global scope
Function scope
Module scope
explanation: Variables declared with var are function-scoped, meaning they are accessible throughout the entire function.

text: What is an arrow function in JavaScript?
options: A function that points to another function
A concise syntax for writing functions using '=>'
A function that only returns arrows
A deprecated function type
explanation: Arrow functions provide a shorter syntax (const fn = () => {}) and do not bind their own 'this' context.

text: What is event bubbling?
options: Events that only fire once
The process where an event propagates from the target element up through its ancestors
Events that are cancelled
A way to create custom events
explanation: Event bubbling means that when an event fires on an element, it also fires on each ancestor element up to the document.

text: What does the 'this' keyword refer to in a regular function call?
options: The function itself
The global object (or undefined in strict mode)
The parent function
The DOM element
explanation: In a regular function call, 'this' refers to the global object (window in browsers), or undefined in strict mode.

text: What is the difference between textContent and innerHTML?
options: They are identical
textContent sets or gets plain text; innerHTML parses and sets HTML markup
innerHTML gets plain text; textContent gets HTML
Neither modifies the DOM
explanation: textContent handles raw text without parsing HTML, while innerHTML interprets the string as HTML markup.

text: What does the Array.push() method do?
options: Removes the first element
Adds one or more elements to the end of an array and returns the new length
Sorts the array
Reverses the array
explanation: The push() method appends elements to the end of an array and returns the updated length.

text: What is a temporal dead zone (TDZ) in JavaScript?
options: A zone where time-related functions are disabled
The period between entering a scope and the variable declaration where let/const variables cannot be accessed
A zone where setTimeout does not work
A garbage collection phase
explanation: The TDZ is the period from when a scope is entered until the let/const declaration is reached; accessing the variable during this time throws a ReferenceError.

text: What is the output of: console.log(0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3)?
options: true
false
undefined
NaN
explanation: Due to IEEE 754 floating-point precision, 0.1 + 0.2 results in 0.30000000000000004, not exactly 0.3.

text: What is event delegation?
options: Assigning events to every child element individually
Attaching a single event listener to a parent to handle events from its descendants using event bubbling
Preventing event propagation
Delegating events to the server
explanation: Event delegation uses bubbling to handle events on a parent element, improving performance when many child elements need the same handler.

text: What does 'use strict' do in JavaScript?
options: Makes the code run faster
Enables strict mode which catches common mistakes and prevents unsafe actions
Disables all warnings
Activates TypeScript mode
explanation: Strict mode catches silent errors, prevents undeclared variables, disables 'this' coercion, and forbids certain syntax.

text: What is the difference between function declarations and function expressions?
options: They are identical
Declarations are hoisted entirely; expressions are not hoisted (only the variable is hoisted if using var)
Expressions are hoisted; declarations are not
Only declarations can take arguments
explanation: Function declarations are fully hoisted, so they can be called before their definition. Function expressions are not hoisted.

text: What is type coercion in JavaScript?
options: Explicitly casting types
The automatic conversion of values from one type to another during operations
A TypeScript feature
A way to define custom types
explanation: Type coercion is JavaScript's automatic conversion of values between types, such as converting a string to a number during comparison.

text: What does the DOMContentLoaded event signify?
options: All images and styles are loaded
The initial HTML document has been fully loaded and parsed, without waiting for external resources
The page is fully rendered
The browser tab is active
explanation: DOMContentLoaded fires when the HTML is fully parsed and the DOM is ready, without waiting for stylesheets, images, or subframes.

text: What is the difference between a shallow copy and a deep copy of an object?
options: They are the same
A shallow copy duplicates top-level properties but shares nested references; a deep copy duplicates all levels
A deep copy only copies top-level properties
Shallow copies are slower
explanation: Shallow copies clone top-level values but nested objects remain as references. Deep copies recursively duplicate all nested structures.

text: What does Object.freeze() do?
options: Deletes all properties
Prevents adding, removing, or modifying properties of an object (makes it immutable at the top level)
Converts the object to a string
Creates a deep copy
explanation: Object.freeze() makes an object immutable at the top level; properties cannot be added, removed, or changed (shallow freeze only).

text: What is the purpose of the Symbol primitive type?
options: To create decorative characters
To create unique, immutable identifiers for object properties, avoiding name collisions
To define mathematical symbols
To encrypt strings
explanation: Symbols create guaranteed unique identifiers, useful for adding properties to objects without risking name collisions.

text: What is a closure in JavaScript?
options: A way to close the browser
A function that has access to its outer function's variables even after the outer function has returned
A method to end a loop
A type of error handling
explanation: A closure is formed when an inner function retains access to the variables of its enclosing function's scope.

text: What does a Promise represent in JavaScript?
options: A guaranteed return value
A value that may be available now, in the future, or never
A type of loop
A CSS animation
explanation: A Promise represents an asynchronous operation that will eventually resolve with a value or reject with a reason.

text: What does the 'async' keyword do before a function?
options: Makes the function run faster
Makes the function return a Promise automatically
Makes the function synchronous
Disables the function
explanation: The async keyword makes a function return a Promise and allows the use of await inside it.

text: What is the fetch API used for?
options: Fetching CSS files
Making HTTP requests from JavaScript
Fetching DOM elements
Retrieving cookies
explanation: The fetch API provides a modern interface for making HTTP requests and handling responses using Promises.

text: What is destructuring in JavaScript?
options: Deleting an object
A syntax for extracting values from arrays or properties from objects into distinct variables
Breaking a function
Removing event listeners
explanation: Destructuring allows you to unpack values from arrays or properties from objects into separate variables concisely.

text: What are template literals in JavaScript?
options: HTML templates
Strings delimited by backticks that support embedded expressions and multiline content
CSS templates
JSON templates
explanation: Template literals use backticks (`) and allow embedded expressions via ${} and multiline strings.

text: What does the spread operator (...) do?
options: Concatenates strings only
Expands an iterable into individual elements
Creates a new function
Defines a class
explanation: The spread operator expands arrays or objects into individual elements, useful for copying and merging.

text: What are the three states of a Promise?
options: Start, middle, end
Pending, fulfilled, rejected
Open, closed, error
Active, inactive, done
explanation: A Promise can be pending (initial state), fulfilled (resolved successfully), or rejected (failed).

text: What does 'await' do in an async function?
options: Skips the next line
Pauses execution until the Promise resolves and returns its result
Creates a new Promise
Throws an error
explanation: The await keyword pauses the async function execution until the Promise settles, then returns the resolved value.

text: What is an ES6 module?
options: A CSS module
A JavaScript file that exports and imports code using import/export statements
A Node.js package
A HTML component
explanation: ES6 modules use import and export statements to share code between JavaScript files in a standardized way.

text: What is the event loop in JavaScript?
options: A for loop that handles events
A mechanism that executes code, collects and processes events, and executes queued sub-tasks from the callback queue
A loop that iterates over DOM events
A recursive function
explanation: The event loop continuously checks the call stack and pushes callbacks from the task queue when the stack is empty, enabling async behavior.

text: What is prototypal inheritance?
options: Inheritance using the 'class' keyword only
A mechanism where objects inherit properties and methods from a prototype object through the prototype chain
Copying all properties manually
A CSS inheritance model
explanation: JavaScript uses prototypal inheritance where objects can inherit from other objects through the prototype chain.

text: What is Promise.all() used for?
options: To run promises sequentially
To wait for all promises in an array to resolve, or reject if any one rejects
To cancel all promises
To create a single promise
explanation: Promise.all() takes an array of promises and resolves when all succeed, or rejects immediately if any promise fails.

text: What is the difference between map() and forEach()?
options: They are identical
map() returns a new array with transformed values; forEach() executes a function but returns undefined
forEach() returns a new array; map() does not
map() only works with objects
explanation: map() creates and returns a new array of transformed elements, while forEach() simply iterates without returning anything.

text: What is a generator function in JavaScript?
options: A function that generates random numbers
A function declared with function* that can pause and resume execution using yield
A function that creates HTML elements
A function that generates CSS
explanation: Generator functions (function*) can pause execution with yield and resume later, producing a sequence of values lazily.

text: What is the purpose of the 'rest' parameter syntax (...args)?
options: To pause function execution
To collect all remaining arguments into an array
To spread an array
To destructure an object
explanation: Rest parameters collect all remaining arguments passed to a function into an array, allowing variadic functions.

text: What does Object.keys() return?
options: An object's values
An array of the object's own enumerable property names
The object's prototype
A boolean
explanation: Object.keys() returns an array containing the names of all own enumerable properties of the object.

text: What is optional chaining (?.) in JavaScript?
options: A way to create optional parameters
An operator that accesses nested properties safely, returning undefined if a reference is null or undefined
A ternary operator shortcut
A loop control statement
explanation: Optional chaining (?.) short-circuits and returns undefined if the left side is null or undefined, preventing runtime errors.

text: What is the nullish coalescing operator (??) ?
options: A comparison operator
An operator that returns the right operand when the left is null or undefined
A logical AND operator
A type checking operator
explanation: The ?? operator returns the right-hand operand only when the left-hand operand is null or undefined, unlike || which also catches falsy values.

text: What is the difference between call() and apply()?
options: They are identical
call() accepts arguments individually; apply() accepts them as an array
apply() accepts arguments individually; call() uses an array
Neither changes the 'this' context
explanation: Both invoke a function with a specified 'this', but call() takes arguments separately while apply() takes them as an array.

text: What is the difference between the microtask queue and the macrotask queue?
options: They are identical
Microtasks (Promises) run before macrotasks (setTimeout) after the current task completes
Macrotasks run before microtasks
There is only one queue in JavaScript
explanation: Microtasks (Promise callbacks) are processed before macrotasks (setTimeout, setInterval) after each task completes.

text: What is a WeakMap and how does it differ from a Map?
options: WeakMap is slower
WeakMap keys must be objects and are weakly referenced, allowing garbage collection when no other reference exists
WeakMap supports string keys; Map does not
They are identical
explanation: WeakMap only accepts objects as keys and holds weak references, meaning entries can be garbage collected when the key object has no other references.

text: What is a Proxy object in JavaScript?
options: A cached copy of an object
An object that wraps another and intercepts fundamental operations like property access and assignment
A server-side object
A polyfill for older browsers
explanation: A Proxy wraps an object and uses handler traps to intercept and customize operations like get, set, and delete.

text: What does Promise.allSettled() do differently from Promise.all()?
options: They are identical
allSettled() waits for all promises to settle (fulfilled or rejected) without short-circuiting on rejection
allSettled() only resolves on rejection
allSettled() runs promises sequentially
explanation: Promise.allSettled() waits for all promises to complete regardless of outcome, returning each result's status and value/reason.

text: What is tail call optimization?
options: Optimizing the last element of an array
A compiler optimization where a function call in the tail position reuses the current stack frame, preventing stack overflow
Removing the last line of code
Optimizing CSS animations
explanation: Tail call optimization reuses the current stack frame for function calls in the tail position, enabling efficient recursion without stack overflow.

text: What is the Iterator protocol in JavaScript?
options: A protocol for HTTP iteration
A convention that defines how objects produce a sequence of values using a next() method that returns {value, done}
A loop syntax
A way to iterate over CSS properties
explanation: The Iterator protocol requires an object with a next() method that returns objects with 'value' and 'done' properties.

text: What is the purpose of Reflect in JavaScript?
options: To create mirrors of objects
To provide methods for interceptable JavaScript operations, complementing Proxy traps
To reflect CSS styles
To debug code
explanation: Reflect provides static methods for the same operations that Proxy traps intercept, making it easier to forward default behavior.

text: What is the difference between dynamic import() and static import?
options: They are the same
Static imports are evaluated at parse time; dynamic import() loads modules at runtime and returns a Promise
Dynamic imports are faster
Static imports happen at runtime
explanation: Static imports are hoisted and resolved at parse time, while dynamic import() loads modules asynchronously at runtime, enabling code splitting.

text: What is a memory leak in JavaScript and how can closures cause one?
options: Memory leaks cannot happen in JavaScript
A closure can prevent garbage collection by maintaining references to large objects in its scope that are no longer needed
Closures always cause memory leaks
Memory leaks only occur in CSS
explanation: If a closure retains references to objects from its outer scope that are no longer needed, those objects cannot be garbage collected, causing a memory leak.

text: What is the structured clone algorithm?
options: A CSS cloning method
An algorithm used by structuredClone() to create deep copies of objects, supporting circular references and complex types
A way to clone DOM elements
A git cloning strategy
explanation: The structured clone algorithm creates deep copies of values including circular references, Maps, Sets, and Dates, unlike JSON.parse(JSON.stringify()).

text: What is a component in frontend frameworks?
options: A database table
A reusable, self-contained piece of UI with its own logic and rendering
A CSS file
A server endpoint
explanation: Components are independent, reusable building blocks that encapsulate HTML, CSS, and JavaScript for a part of the UI.

text: What does SPA stand for?
options: Simple Page Application
Single Page Application
Static Page Architecture
Server Page Application
explanation: SPA (Single Page Application) loads a single HTML page and dynamically updates content without full page reloads.

text: Which of the following is a popular JavaScript frontend framework?
options: Django
Flask
React
Laravel
explanation: React is a popular JavaScript library for building user interfaces. Django, Flask, and Laravel are backend frameworks.

text: What is state in a frontend component?
options: The component's CSS styles
Data that the component manages and that can change over time, triggering re-renders
The component's HTML structure
The server response
explanation: State is mutable data managed within a component that, when changed, causes the component to re-render.

text: What is the virtual DOM?
options: A copy of the server
A lightweight in-memory representation of the real DOM used to optimize UI updates
A CSS rendering engine
A type of database
explanation: The virtual DOM is an in-memory representation of the real DOM that frameworks use to efficiently calculate and apply minimal updates.

text: What are props in React?
options: CSS properties
Data passed from a parent component to a child component
Server-side variables
HTML attributes only
explanation: Props (short for properties) are read-only data passed from parent to child components to configure them.

text: What is client-side routing?
options: Routing handled by the server
Navigation between views handled by JavaScript in the browser without full page reloads
DNS routing
Database routing
explanation: Client-side routing uses JavaScript to update the URL and render different views without requesting a new page from the server.

text: What is JSX?
options: A new programming language
A syntax extension for JavaScript that allows writing HTML-like code in React
A CSS framework
A testing library
explanation: JSX is a syntax extension used in React that lets you write HTML-like markup inside JavaScript code.

text: What is the difference between SPA and MPA?
options: There is no difference
SPA loads one page and updates dynamically; MPA loads a new page for each route
MPA is faster than SPA always
SPA cannot use JavaScript
explanation: SPAs load once and update content dynamically, while MPAs request and load a new HTML page for each navigation.

text: What is Angular?
options: A CSS library
A TypeScript-based frontend framework developed by Google for building web applications
A database tool
A backend framework
explanation: Angular is a comprehensive frontend framework by Google that uses TypeScript and provides tools for building complex web apps.

text: What is the purpose of React hooks?
options: To hook into the DOM directly
To allow functional components to use state and lifecycle features without writing classes
To create HTTP hooks
To hook into the server
explanation: React hooks (like useState, useEffect) let functional components manage state and side effects without class syntax.

text: What does the useEffect hook do in React?
options: Manages component state
Performs side effects like data fetching, subscriptions, or DOM manipulation after render
Creates a new component
Handles routing
explanation: useEffect runs side effects after the component renders, replacing lifecycle methods like componentDidMount and componentDidUpdate.

text: What is two-way data binding?
options: Data flowing only from parent to child
A synchronization where changes in the UI update the model and vice versa automatically
Binding data to two databases
A server-side concept
explanation: Two-way data binding automatically synchronizes data between the model and the view, common in Angular and Vue with v-model.

text: What is Vue.js's reactivity system based on?
options: Manual DOM updates
JavaScript Proxies (Vue 3) that track dependencies and trigger updates when reactive data changes
Polling for changes
Web Workers
