text: What is flaky test and why is it problematic?
options: A test that always passes
A test that sometimes passes and sometimes fails without any code changes, eroding trust in the test suite and wasting developer time investigating false failures
A test that runs very slowly
A test that has no assertions
explanation: Flaky tests produce inconsistent results due to timing issues, shared state, or external dependencies, undermining confidence in the test suite and causing wasted investigation time.

text: What is the Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern in testing?
options: A pattern for naming test files
A test structure pattern where you set up test data (Arrange), execute the code under test (Act), and verify the results (Assert)
A pattern for organizing test folders
A pattern for test automation tools
explanation: AAA organizes each test into three clear phases: Arrange (set up preconditions), Act (execute the tested behavior), and Assert (verify the expected outcome).

text: What is the Istanbul code coverage tool?
options: A JavaScript minifier
A code coverage instrumentation library that tracks which lines, branches, functions, and statements are executed during tests
A CSS testing tool
A deployment platform
explanation: Istanbul (used via nyc or built into Jest) instruments JavaScript code to track execution coverage across statements, branches, functions, and lines during test runs.

text: What does CI/CD stand for?
options: Code Integration / Code Delivery
Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment (or Delivery)
Central Interface / Central Database
Client Interface / Client Data
explanation: CI/CD stands for Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment/Delivery, automating the build, test, and deployment process for faster and more reliable releases.

text: What is Docker?
options: A programming language
A platform for building, shipping, and running applications in lightweight containers
A JavaScript testing framework
A database management system
explanation: Docker is a containerization platform that packages applications and their dependencies into lightweight, portable containers that run consistently across environments.

text: What is Vercel primarily used for?
options: Database hosting
Deploying and hosting frontend frameworks and serverless functions, optimized for Next.js
Email management
Video streaming
explanation: Vercel is a cloud platform optimized for deploying frontend frameworks (especially Next.js) with features like serverless functions, edge computing, and automatic HTTPS.

text: What are environment variables used for in deployment?
options: Styling the application
Storing configuration values like API keys and database URLs that differ between environments without hardcoding them
Compressing application files
Managing user sessions
explanation: Environment variables store configuration data (API keys, database URLs, secrets) externally, allowing the same code to run in different environments with different settings.

text: What is an SSL certificate?
options: A JavaScript library for security
A digital certificate that authenticates a website's identity and enables HTTPS encrypted connections
A database encryption tool
A CSS validation certificate
explanation: An SSL/TLS certificate verifies a website's identity and enables encrypted HTTPS connections, displayed as a padlock icon in the browser's address bar.

text: What is Netlify?
options: A database service
A cloud platform for deploying static sites and serverless functions with continuous deployment from Git
A CSS framework
A JavaScript runtime
explanation: Netlify is a web hosting platform that simplifies deploying static sites and serverless functions with features like continuous deployment from Git, form handling, and CDN.

text: What is the purpose of a domain name?
options: To store website files
To provide a human-readable address that maps to a server's IP address for accessing a website
To encrypt website data
To compile server-side code
explanation: A domain name is a human-readable address (like example.com) that maps to a server's IP address via DNS, making websites easy to access and remember.

text: What is a Dockerfile?
options: A log file for Docker containers
A text file containing instructions for building a Docker image, specifying the base image, dependencies, and commands
A Docker configuration for networking
A Docker database backup file
explanation: A Dockerfile is a script with instructions that Docker uses to build an image, including the base OS, installed packages, copied files, and startup commands.

text: What does DNS stand for?
options: Dynamic Network Service
Domain Name System
Data Network Storage
Distributed Node Server
explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses, functioning as the internet's phone book.

text: What is the purpose of a build step in web deployment?
options: To design the UI mockups
To transform source code (compiling TypeScript, bundling, minifying) into optimized production-ready files
To purchase a domain name
To set up the server hardware
explanation: The build step transforms development source code into optimized production assets through compilation, bundling, minification, and other transformations.

text: What is the difference between a Docker image and a Docker container?
options: They are the same thing
An image is a read-only template with the application and dependencies; a container is a running instance of that image
A container is a template; an image is a running instance
Images are for development; containers are for production
explanation: A Docker image is an immutable blueprint containing the application and dependencies, while a container is a running, writable instance created from that image.

text: What is a CI/CD pipeline?
options: A physical network connection between servers
An automated sequence of steps that builds, tests, and deploys code whenever changes are committed to a repository
A database replication mechanism
A CDN routing configuration
explanation: A CI/CD pipeline automates the workflow from code commit to deployment, including building, testing, and releasing, ensuring consistent and reliable software delivery.

text: What is the purpose of Docker Compose?
options: To build Docker images faster
To define and run multi-container Docker applications using a YAML configuration file
To monitor Docker container logs
To compress Docker images
explanation: Docker Compose uses a docker-compose.yml file to define multi-container applications, allowing you to start, stop, and manage all services with a single command.

text: What is a staging environment?
options: The developer's local machine
A pre-production environment that mirrors the production setup, used for final testing before deployment
The live production server
A code repository branch
explanation: A staging environment closely replicates production, allowing teams to test changes in realistic conditions before deploying to the live site.

text: What is GitHub Actions?
options: A GitHub feature for managing issues
A CI/CD platform built into GitHub that automates workflows like building, testing, and deploying code based on repository events
A GitHub code editor
A GitHub project management tool
explanation: GitHub Actions provides CI/CD automation directly in GitHub, triggering customizable workflows on events like pushes, pull requests, or schedules.

text: What is a rolling deployment?
options: Deploying to all servers simultaneously
Gradually replacing instances of the old version with the new version, ensuring some instances are always available
Rolling back to a previous version
Deploying once a week on a schedule
explanation: Rolling deployment gradually updates servers one at a time (or in small batches), maintaining availability by keeping some instances running the old version during the update.

text: What is Let's Encrypt?
options: A JavaScript encryption library
A free, automated certificate authority that provides SSL/TLS certificates for enabling HTTPS on websites
A password management tool
A database encryption service
explanation: Let's Encrypt is a free, automated, open certificate authority that provides SSL/TLS certificates, making HTTPS accessible to all website owners.

text: What is the purpose of health checks in deployment?
options: Checking the health of developers
Automated checks that verify an application is running correctly by probing specific endpoints, used for monitoring and load balancing
Checking the physical health of servers
Validating HTML markup
explanation: Health checks probe application endpoints to verify the service is running and healthy, used by load balancers and orchestrators to route traffic and restart failed instances.

text: What is an A record in DNS?
options: An authentication record
An address record that maps a domain name to an IPv4 address
An analytics record
An access control record
explanation: An A (Address) record is a DNS record type that maps a domain name to its corresponding IPv4 address, directing traffic to the correct server.

text: What is the .env file used for?
options: Defining CSS environment variables
Storing environment-specific configuration variables locally that should not be committed to version control
Configuring the build tool
Defining database schemas
explanation: The .env file stores environment variables (API keys, secrets, database URLs) locally, and should be added to .gitignore to prevent sensitive data from being committed.

text: What is a blue-green deployment strategy?
options: Deploying to different colored server racks
Maintaining two identical production environments (blue and green), deploying to the inactive one and switching traffic once verified
A color-coding system for server logs
Deploying frontend and backend separately
explanation: Blue-green deployment maintains two identical environments; the new version deploys to the idle one, and traffic switches over instantly, allowing immediate rollback by switching back.

text: What is a canary deployment?
options: Deploying only during morning hours
Gradually rolling out a new version to a small subset of users first, monitoring for issues before full deployment
Deploying to a test environment only
A deployment that cannot be rolled back
explanation: Canary deployment releases the new version to a small percentage of users first, monitoring for errors before gradually increasing the rollout to all users.

text: What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?
options: Writing application code on server hardware
Managing and provisioning infrastructure through machine-readable definition files rather than manual processes, using tools like Terraform or CloudFormation
Coding directly on production servers
Using infrastructure diagrams as documentation
explanation: IaC manages infrastructure (servers, networks, databases) through declarative code files, enabling version control, reproducibility, and automation of infrastructure changes.

text: What is Kubernetes used for?
options: A JavaScript testing framework
An open-source container orchestration platform that automates deploying, scaling, and managing containerized applications
A CSS preprocessor
A database management system
explanation: Kubernetes (K8s) automates the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications, handling load balancing, self-healing, and rolling updates across clusters.

text: What is a multi-stage Docker build?
options: Building multiple Docker containers at once
Using multiple FROM statements in a Dockerfile to create intermediate build stages, producing smaller final images by excluding build tools and dependencies
Running Docker on multiple servers
Building Docker images for multiple architectures
explanation: Multi-stage builds use multiple FROM statements to separate build and runtime stages, copying only necessary artifacts to the final image and significantly reducing its size.

text: What is the purpose of a reverse proxy like Nginx in production deployments?
options: To write server-side JavaScript
To handle SSL termination, load balancing, static file serving, and request routing in front of application servers
To compile TypeScript
To manage database migrations
explanation: Nginx as a reverse proxy handles SSL termination, distributes traffic across application servers, serves static files efficiently, and provides caching and compression.

text: What is a CNAME record in DNS and when would you use it?
options: A certificate name record for SSL
A canonical name record that creates an alias pointing one domain name to another, commonly used for subdomains or CDN routing
A content name record for caching
A connection name record for load balancing
explanation: A CNAME record creates an alias from one domain to another (e.g., www.example.com to example.com), commonly used for subdomains and pointing to CDN or hosting provider domains.

text: What is the 12-Factor App methodology?
options: A design pattern for 12-page websites
A methodology of twelve best practices for building scalable, maintainable SaaS applications, covering codebase, dependencies, config, and more
A security checklist with 12 items
A 12-step deployment process
explanation: The 12-Factor App defines best practices (like storing config in environment, treating logs as streams, and keeping dev/prod parity) for building portable, scalable cloud applications.

text: What is the purpose of container orchestration?
options: To write code inside containers
To automate the deployment, management, scaling, and networking of containerized applications across clusters of hosts
To compress Docker images
To create Dockerfiles
explanation: Container orchestration automates managing containers at scale, handling scheduling, scaling, load balancing, service discovery, and self-healing across multiple hosts.

text: What is GitOps?
options: Using Git for version control
A deployment practice where Git repositories serve as the single source of truth for infrastructure and application configurations, with automated reconciliation
A Git branching strategy
A Git code review process
explanation: GitOps uses Git as the single source of truth for declarative infrastructure and applications, with automated agents continuously reconciling the live state to match the desired state in Git.

text: What does REST stand for?
options: Remote Execution Service Technology
Representational State Transfer
Reliable Encrypted Socket Transfer
Rapid Event Stream Technology
explanation: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing networked applications using standard HTTP methods and stateless communication.

text: What does API stand for?
options: Application Programming Interface
Automated Processing Integration
Advanced Protocol Interchange
Application Protocol Identifier
explanation: API (Application Programming Interface) is a set of rules and protocols that allows different software applications to communicate with each other.

text: Which HTTP method is used to retrieve data from a REST API?
options: POST
DELETE
PUT
GET
explanation: The GET method is used to request data from a specified resource without modifying it, making it the standard method for retrieving information from REST APIs.

text: What is JSON used for in web APIs?
options: Styling web pages
A lightweight data interchange format commonly used to transmit data between a server and client
Creating database schemas
Compiling JavaScript code
explanation: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight, human-readable data format used to exchange data between servers and clients in web APIs.

text: What is GraphQL?
options: A graph database
A query language for APIs that allows clients to request exactly the data they need
A charting library
A CSS framework
explanation: GraphQL is a query language and runtime for APIs developed by Meta that lets clients specify the exact data they need, avoiding over-fetching and under-fetching.

text: What HTTP status code indicates a successful request?
explanation: HTTP status code 200 (OK) indicates that the request was successful and the server has returned the requested resource.

text: What is WebSocket?
options: A type of network cable
A protocol that provides full-duplex, persistent communication between client and server over a single connection
A JavaScript file format
A type of web server
explanation: WebSocket provides a persistent, bidirectional communication channel between client and server, ideal for real-time applications like chat, gaming, and live updates.

text: What HTTP status code means 'Not Found'?
explanation: HTTP 404 (Not Found) indicates that the server cannot find the requested resource, commonly seen when a URL does not match any endpoint.

text: What is the purpose of the PUT HTTP method?
options: To delete a resource
To update or replace an existing resource with the provided data
To search for resources
To redirect to another URL
explanation: The PUT method replaces the entire target resource with the request payload, commonly used to update existing records in REST APIs.

text: What is an API endpoint?
options: The physical location of a server
A specific URL where an API can be accessed to perform a particular operation
The end of an API's lifecycle
An API error message
explanation: An API endpoint is a specific URL path (like /api/users) that represents a particular resource or action that the API exposes for client interaction.

text: What is the difference between PUT and PATCH HTTP methods?
options: They are identical methods
PUT replaces the entire resource; PATCH applies partial modifications to a resource
PATCH replaces the entire resource; PUT applies partial changes
PUT is for creating; PATCH is for deleting
explanation: PUT replaces the entire resource with the provided data, while PATCH applies partial updates, modifying only the specified fields of the resource.

text: What is API versioning and why is it important?
options: Tracking which developers worked on the API
Maintaining different versions of an API to allow backward-compatible changes and gradual migration for consumers
Version controlling the API source code
Numbering API error codes
explanation: API versioning (via URL paths like /v1/, headers, or query params) allows introducing breaking changes in new versions while existing consumers continue using older versions.

text: What is rate limiting in the context of APIs?
options: Limiting the speed of API response times
Restricting the number of API requests a client can make within a given time window to prevent abuse and ensure fair usage
Limiting the size of API payloads
Limiting the number of API endpoints
explanation: Rate limiting controls how many requests a client can make per time period, protecting APIs from abuse, DoS attacks, and ensuring fair resource distribution.

text: What is OpenAPI (formerly Swagger)?
options: A JavaScript framework for building APIs
A specification for describing, producing, consuming, and visualizing RESTful APIs through a standard, language-agnostic interface definition
A database for storing API data
A deployment platform for APIs
explanation: OpenAPI is a specification standard for describing RESTful APIs, enabling auto-generation of documentation, client SDKs, and server stubs from a single definition file.

text: What is a webhook?
options: A tool for monitoring web traffic
An HTTP callback that sends automated notifications to a specified URL when a particular event occurs
A web scraping technique
A type of web server
explanation: A webhook is a user-defined HTTP callback triggered by specific events, allowing one application to automatically notify another when something happens (like a payment or push).

text: What is the main advantage of GraphQL over REST?
options: GraphQL is always faster than REST
GraphQL allows clients to request exactly the data they need in a single query, avoiding over-fetching and under-fetching
GraphQL does not require a server
GraphQL uses less bandwidth in all cases
explanation: GraphQL lets clients specify precisely which fields they need, eliminating over-fetching (getting too much data) and under-fetching (needing multiple requests) common in REST.

text: What is an API gateway?
options: A physical device connecting networks
A server that acts as a single entry point for API requests, handling routing, authentication, rate limiting, and load balancing
A database for API keys
A JavaScript library for making API calls
explanation: An API gateway sits between clients and backend services, managing cross-cutting concerns like authentication, rate limiting, request routing, and response aggregation.

text: What is CORS preflight request?
options: The first request made when a website loads
An OPTIONS request the browser sends before the actual cross-origin request to check if the server allows the intended request method and headers
A request to test server performance
A request to cache API responses
explanation: A preflight request is an automatic OPTIONS request sent by browsers before cross-origin requests with certain methods or headers, checking the server's CORS policy.

text: What is pagination in APIs?
options: Creating web pages for each API endpoint
Dividing large data sets into smaller pages of results, returning a subset per request with navigation metadata
Adding page numbers to API documentation
Caching API responses by page
explanation: API pagination breaks large result sets into manageable pages (using offset/limit or cursor-based approaches), improving performance and reducing payload sizes.

text: What is an idempotent HTTP method?
options: A method that can only be called once
A method where making the same request multiple times produces the same result as making it once, such as GET, PUT, and DELETE
A method that always returns an error
A method that encrypts data automatically
explanation: Idempotent methods (GET, PUT, DELETE) produce the same server state regardless of how many times they are called, unlike POST which may create duplicate resources.

text: What is HATEOAS in RESTful API design?
options: A hashing algorithm for API security
Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State — a REST constraint where responses include links to related actions and resources
A caching strategy for API responses
An HTTP header for API versioning
explanation: HATEOAS is a REST constraint where API responses include hypermedia links that guide clients on available actions, making the API self-discoverable without prior documentation knowledge.

text: What is the N+1 problem in GraphQL?
options: A limit on the number of GraphQL queries
When resolving nested fields triggers separate database queries for each parent item, leading to N+1 total queries; solved by DataLoader batching
A restriction on GraphQL schema size
A versioning limitation in GraphQL
explanation: In GraphQL, nested field resolution can trigger individual queries per item; DataLoader solves this by batching and deduplicating data loading within a single request cycle.

text: What is the difference between cursor-based and offset-based pagination?
options: They are identical approaches
Offset-based uses skip/limit which becomes slow for large datasets; cursor-based uses an opaque pointer to the last item, providing consistent performance
Cursor-based is always slower
Offset-based is only for NoSQL databases
explanation: Offset pagination skips rows (slow for large offsets), while cursor-based pagination uses a pointer to the last retrieved item for consistent O(1) performance regardless of dataset position.

text: What is API throttling and how does it differ from rate limiting?
options: They are exactly the same concept
Rate limiting rejects requests exceeding the limit; throttling slows down or queues requests instead of rejecting them outright
Throttling is faster than rate limiting
Rate limiting only applies to GET requests
explanation: Rate limiting hard-blocks requests exceeding the limit (returning 429), while throttling may slow down, queue, or degrade requests instead of outright rejection.

text: What is gRPC and how does it compare to REST?
options: A JavaScript framework similar to React
A high-performance RPC framework using HTTP/2 and Protocol Buffers that offers faster serialization, streaming, and strong typing compared to JSON-based REST
A database query protocol
A CSS grid replacement
explanation: gRPC uses HTTP/2 for multiplexed connections and Protocol Buffers for efficient binary serialization, offering better performance than REST/JSON for inter-service communication.

text: What is the purpose of ETag headers in API caching?
options: To tag API endpoints for monitoring
To provide a unique identifier for a specific version of a resource, enabling conditional requests that avoid transferring unchanged data
To encrypt API responses
To track API usage analytics
explanation: ETags act as fingerprints for resource versions; clients send the ETag in subsequent requests, and the server returns 304 Not Modified if the resource hasn't changed, saving bandwidth.

text: What is the Backend for Frontend (BFF) pattern?
options: A frontend framework that includes a backend
Creating separate backend services tailored to the specific needs of each frontend (web, mobile, etc.) rather than one general-purpose API
A pattern for backend-only applications
A database design pattern
explanation: BFF creates dedicated backend services for each frontend type, optimizing API responses and aggregation logic for the specific needs of web, mobile, or other clients.

text: What is API schema validation and why is it important?
options: Validating the CSS of API documentation pages
Verifying that API request and response payloads conform to a defined schema, preventing malformed data from causing errors or security issues
Checking if the API server is running
Validating API endpoint URLs
explanation: Schema validation ensures API payloads match expected structures (types, required fields, formats), preventing invalid data from entering the system and catching integration issues early.

text: What is the purpose of GraphQL subscriptions?
options: Subscribing to a GraphQL hosting plan
Enabling real-time data updates by establishing a persistent connection (typically WebSocket) that pushes data to clients when specified events occur
Subscribing to API documentation updates
Purchasing API access tokens
explanation: GraphQL subscriptions enable real-time communication by maintaining a persistent connection where the server pushes updates to subscribed clients when relevant data changes.

text: What is content negotiation in REST APIs?
options: Negotiating content licensing agreements
A mechanism where client and server agree on the response format (JSON, XML, etc.) through Accept and Content-Type headers
Compressing API response content
Filtering API results by content type
explanation: Content negotiation uses HTTP headers (Accept, Content-Type) to let clients request and servers provide responses in preferred formats (JSON, XML, HTML, etc.).

text: What is a Progressive Web App (PWA)?
options: A native mobile application
A web application that uses modern web technologies to provide an app-like experience, including offline support and installability
A desktop application built with Java
A browser extension
explanation: A PWA is a web application that leverages service workers, manifests, and modern APIs to deliver reliable, fast, and installable experiences similar to native apps.

text: What is a service worker?
options: A server-side process that handles requests
A JavaScript file that runs in the background, separate from the web page, enabling features like offline caching and push notifications
A customer support chatbot
A CSS rendering engine
explanation: A service worker is a browser-based script that runs in the background, intercepting network requests and enabling offline functionality, caching, and push notifications.

text: What does SSG stand for in web development?
options: Server-Side Generation
Static Site Generation
Secure Socket Gateway
Simple Style Guide
explanation: SSG (Static Site Generation) pre-builds HTML pages at build time rather than on each request, resulting in fast page loads and easy CDN deployment.

text: What is JAMstack?
options: A JavaScript animation library
A web architecture based on JavaScript, APIs, and Markup that decouples the frontend from backend services
A Java application management stack
A JSON API management system
explanation: JAMstack is a modern web architecture using pre-rendered JavaScript, reusable APIs, and static Markup, delivering fast, secure, and scalable websites.

text: What is a headless CMS?
options: A CMS without any user interface
A content management system that provides content through APIs without a coupled frontend, allowing any frontend to consume the content
A CMS that only manages images
A CMS that runs without a server
explanation: A headless CMS separates the content management backend from the presentation layer, delivering content via APIs to any frontend (web, mobile, IoT).

text: What is the key difference between SSR and SSG?
options: SSR and SSG produce identical results
SSR generates HTML on each request at runtime; SSG generates HTML once at build time
SSG is slower than SSR
SSR does not use a server
explanation: SSR generates HTML on the server for each incoming request (dynamic), while SSG pre-generates all HTML at build time (static), trading freshness for speed.

text: What are Web Components?
options: Third-party JavaScript libraries
A set of web platform APIs that allow creating reusable, encapsulated custom HTML elements
Pre-built CSS templates
Server-side rendering frameworks
explanation: Web Components use Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, and HTML Templates to create reusable, framework-agnostic UI components with encapsulated styling and behavior.

text: What is WebAssembly (Wasm)?
options: A new version of JavaScript
A binary instruction format that allows code written in languages like C, C++, and Rust to run in the browser at near-native speed
A web assembly framework for building UIs
A CSS preprocessor
explanation: WebAssembly is a low-level binary format designed to run compiled code (from C, C++, Rust, etc.) in the browser at near-native performance speeds alongside JavaScript.

text: What is a Single Page Application (SPA)?
options: A website with only one page of content
A web application that loads a single HTML page and dynamically updates content without full page reloads
A one-page marketing website
An application that only works on one browser
explanation: An SPA loads one HTML page initially and dynamically rewrites content using JavaScript, providing a fluid experience without full page reloads when navigating.

text: What is edge computing in the context of web development?
options: Computing only in the browser
Running server-side logic at CDN edge locations geographically closer to users for lower latency
A CSS technique for rounded corners
Computing at the edge of a network firewall
explanation: Edge computing runs application logic at CDN edge locations worldwide, reducing latency by processing requests closer to users rather than in a centralized data center.

text: What is Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR)?
options: Regenerating all static pages at once
A technique that allows updating static pages after build time by regenerating them in the background when traffic arrives, without rebuilding the entire site
Incrementally loading JavaScript files
A database replication strategy
explanation: ISR combines SSG and SSR benefits by serving static pages and regenerating them in the background at specified intervals, keeping content fresh without full rebuilds.

text: What is the Shadow DOM?
options: A hidden DOM used by browser extensions
A browser API that provides encapsulated DOM and CSS scope for Web Components, preventing style leakage
A backup copy of the DOM
The DOM as rendered by screen readers
explanation: Shadow DOM creates an encapsulated DOM tree attached to a component, isolating its internal structure and styles from the rest of the page to prevent conflicts.

text: What is the app shell architecture in PWAs?
options: A command-line interface for PWAs
A design pattern where the minimal HTML, CSS, and JavaScript for the UI shell is cached locally, enabling instant loading and offline access
A server-side rendering technique
A security shell around the application
explanation: The app shell model caches the core UI framework (navigation, layout) locally, allowing instant loading on repeat visits and filling in dynamic content from the network.

text: What is the web app manifest?
options: A shipping manifest for web deployments
A JSON file that provides metadata about a PWA (name, icons, theme color, display mode) enabling installation and app-like behavior
A list of all JavaScript dependencies
A security configuration file
explanation: The web app manifest is a JSON file that defines how a PWA appears when installed, including its name, icons, theme colors, start URL, and display mode.

text: What is server-side rendering hydration?
options: Adding water cooling to servers
The process where client-side JavaScript attaches event listeners and state to server-rendered HTML, making it interactive
Compressing server responses with water-marking
Loading external CSS files on the server
explanation: Hydration is the process where the client-side framework takes over server-rendered HTML by attaching event handlers and initializing component state, making the page interactive.

text: What are micro frontends?
options: Very small web pages
An architectural approach that extends microservices to the frontend, where independent teams own and deploy separate sections of the UI
Responsive design for small screens
Minimized CSS files
explanation: Micro frontends decompose a frontend into independently deployable pieces owned by different teams, enabling independent development cycles and technology choices.

text: What is the purpose of the Cache API in service workers?
options: To cache database queries
To programmatically store and retrieve network request-response pairs, enabling offline functionality and faster loading
To cache CSS variables
To store user preferences
explanation: The Cache API allows service workers to store request-response pairs programmatically, enabling custom caching strategies for offline support and performance optimization.

text: What is the difference between a web app and a native app?
options: There is no difference
Web apps run in browsers and are accessed via URLs; native apps are installed from app stores and have direct access to device hardware APIs
Native apps only work offline
Web apps are always faster than native apps
explanation: Web apps run in browsers using web technologies, while native apps are platform-specific, installed from stores, and have full access to device capabilities like camera and sensors.

text: What are custom elements in Web Components?
options: Non-standard HTML attributes
JavaScript-defined HTML elements with custom tag names and behavior that extend the browser's built-in element set
CSS custom properties (variables)
Custom form validation messages
explanation: Custom elements allow developers to define new HTML tags with custom behavior using JavaScript classes, extending HTMLElement and registering with customElements.define().

text: What is Astro in modern web development?
options: A JavaScript animation library
A web framework that delivers zero-JavaScript by default, using island architecture to hydrate only interactive components
A CSS framework similar to Tailwind
A testing framework
explanation: Astro is a modern web framework that ships zero JavaScript by default and uses an island architecture, only hydrating interactive components while keeping the rest as static HTML.

text: What is the islands architecture in web development?
options: A design pattern for island-themed websites
An architecture where a mostly static HTML page contains independent interactive regions (islands) that are hydrated separately, reducing total JavaScript
A server deployment pattern across geographic islands
A database sharding strategy
explanation: Islands architecture renders most of the page as static HTML, with isolated interactive components (islands) that hydrate independently, minimizing client-side JavaScript overhead.

text: What is partial hydration?
options: Loading half of the CSS stylesheets
Selectively hydrating only the interactive components of a server-rendered page instead of hydrating the entire page
Loading partial HTML templates
Using half the available memory for rendering
explanation: Partial hydration selectively makes only interactive components on a server-rendered page dynamic, avoiding the cost of hydrating static content and reducing JavaScript execution.

text: What is React Server Components?
options: React components that only render CSS
Components that render exclusively on the server, sending only the rendered output (not component JavaScript) to the client, reducing bundle size
A React testing utility for servers
Components that manage server configurations
explanation: React Server Components run only on the server, accessing backend resources directly and sending rendered output without the component code to the client, reducing JavaScript bundle size.

text: What is streaming SSR?
options: Streaming video from the server
A rendering technique that sends HTML to the client in chunks as it is generated, allowing the browser to start rendering before the entire page is ready
Streaming CSS animations
Sending server logs in real-time
explanation: Streaming SSR sends HTML incrementally as it's generated, allowing the browser to start painting content immediately rather than waiting for the complete HTML response.

text: What is the purpose of Workbox in PWA development?
options: A CSS layout tool
A set of libraries from Google that simplify service worker creation with pre-built caching strategies, precaching, and background sync
A JavaScript bundler
A database for offline storage
explanation: Workbox is a Google library that simplifies service worker implementation with modules for common patterns like runtime caching strategies, precaching, and background sync.

text: What is the difference between Web Workers and Service Workers?
options: They are identical browser features
Web Workers run background JavaScript for CPU-intensive tasks; Service Workers are proxy-like workers that intercept network requests and enable offline features
Service Workers are faster than Web Workers
Web Workers only work in Node.js
explanation: Web Workers handle CPU-intensive computations off the main thread, while Service Workers act as network proxies between the browser and server, managing caching and offline capabilities.

text: What is edge-side rendering (ESR)?
options: Rendering CSS border edges
A technique that renders dynamic content at CDN edge locations, combining the benefits of SSR (dynamic content) with CDN-like proximity to users
Rendering fallback pages during errors
A technique for rendering SVG edges
explanation: Edge-side rendering executes server-side rendering logic at CDN edge nodes close to users, delivering dynamic content with the low latency benefits of edge computing.

text: What is the Qwik framework's resumability concept?
options: Resuming paused animations
An approach where the application serializes its state during SSR so the client can resume execution without re-executing all component code, eliminating hydration
Restarting crashed servers automatically
Resuming failed network requests
explanation: Qwik's resumability serializes component state and event bindings during SSR, allowing the client to resume where the server left off without replaying component initialization (hydration).

text: What is the Speculation Rules API?
options: An API for financial speculation applications
A browser API that allows developers to define rules for speculative prerendering and prefetching of future navigations, improving perceived performance
An API for guessing user preferences
A testing API for experimental features
explanation: The Speculation Rules API lets developers define JSON rules for speculative prefetch and prerender of URLs the user might navigate to, making subsequent page loads nearly instant.

text: What is the View Transitions API?
options: An API for managing database views
A browser API that enables smooth animated transitions between different DOM states or page navigations, without requiring a JavaScript animation framework
An API for switching between dark and light mode
An API for managing viewport sizes
explanation: The View Transitions API provides a native mechanism for creating smooth visual transitions between page states or navigations, capturing snapshots and animating between old and new views.

text: What protocol is commonly used to transfer web pages over the internet?
options: FTP (File Transfer Protocol)
SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer)
HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)
SSH (Secure Shell Protocol)
explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the foundation of data communication on the World Wide Web.

text: What does CSS stand for?
options: Computer Style Sheets
Cascading Style Sheets
Creative Style System
Colorful Style Syntax
explanation: CSS stands for Cascading Style Sheets, used to control the visual presentation of HTML documents.

text: Which of the following is considered a back-end programming language?
options: HTML markup language
CSS styling language
Python programming language
SVG graphics format
explanation: Python is a general-purpose programming language commonly used for back-end server logic.

text: What is a domain name in the context of the web?
options: A type of web programming language
A human-readable address for a website
A database management system tool
A browser extension for bookmarks
explanation: A domain name is a human-readable address like example.com that maps to a server's IP address.

text: What does DNS stand for in web technology?
options: Dynamic Network Service
Digital Naming Standard
Domain Name System
Data Network Security
explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses.

text: Which element is the root element of an HTML document?
options: <body> element
<head> element
<html> element
<meta> element
explanation: The <html> element is the root element that wraps all content in an HTML document.

text: What is the purpose of HTTPS?
options: To speed up page loading times
To encrypt communication between browser and server
To compress images on web pages
To cache static files in the browser
explanation: HTTPS encrypts data transmitted between the browser and server using TLS/SSL for secure communication.

text: What is a static website?
options: A site that generates content dynamically per request
A site whose content is fixed and does not change per user
A site that requires a database backend to function
A site built exclusively with JavaScript frameworks
explanation: A static website delivers the same pre-built HTML files to every visitor without server-side processing.

text: What does the term 'responsive design' mean?
options: A design that loads content very quickly
A design that adapts layout to different screen sizes
A design that responds to voice commands
A design built only for desktop monitors
explanation: Responsive design adjusts the layout and content of a website to fit different screen sizes and devices.

text: What is a web hosting service?
options: Software for writing HTML code
A service that stores website files on a server
A browser plugin for faster browsing
A tool for testing website performance
explanation: A web hosting service provides server space to store website files and makes them accessible via the internet.

text: What is the main difference between a web application and a static website?
options: Web applications use HTML while static sites use XML
Static sites require a database while web apps do not
Web applications have interactive server-side logic while static sites serve fixed content
Static sites support user authentication while web apps do not
explanation: Web applications process user input with server-side logic and generate dynamic content, while static sites deliver fixed HTML files.

text: What is the role of a package manager like npm in web development?
options: It compiles TypeScript code into JavaScript
It manages third-party libraries and their dependencies
It serves as a web server for production deployment
It provides a visual editor for designing web pages
explanation: Package managers like npm automate installing, updating, and managing project dependencies and third-party libraries.

text: What is a Content Delivery Network (CDN)?
options: A tool for writing server-side application code
A distributed network of servers that delivers content from locations near the user
A database system for managing website content
A programming language for building web services
explanation: A CDN distributes copies of content across multiple geographically dispersed servers to reduce latency.

text: What does the term 'full-stack developer' mean?
options: A developer who only works on mobile applications
A developer who specializes in database administration only
A developer skilled in both front-end and back-end technologies
A developer who focuses exclusively on UI/UX design
explanation: A full-stack developer has skills in both client-side (front-end) and server-side (back-end) development.

text: What is the purpose of version control systems like Git in web development?
options: To host websites on production servers
To track changes in code and enable collaborative development
To compile code into machine-readable format
To optimize images for faster page loading
explanation: Version control systems track code history, enable collaboration, and allow reverting to previous states of a codebase.

text: What is the difference between client-side and server-side rendering?
options: Client-side rendering only works on desktop computers
Server-side rendering generates HTML on the server before sending it to the browser
Client-side rendering requires no JavaScript at all
Server-side rendering does not support dynamic content
explanation: Server-side rendering produces HTML on the server for each request, while client-side rendering builds the page in the browser using JavaScript.

text: What is the purpose of a web framework?
options: To replace the need for HTML in web pages
To provide a structured foundation with reusable tools for building web applications
To serve as a web browser alternative for users
To manage operating system resources for servers
explanation: A web framework provides pre-built components, conventions, and tools that simplify and accelerate web application development.

text: What is minification in web development?
options: Reducing the number of pages on a website
Removing unnecessary characters from code without changing functionality
Decreasing the resolution of images for mobile
Limiting the number of users who can access a site
explanation: Minification removes whitespace, comments, and shortens variable names to reduce file size and improve load times.

text: What is the difference between HTTP GET and POST methods?
options: GET is encrypted while POST sends data in plain text
GET requests data from a server while POST submits data to a server
POST is faster than GET for all types of requests
GET can send large file uploads while POST cannot
explanation: GET retrieves resources from a server and appends data to the URL, while POST sends data in the request body for creating or updating resources.

text: What is the Document Object Model (DOM)?
options: A CSS framework for responsive layouts
A programming interface representing HTML as a tree of objects
A server-side database management tool
A file format for storing web page data
explanation: The DOM is a programming interface that represents an HTML document as a tree structure, allowing JavaScript to manipulate page content.

text: What is the critical rendering path in web browsers?
options: The file path where browser cache is stored on disk
The sequence of steps a browser takes to convert HTML, CSS, and JS into rendered pixels
The network route data travels from server to browser
The order in which JavaScript functions are executed
explanation: The critical rendering path is the sequence of steps (DOM construction, CSSOM construction, render tree, layout, paint) the browser follows to render a page.

text: What is the purpose of a service worker in web applications?
options: To manage server-side database connections
To run background scripts that enable offline support and push notifications
To compile TypeScript code during the build process
To handle routing between different server endpoints
explanation: Service workers are scripts that run in the background, enabling features like offline caching, push notifications, and background sync.

text: What is the difference between progressive enhancement and graceful degradation?
options: Progressive enhancement starts with a basic experience and adds features, while graceful degradation starts full-featured and falls back
Graceful degradation requires no JavaScript while progressive enhancement does
Progressive enhancement only applies to mobile devices
Graceful degradation is a front-end concept while progressive enhancement is back-end only
explanation: Progressive enhancement builds from a basic functional baseline upward, while graceful degradation starts with full features and provides fallbacks for less capable browsers.

text: What is the purpose of a Content Security Policy (CSP) header?
options: To compress HTTP responses for faster delivery
To specify which content sources the browser should trust and execute
To cache static assets on the client side
To define the character encoding of HTML documents
explanation: CSP is an HTTP header that restricts which sources of content (scripts, styles, images) a browser may load, helping prevent XSS attacks.

text: What is the role of WebAssembly (Wasm) in modern web development?
options: It replaces CSS for styling web applications
It allows running compiled code from languages like C++ and Rust in the browser at near-native speed
It is a server-side templating engine for HTML
It provides a database engine that runs inside browsers
explanation: WebAssembly is a binary instruction format that enables code written in languages like C, C++, and Rust to run in browsers at near-native performance.

text: What is the difference between a monolithic and microservices web architecture?
options: Monolithic uses JavaScript while microservices use Python exclusively
Monolithic deploys as a single unit while microservices are independently deployable services
Microservices cannot communicate over HTTP
Monolithic architectures do not support databases
explanation: Monolithic applications are deployed as a single unit, while microservices decompose functionality into small, independently deployable services.

text: What is tree shaking in modern JavaScript bundlers?
options: A method for organizing file directories in a project
A technique that removes unused code from the final production bundle
A process of randomizing variable names for security
A strategy for balancing server load across nodes
explanation: Tree shaking is a dead-code elimination technique that analyzes import/export statements to remove unused modules from the final bundle.

text: What is a Progressive Web Application (PWA)?
options: A mobile-only application built with Swift or Kotlin
A web app that uses modern APIs to provide app-like experiences including offline support and installability
A website that progressively loads more advertisements
A framework for building server-side rendered pages
explanation: PWAs combine web technologies with modern APIs like service workers and web manifests to deliver reliable, installable, app-like experiences.

text: What is the Jamstack architecture?
options: A stack using Java, Angular, and MySQL exclusively
An architecture using pre-rendered markup, JavaScript, and APIs to decouple the front end from the back end
A Java-based monolithic application framework
A JavaScript testing methodology for web components
explanation: Jamstack (JavaScript, APIs, Markup) pre-renders pages at build time and uses APIs for dynamic functionality, decoupling the front end from back-end servers.

text: What is the purpose of HTTP/2 server push?
options: To push database updates to connected clients in real time
To allow the server to send resources to the client before the client requests them
To force the browser to clear its local cache
To redirect users from HTTP to HTTPS automatically
explanation: HTTP/2 server push allows the server to proactively send resources like CSS and JS files to the client along with the initial HTML response before they are requested.

text: What is a client in client-server architecture?
options: The database that stores application data
The device or application that requests services from a server
The programming language used on the backend
The network cable connecting two computers
explanation: A client is the device or software application that sends requests to a server and receives responses.

text: What does the HTTP status code 200 indicate?
options: Resource not found on server
Internal server error occurred
Request was successful (OK)
Client is unauthorized to access
explanation: HTTP status code 200 means the request has succeeded and the server returned the requested resource.

text: What is an IP address?
options: A password used to log into websites
A unique numerical label assigned to each device on a network
A programming language for server scripts
A file format for storing web pages
explanation: An IP address is a unique numerical identifier assigned to every device connected to a computer network.

text: What does the HTTP status code 404 indicate?
options: The server processed the request successfully
The client needs to authenticate first
The requested resource was not found
The server is temporarily unavailable
explanation: HTTP 404 means the server cannot find the requested resource at the given URL.

text: What is a request header in HTTP?
options: The body content sent in a POST request
Metadata sent by the client to the server with a request
The URL path of the requested resource
The visual header section of a web page
explanation: HTTP request headers contain metadata such as content type, authorization tokens, and accepted formats sent from client to server.

text: What is a server in web architecture?
options: A computer that provides resources and services to clients
A tool for designing website user interfaces
A browser extension that blocks advertisements
A scripting language used for animations
explanation: A server is a computer or software that listens for client requests and provides resources or services in response.

text: What is the default port number for HTTP?
options: Port 21
Port 443
Port 80
Port 3306
explanation: HTTP traffic uses port 80 by default, while HTTPS uses port 443.

text: What does REST stand for in web APIs?
options: Remote Execution Server Technology
Representational State Transfer
Real-time Event Streaming Tool
Responsive Element Style Template
explanation: REST stands for Representational State Transfer, an architectural style for designing networked applications.

text: What is a cookie in web technology?
options: A piece of data stored by the browser on behalf of a website
A type of server-side programming language
A CSS property for styling form inputs
A JavaScript function for DOM manipulation
explanation: A cookie is a small piece of data stored in the user's browser by a website, often used for session management and tracking.

text: What does the HTTP status code 500 indicate?
options: The request was redirected permanently
The resource was successfully created
The client sent a bad request format
An internal server error has occurred
explanation: HTTP 500 indicates that the server encountered an unexpected condition that prevented it from fulfilling the request.

text: What is the difference between stateful and stateless protocols?
options: Stateful protocols are faster than stateless protocols
Stateless protocols retain session data while stateful do not
Stateful protocols remember previous interactions while stateless protocols treat each request independently
Stateless protocols can only handle GET requests
explanation: Stateful protocols retain information about previous requests across a session, while stateless protocols like HTTP treat each request as independent.

text: What is a reverse proxy in web architecture?
options: A client-side script that modifies outgoing requests
A server that sits in front of backend servers and forwards client requests to them
A browser plugin that hides the user's identity
A database that stores proxy configuration data
explanation: A reverse proxy receives client requests and forwards them to appropriate backend servers, providing load balancing, caching, and security benefits.

text: What is the purpose of a load balancer?
options: To encrypt data between client and server
To distribute incoming network traffic across multiple servers
To compress JavaScript files for faster loading
To convert HTML pages into PDF documents
explanation: A load balancer distributes incoming requests across multiple backend servers to ensure no single server is overwhelmed.

text: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling?
options: Horizontal scaling adds more servers while vertical scaling adds more power to an existing server
Vertical scaling adds more servers while horizontal scaling adds more CPU
Horizontal scaling only works with databases
Vertical scaling is always cheaper than horizontal scaling
explanation: Horizontal scaling (scaling out) adds more machines to the pool, while vertical scaling (scaling up) adds more resources to an existing machine.

text: What is a WebSocket connection?
options: A one-way communication channel from server to client only
A full-duplex communication protocol that maintains a persistent connection between client and server
A protocol used exclusively for file uploads
A method for connecting to SQL databases from the browser
explanation: WebSocket provides full-duplex communication over a single persistent TCP connection, allowing both client and server to send data at any time.

text: What is the purpose of an API gateway?
options: To render HTML pages on the server side
To serve as a single entry point that routes requests to multiple backend services
To store API documentation in a database
To compile JavaScript code into machine language
explanation: An API gateway acts as a single entry point for client requests, handling routing, rate limiting, authentication, and forwarding to appropriate microservices.

text: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous communication?
options: Synchronous is always faster than asynchronous
Asynchronous communication requires the sender to wait for a response before continuing
Synchronous communication blocks until a response is received while asynchronous allows continuing without waiting
Asynchronous communication only works with WebSocket connections
explanation: Synchronous communication requires waiting for a response before proceeding, while asynchronous allows processing to continue and handles the response when it arrives.

text: What is the role of a message queue in web architecture?
options: To display chat messages on a web page
To buffer and manage asynchronous communication between services
To sort email messages for a mail server
To queue CSS styles for rendering priority
explanation: A message queue enables asynchronous communication between services by storing messages until the consuming service is ready to process them.

text: What is session management in web applications?
options: Managing CSS animation sessions in the browser
Tracking and maintaining user state across multiple HTTP requests
Scheduling database backup sessions on the server
Managing concurrent JavaScript execution threads
explanation: Session management tracks user state across stateless HTTP requests using mechanisms like cookies, tokens, or server-side sessions.

text: What is caching in web architecture?
options: Permanently deleting old data from servers
Storing copies of frequently accessed data in a faster storage layer to reduce latency
Converting images to a smaller file format
Encrypting sensitive data before transmission
explanation: Caching stores copies of data or computed results in a faster storage layer (like memory) to reduce response times and server load.

text: What is the CAP theorem in distributed systems?
options: A theorem stating that a system must use Caching, APIs, and Proxies
A theorem stating that a distributed system can guarantee at most two of Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance simultaneously
A theorem about compressing, archiving, and packaging web assets
A theorem defining the maximum connections a server can handle
explanation: The CAP theorem states that in a distributed system, you can only guarantee two of three properties: Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance.

text: What is eventual consistency in distributed systems?
options: A guarantee that all reads always return the latest write immediately
A model where all replicas will converge to the same state given enough time without new updates
A method for eventually removing stale cache entries
A technique for gradually loading web page components
explanation: Eventual consistency guarantees that if no new updates are made, all replicas will eventually converge to the same value, trading immediate consistency for availability.

text: What is the circuit breaker pattern in microservices?
options: A hardware component that protects servers from power surges
A design pattern that prevents cascading failures by stopping requests to a failing service after a threshold
A technique for breaking up large CSS files into smaller ones
A method for splitting database tables across multiple servers
explanation: The circuit breaker pattern monitors for failures and trips open after a threshold, stopping requests to the failing service and allowing it time to recover.

text: What is the difference between a forward proxy and a reverse proxy?
options: A forward proxy sits in front of clients while a reverse proxy sits in front of servers
A reverse proxy is used only for HTTPS while a forward proxy handles HTTP
A forward proxy handles server responses while a reverse proxy handles client requests
A forward proxy is faster than a reverse proxy in all scenarios
explanation: A forward proxy acts on behalf of clients to access servers, while a reverse proxy acts on behalf of servers to handle incoming client requests.

text: What is the purpose of a service mesh in microservices architecture?
options: To create HTML mesh layouts for web pages
To manage inter-service communication with features like load balancing, encryption, and observability
To connect multiple databases into a single query endpoint
To generate CSS grid mesh patterns for responsive design
explanation: A service mesh is a dedicated infrastructure layer that handles service-to-service communication, providing traffic management, security, and observability.

text: What is the saga pattern for managing distributed transactions?
options: A design pattern for creating long narrative content on web pages
A pattern that manages distributed transactions through a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions for rollback
A method for logging server activity over extended time periods
A technique for progressively loading large datasets from a database
explanation: The saga pattern splits a distributed transaction into a series of local transactions, each with a compensating action that undoes its effect if a later step fails.

text: What is the CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) pattern?
options: A CSS technique for querying responsive breakpoints
A pattern that separates read and write operations into different models for scalability and performance
A method for compressing query strings in URLs
A technique for combining multiple REST endpoints into one
explanation: CQRS separates the data modification (command) model from the data retrieval (query) model, allowing each to be optimized and scaled independently.

text: What is event sourcing in web application architecture?
options: A technique for handling DOM events in JavaScript
A pattern that stores state changes as a sequence of immutable events rather than current state only
A method for sourcing third-party event handling libraries
A strategy for outsourcing event planning to external APIs
explanation: Event sourcing persists all state changes as an append-only sequence of events, allowing the system to reconstruct any past state by replaying events.

text: What is the sidecar pattern in microservices?
options: A technique for rendering sidebar navigation components
A pattern where a helper process runs alongside a main service to provide supporting functionality like logging and proxying
A method for attaching secondary stylesheets to HTML pages
A strategy for deploying backup servers alongside primary ones
explanation: The sidecar pattern deploys a helper container or process alongside a main service to handle cross-cutting concerns like logging, monitoring, and networking.

text: What is the back-pressure mechanism in distributed systems?
options: A method for compressing HTTP response bodies
A strategy where a consuming service signals upstream producers to slow down when overwhelmed
A technique for reversing failed database transactions
A pattern for rolling back deployment changes in production
explanation: Back-pressure is a flow control mechanism where an overwhelmed downstream service signals upstream producers to reduce the rate of incoming data to prevent overload.

text: Which HTML tag is used to create a paragraph?
options: <div> tag
<span> tag
tag
tag
explanation:
The

tag defines a paragraph of text in an HTML document.

text: What is the purpose of the <title> tag in HTML?
options: To display a heading on the page
To set the document's title shown in the browser tab
To create a tooltip on an element
To define a caption for a table
explanation: The <title> tag defines the title of the HTML document, which appears in the browser tab and search results.

text: Which HTML tag is used to insert an image?
options: <picture> tag
tag
<figure> tag
<media> tag
explanation: The tag is used to embed an image in an HTML document using the src attribute.

text: What is the purpose of the <a> tag in HTML?
options: To define an article section
To create a hyperlink to another page
To add an audio element to a page
To align text within a container
explanation: The <a> (anchor) tag creates hyperlinks that navigate to other pages, files, or locations within the same page.

text: Which attribute specifies the URL in an anchor tag?
options: src attribute
link attribute
href attribute
url attribute
explanation: The href (hypertext reference) attribute specifies the URL that the hyperlink points to.

text: Which HTML tag creates an unordered list?
options: <ol> tag
<li> tag
<dl> tag
<ul> tag
explanation: The <ul> tag creates an unordered (bulleted) list, with each item wrapped in an <li> tag.

text:
What does the
tag do in HTML?
options: Creates a bold text element
Inserts a horizontal rule line
Defines a line break in text
Creates a border around elements
explanation:
The
tag inserts a single line break in the content, and it is a self-closing tag.

text: Which HTML tag is used to define a table row?
options: <td> tag
<th> tag
<tr> tag
<table> tag
explanation: The <tr> tag defines a row in an HTML table, containing <td> or <th> cells.

text: What is the purpose of the <head> section in HTML?
options: To display the main content visible to users
To contain metadata, links to stylesheets, and the document title
To define the header navigation of a web page
To create a heading element for page sections
explanation: The <head> section contains metadata, links to CSS, scripts, and the document title, but its content is not directly displayed on the page.

text: Which HTML attribute is used to provide alternative text for an image?
options: title attribute
alt attribute
desc attribute
label attribute
explanation: The alt attribute provides alternative text that describes the image for screen readers and when the image cannot be displayed.

text: What is the difference between the <section> and <div> elements?
options: The <div> element has semantic meaning while <section> does not
The <section> element represents a thematic grouping of content while <div> is a generic container
The <section> element can only contain text while <div> holds any content
The <div> element is a block element while <section> is inline
explanation: The <section> element is semantic and represents a thematic grouping of content, while <div> is a generic container with no inherent meaning.

text: What is the purpose of the <meta charset='UTF-8'> tag?
options: It defines the color scheme for the web page
It specifies the character encoding used by the HTML document
It sets the page zoom level for mobile devices
It determines the default font family for text
explanation: The meta charset tag declares the character encoding (UTF-8) for the document, ensuring text is displayed correctly across languages.

text: What is the difference between inline and block-level HTML elements?
options: Inline elements start on a new line while block elements do not
Block-level elements take up the full width available while inline elements only take up necessary width
Inline elements cannot contain text content
Block-level elements cannot be nested inside other elements
explanation: Block-level elements start on a new line and span the full width, while inline elements flow within text and only occupy the space their content requires.

text: What is the purpose of the <fieldset> element in HTML forms?
options: To create a text input field in the form
To group related form controls together with an optional caption
To set the field width of all input elements
To validate form data before submission
explanation: The <fieldset> element groups related form controls and labels, optionally with a <legend> element providing a caption for the group.

text: What does the 'defer' attribute do on a <script> tag?
options: It delays the script download until user interaction
It downloads the script in parallel and executes it after HTML parsing is complete
It prevents the script from accessing the DOM
It defers the script to run only on mobile devices
explanation: The defer attribute downloads the script asynchronously during HTML parsing and executes it in order after the document has been fully parsed.

text: What is the purpose of the <datalist> element?
options: To define a list of data tables on the page
To provide a predefined list of autocomplete suggestions for an input element
To store structured data in the HTML document
To create a database connection from the browser
explanation: The <datalist> element provides a list of predefined <option> values that appear as autocomplete suggestions when a user types in an associated input field.

text: What is the difference between the 'id' and 'class' attributes in HTML?
options: An id can be reused on multiple elements while a class must be unique
An id must be unique within a page while a class can be applied to multiple elements
A class is used for JavaScript while an id is only for CSS
An id applies styles globally while a class applies them locally
explanation: The id attribute must be unique per page and identifies a single element, while the class attribute can be shared across multiple elements for grouping.

text: What is the purpose of the <nav> semantic element?
options: To define the main content area of a page
To represent a section of navigation links
To add a navigational sidebar with scrolling
To create animated page transitions
explanation: The <nav> element represents a section of the page that contains navigation links to other pages or sections within the page.

text: What does the 'required' attribute do on an HTML form input?
options: It makes the input field read-only for users
It prevents the form from submitting if the input is empty
It automatically fills the input with a default value
It requires the input to be a specific data type
explanation: The required attribute specifies that the form field must be filled in before the form can be submitted, triggering browser validation.

text: What is the purpose of the <output> element in HTML?
options: To display server log output on a web page
To represent the result of a calculation or user action
To output error messages from JavaScript
To export data from an HTML table to a file
explanation: The <output> element represents the result of a calculation or user action, typically associated with form inputs via the for attribute.

text: What is the Shadow DOM in web components?
options: A hidden backup copy of the DOM stored in memory
An encapsulated DOM tree attached to an element that isolates its styles and markup from the main document
A technique for rendering invisible elements on a page
A browser debugging tool for inspecting dark-themed pages
explanation: The Shadow DOM provides encapsulation by attaching a hidden DOM tree to an element, keeping its internal structure, styles, and behavior isolated from the rest of the page.

text: What is the purpose of the 'content-editable' attribute?
options: It allows the server to edit HTML content remotely
It makes the element's content directly editable by the user in the browser
It restricts content editing to authenticated admins
It enables CSS properties to be modified at runtime
explanation: The contenteditable attribute makes an element's content editable by the user directly in the browser, enabling rich text editing without form inputs.

text: What is the purpose of the <template> element in HTML?
options: To define a CSS template for styling multiple pages
To hold HTML content that is not rendered on load but can be instantiated later via JavaScript
To create a template for server-side email generation
To define placeholder text for empty form fields
explanation: The <template> element holds HTML markup that is parsed but not rendered until it is cloned and inserted into the DOM via JavaScript.

text: What are custom HTML elements and how are they defined?
options: Elements created by modifying browser source code directly
User-defined elements registered with customElements.define() that extend HTMLElement
Elements that are only available in specific browsers
Predefined elements with custom CSS class names applied
explanation: Custom elements are defined by creating a class that extends HTMLElement and registering it with customElements.define(), enabling reusable web components.

text: What is the difference between the 'async' and 'defer' attributes on script tags?
options: Async scripts execute in document order while defer scripts execute as soon as downloaded
Async scripts execute immediately after download regardless of parsing, while defer scripts execute after parsing in document order
Defer scripts block HTML parsing while async scripts do not
Async and defer both execute scripts before the DOM is constructed
explanation: Async scripts download in parallel and execute immediately when ready (out of order), while defer scripts download in parallel but execute in order after HTML parsing completes.

text: What is the Intersection Observer API used for in HTML/JavaScript?
options: Observing changes to an element's CSS properties
Detecting when an element enters or exits the visible viewport efficiently
Monitoring network request intersections between APIs
Tracking mouse cursor intersections with clickable elements
explanation: The Intersection Observer API asynchronously observes changes in the intersection of a target element with the viewport or a parent, used for lazy loading and infinite scroll.

text: What is the purpose of ARIA roles in HTML?
options: To define animation roles for CSS transitions
To provide semantic meaning to elements for assistive technologies like screen readers
To assign permission roles for user authentication
To specify the rendering priority of page elements
explanation: ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) roles provide additional semantic information to assistive technologies, improving accessibility for users with disabilities.

text: What is the purpose of the 'srcset' attribute on the tag?
options: To set the source code of a JavaScript module
To provide multiple image sources for different screen resolutions and sizes
To define a set of CSS source files for the image
To specify backup URLs if the primary image fails
explanation: The srcset attribute lets browsers choose the most appropriate image from a set of candidates based on screen resolution, viewport size, and pixel density.

text: What is the purpose of the <slot> element in web components?
options: To reserve memory slots for JavaScript variables
To define placeholders inside a shadow DOM where external content can be projected
To create time slot pickers in scheduling forms
To allocate CSS grid slots for layout positioning
explanation: The <slot> element is a placeholder inside a web component's shadow DOM that allows users to insert their own markup into predefined locations.

text: What is the difference between HTML entities and character references?
options: HTML entities are numerical while character references are named
Both refer to special sequences that represent reserved or special characters, using either named (&) or numeric (&) forms
Character references only work in XML documents
HTML entities are deprecated in HTML5 in favor of Unicode
explanation: HTML character references include named entities (like &) and numeric references (like &), both used to display reserved or special characters in HTML.

text: Which CSS property is used to change the text color of an element?
options: background-color property
font-style property
text-decoration property
color property
explanation: The color property in CSS sets the foreground color of an element's text content.

text: Which CSS property controls the size of text?
options: text-size property
font-weight property
font-size property
text-style property
explanation: The font-size property sets the size of the text in an element.

text: How do you select an element with a specific class in CSS?
options: Using a hash symbol (#classname)
Using a dot prefix (.classname)
Using an at sign (@classname)
Using an ampersand (&classname)
explanation: In CSS, a class selector uses a dot (.) prefix followed by the class name to target elements with that class.

text: Which CSS property sets the background color of an element?
options: color property
fill-color property
bg-color property
background-color property
explanation: The background-color property sets the background color of an element.

text: What is the CSS box model?
options: A model for creating 3D boxes on web pages
