explanation: Optimistic concurrency allows operations without locking and detects conflicts at commit time, while pessimistic concurrency acquires locks upfront to prevent conflicts.

text: What is the purpose of a dead letter queue in message-based architectures?
options: A queue for storing deleted user messages permanently
A queue that stores messages that cannot be processed successfully after maximum retry attempts for later analysis
A queue for scheduling messages to be sent at a future date
A queue that automatically deletes old messages after expiry
explanation: A dead letter queue captures messages that fail processing after exhausting retry attempts, allowing developers to analyze failures and reprocess them manually.

text: What is the difference between serverless and traditional server-based architecture?
options: Serverless applications do not use any servers at all
Serverless abstracts server management so developers only deploy functions that auto-scale and are billed per execution
Traditional servers are always more expensive than serverless
Serverless only supports Python and Go programming languages
explanation: Serverless computing abstracts infrastructure management, letting developers deploy individual functions that auto-scale and are billed per invocation rather than by server uptime.

text: What is database migration in backend development?
options: Moving data from one cloud provider to another
The process of managing incremental, reversible changes to the database schema through versioned scripts
Migrating user sessions from memory to the database
Converting a SQL database to a NoSQL database
explanation: Database migrations are versioned scripts that make incremental, trackable changes to the database schema, allowing teams to evolve the schema in a controlled way.

text: What is the outbox pattern in distributed systems?
options: A pattern for managing email outbox functionality in web apps
A pattern where events are written to a database outbox table in the same transaction as domain changes, then published asynchronously
A pattern for queuing outbound HTTP responses in the server
A pattern for storing undelivered notifications in the browser
explanation: The outbox pattern writes events to an outbox table atomically with domain data changes, then a separate process publishes them, ensuring reliable event delivery without distributed transactions.

text: What is the bulkhead pattern in microservices?
options: A pattern for storing bulk data in separate database partitions
A pattern that isolates different service consumers or functionalities into separate resource pools to prevent one failure from cascading
A pattern for processing bulk API requests in batches
A pattern for building thick boundary walls between microservices
explanation: The bulkhead pattern isolates resources (thread pools, connection pools) for different operations, so a failure or overload in one area does not exhaust resources for others.

text: What is server-side rendering (SSR)?
options: Rendering CSS animations on the server
Generating HTML content on the server for each request before sending it to the browser
Running JavaScript tests on the server before deployment
Storing rendered images on the server for caching
explanation: Server-side rendering generates the full HTML for a page on the server per request, sending ready-to-display content to the browser.

text: What is a template engine in server-side development?
options: A tool for creating CSS template files
Software that combines templates with data to produce HTML pages dynamically
An engine that generates JavaScript from HTML templates
A framework for building email templates only
explanation: A template engine (like EJS, Pug, or Handlebars) merges HTML templates with dynamic data to generate complete HTML pages on the server.

text: What is a session in server-side web development?
options: A CSS class applied during user interaction
A server-side mechanism for storing user-specific data across multiple requests
A JavaScript timer that tracks page visit duration
A database table for storing user preferences
explanation: A session stores user-specific data on the server side, identified by a session ID typically stored in a cookie, persisting across multiple HTTP requests.

text: What is the purpose of a routing system on the server?
options: To route electricity to different server components
To map incoming URL requests to the appropriate handler functions
To route CSS styles to specific HTML elements
To direct network packets between physical servers
explanation: Server-side routing maps incoming HTTP request URLs and methods to specific handler functions that process the request and return a response.

text: What is Django in web development?
options: A JavaScript runtime for building APIs
A high-level Python web framework that encourages rapid development
A CSS utility framework for responsive design
A database management system written in Go
explanation: Django is a high-level Python web framework that follows the model-template-view pattern and provides built-in features like ORM, authentication, and admin interface.

text: What is the MVC pattern in server-side development?
options: A JavaScript module versioning convention
An architectural pattern that separates an application into Model, View, and Controller components
A database management and version control system
A method for validating CSS against HTML markup
explanation: MVC separates application logic into Model (data), View (presentation), and Controller (request handling), promoting organized and maintainable code.

text: What is static site generation (SSG)?
options: Generating CSS files statically before deployment
Pre-rendering HTML pages at build time rather than on each request
Creating static IP addresses for web servers
Generating database schemas from static configuration files
explanation: Static site generation pre-renders all pages to HTML at build time, resulting in fast-loading pages that can be served directly from a CDN.

text: What is a web application framework?
options: A physical framework for mounting servers in data centers
A software framework that provides structure and tools for building web applications
A CSS framework for creating web page wireframes
A browser extension framework for adding features
explanation: A web application framework provides reusable components, conventions, and tools (routing, templating, database access) for building web applications efficiently.

text: What is Flask in Python web development?
options: A full-featured database management tool for Python
A lightweight WSGI micro web framework for Python
A Python-based CSS preprocessor
A testing framework for Python applications
explanation: Flask is a lightweight Python micro web framework that provides core features like routing and templating without enforcing specific project structures.

text: What is server-side scripting?
options: Writing CSS scripts for server configuration
Executing code on the server to generate dynamic web content before sending it to the client
Scripting that runs inside the browser's console
Automating file management tasks on web servers
explanation: Server-side scripting uses languages like PHP, Python, or Node.js to execute code on the server, generating dynamic HTML content per request.

text: What is the difference between SSR and SSG?
options: SSR generates pages at build time while SSG generates pages at request time
SSR generates HTML on each request while SSG pre-renders HTML at build time
SSG requires a running server while SSR does not
SSR and SSG both generate pages at build time with no difference
explanation: SSR generates HTML dynamically on the server for each incoming request, while SSG generates all HTML pages at build time, serving them as static files.

text: What is the purpose of a reverse proxy like Nginx in server-side architecture?
options: To write server-side JavaScript code faster
To sit in front of application servers handling load balancing, SSL termination, and static file serving
To reverse the order of database queries for optimization
To proxy CSS requests to a dedicated styling server
explanation: Nginx as a reverse proxy handles load balancing, SSL termination, caching, and static file serving in front of application servers.

text: What is incremental static regeneration (ISR)?
options: A technique for incrementally updating CSS styles across pages
A hybrid approach that regenerates static pages in the background after a specified revalidation period
A method for incrementally loading JavaScript modules
A process for regenerating server SSL certificates automatically
explanation: ISR combines SSG with on-demand regeneration, serving cached static pages while rebuilding them in the background after a configured revalidation interval.

text: What is the purpose of server-side caching?
options: To cache CSS files in the server's memory
To store computed responses or data in memory to reduce processing time and database load for repeated requests
To cache user credentials on the server permanently
To store JavaScript bundles on the server for faster compilation
explanation: Server-side caching stores frequently accessed data or computed responses in memory (using tools like Redis) to reduce database queries and response times.

text: What is the purpose of a process manager like PM2 for Node.js?
options: To manage CSS processing pipelines on the server
To keep Node.js applications running, handle restarts on crashes, and enable clustering
To manage database processes and query optimization
To process and minify JavaScript files for production
explanation: PM2 is a process manager that keeps Node.js apps alive, automatically restarts on crashes, provides clustering for multi-core usage, and offers monitoring.

text: What is the purpose of streaming SSR?
options: To stream video content from the server to clients
To send HTML in chunks as it is generated, allowing the browser to start rendering before the full page is ready
To stream CSS styles incrementally to the browser
To broadcast server events to multiple clients simultaneously
explanation: Streaming SSR sends HTML in chunks as the server generates them, allowing the browser to start parsing and rendering early, improving Time to First Byte.

text: What is the purpose of Next.js in the React ecosystem?
options: A CSS framework specifically designed for React styling
A React framework that provides SSR, SSG, routing, and full-stack capabilities out of the box
A testing library for React component unit tests
A state management library that replaces Redux
explanation: Next.js is a React framework providing server-side rendering, static site generation, file-based routing, API routes, and other full-stack features.

text: What is webhook in server-side development?
options: A CSS hook that triggers style changes on the server
An HTTP callback that sends real-time data to a specified URL when an event occurs
A JavaScript hook for intercepting network requests
A method for hooking into the browser's rendering pipeline
explanation: A webhook is a user-defined HTTP callback that is triggered by a specific event, sending real-time data to a configured URL when that event occurs.

text: What is the difference between monolithic and modular server architectures?
options: Monolithic uses multiple servers while modular uses one server
Monolithic bundles all functionality in one codebase while modular separates concerns into independent modules
Modular architectures cannot be deployed to the cloud
Monolithic architectures are always faster than modular ones
explanation: Monolithic architectures combine all application functionality in a single codebase, while modular architectures separate concerns into independent modules or services.

text: What is the role of a task queue in server-side applications?
options: To queue CSS rendering tasks in the browser
To offload time-consuming tasks to background workers so the main request-response cycle is not blocked
To queue database backup tasks on a schedule
To manage the order of HTML template compilation
explanation: Task queues (like Celery, Bull) offload long-running operations (email sending, image processing) to background workers, keeping the API responsive.

text: What is the difference between thread-based and event-driven server concurrency models?
options: Thread-based is only for Python while event-driven is only for JavaScript
Thread-based assigns a thread per connection while event-driven uses a single thread with non-blocking I/O and an event loop
Event-driven creates more threads than thread-based models
Thread-based models cannot handle concurrent requests
explanation: Thread-based servers (like Apache) allocate a thread per connection, while event-driven servers (like Node.js) use a single thread with non-blocking I/O to handle many concurrent connections.

text: What is the purpose of a request pipeline in server-side frameworks?
options: To create a visual pipeline diagram of server architecture
To process requests through an ordered chain of middleware functions that can modify the request/response or short-circuit the chain
To pipeline multiple database queries into a single request
To pipe server logs to external monitoring services
explanation: The request pipeline processes each request through a series of middleware in order, where each can modify the request/response, perform logic, or pass control to the next middleware.

text: What is the purpose of graceful shutdown in server applications?
options: To shut down the server instantly without any delay
To stop accepting new requests while completing in-flight requests before the server process exits
To gracefully reduce the visual quality of the application
To slowly decrease server memory allocation over time
explanation: Graceful shutdown stops accepting new connections while allowing in-flight requests to complete, preventing data loss and ensuring clean resource cleanup before exit.

text: What is the purpose of distributed tracing in microservices?
options: To trace the origin of CSS style inheritance across components
To track a request's journey across multiple services by propagating trace IDs for debugging and performance analysis
To trace network cable routes in a data center
To trace memory allocation patterns in a single server
explanation: Distributed tracing (using tools like Jaeger or Zipkin) tracks requests across service boundaries with propagated trace IDs, enabling root cause analysis of latency and failures.

text: What is the strangler fig pattern for legacy system migration?
options: A pattern for aggressively deleting legacy code all at once
A gradual migration strategy that incrementally replaces parts of a legacy system with new services while both run in parallel
A pattern for strangling server resources to force downtime
A technique for wrapping legacy CSS in modern framework styles
explanation: The strangler fig pattern incrementally replaces legacy system functionality with new services, routing traffic between old and new systems until the legacy system can be decommissioned.

text: What are edge functions in modern server-side computing?
options: Functions that handle edge cases in application logic
Serverless functions that execute at CDN edge locations close to the user for reduced latency
Functions that process data at the edge of a database table
Functions that run on the client side near the browser edge
explanation: Edge functions are serverless functions deployed at CDN edge locations worldwide, running code close to users for minimal latency, used for routing, personalization, and A/B testing.

text: What is the purpose of health check endpoints in server applications?
options: To check the health of HTML markup for validity
To provide endpoints that load balancers and orchestrators use to verify a service is running and ready to handle requests
To monitor the physical health of server hardware components
To check the health of CSS stylesheets for syntax errors
explanation: Health check endpoints return the operational status of a service, allowing load balancers and container orchestrators to detect failures and route traffic accordingly.

text: What is the purpose of feature flags in server-side applications?
options: Flags that mark CSS features supported by the server browser
Toggles that control feature availability at runtime without deploying new code, enabling gradual rollouts and A/B testing
Flags used to signal errors in server-side JavaScript
Boolean flags stored in HTML meta tags for feature detection
explanation: Feature flags are runtime toggles that enable or disable features without code deployment, supporting gradual rollouts, A/B testing, and quick feature rollbacks.

text: What is the blue-green deployment strategy?
options: A strategy for applying blue and green color themes to the application
A deployment strategy using two identical environments where traffic is switched from the current (blue) to the new (green) version
A method for testing CSS color contrast ratios during deployment
A strategy for deploying to two different cloud providers simultaneously
explanation: Blue-green deployment maintains two identical environments; the new version deploys to the idle one, and traffic is switched over instantly, enabling zero-downtime releases and easy rollback.

text: What is the purpose of canary deployments?
options: To deploy only to servers named after canary islands
To gradually roll out changes to a small subset of users before a full release to detect issues early
To test CSS color changes on a small group of pages
To deploy canary scripts that monitor server health
explanation: Canary deployments release new versions to a small percentage of users first, monitoring for issues before gradually expanding to the full user base.

text: What is SQL?
options: A server-side scripting language for web pages
A standard language for managing and querying relational databases
A CSS selector query language for styling elements
A JavaScript library for creating animations
explanation: SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language used to create, read, update, and delete data in relational databases.

text: What is a primary key in a database table?
options: The first column in any database table
A unique identifier for each record in a table
A password used to access the database
The main CSS class applied to table elements
explanation: A primary key is a column (or combination of columns) that uniquely identifies each row in a database table, ensuring no duplicates.

text: What is MongoDB?
options: A SQL-based relational database system
A NoSQL document-oriented database that stores data in JSON-like documents
A JavaScript framework for building web applications
A CSS preprocessor for managing stylesheets
explanation: MongoDB is a NoSQL document database that stores data as flexible JSON-like documents (BSON), allowing varied structures within the same collection.

text: What is a foreign key in a relational database?
options: A key used to encrypt database connections
A column that references the primary key of another table to establish relationships
A key for accessing foreign database servers
The last column in a database table
explanation: A foreign key is a column that references the primary key of another table, creating a relationship between the two tables.

text: What does the SQL SELECT statement do?
options: It selects which database to connect to
It retrieves data from one or more database tables
It selects CSS styles to apply to a page
It selects which server to deploy code to
explanation: The SELECT statement queries and retrieves specific columns or all data from one or more database tables.

text: What is an index in a database?
options: The first page of the database documentation
A data structure that speeds up data retrieval operations on a table
A numbered list of all database tables
A CSS property for ordering elements
explanation: A database index is a data structure that improves the speed of data retrieval by allowing the database to find rows without scanning the entire table.

text: What is MySQL?
options: A JavaScript testing framework
An open-source relational database management system
A CSS methodology for naming conventions
A Node.js web application framework
explanation: MySQL is a widely-used open-source relational database management system that uses SQL for data management.

text: What is the purpose of the SQL WHERE clause?
options: To specify where to save the query results
To filter records based on a specified condition
To define where on the page to display data
To specify where the database server is located
explanation: The WHERE clause filters rows in a SQL query by applying a condition, returning only the rows that match the criteria.

text: What is PostgreSQL?
options: A frontend JavaScript framework for data visualization
An advanced open-source relational database system known for its extensibility and standards compliance
A CSS postprocessor for optimizing stylesheets
A JavaScript library for creating SQL queries
explanation: PostgreSQL is a powerful open-source relational database system known for its advanced features, extensibility, and strong SQL standards compliance.

text: What is a database schema?
options: A visual color scheme for database administration tools
The structure that defines tables, columns, data types, and relationships in a database
A CSS layout scheme for database-driven web pages
A JavaScript scheme for validating form data
explanation: A database schema defines the logical structure of a database including its tables, columns, data types, constraints, and relationships.

text: What is database normalization?
options: The process of converting all data to a single format
The process of organizing tables to reduce data redundancy and improve data integrity through normal forms
The process of normalizing query response times
The technique of standardizing database server configurations
explanation: Database normalization organizes tables according to normal forms (1NF, 2NF, 3NF) to eliminate redundant data and ensure data dependencies make sense.

text: What is the difference between INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN in SQL?
options: INNER JOIN returns all rows from both tables while LEFT JOIN only returns matching rows
INNER JOIN returns only matching rows from both tables while LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table plus matches from the right
LEFT JOIN is faster than INNER JOIN in all scenarios
INNER JOIN works with NoSQL while LEFT JOIN only works with SQL
explanation: INNER JOIN returns only rows with matching values in both tables, while LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right (with NULLs for non-matches).

text: What is Redis commonly used for in web applications?
options: As a primary relational database for storing user data
As an in-memory data store for caching, session management, and real-time features
As a CSS preprocessor for generating dynamic styles
As a frontend framework for building reactive interfaces
explanation: Redis is an in-memory key-value data store commonly used for caching, session storage, rate limiting, pub/sub messaging, and real-time leaderboards.

text: What is an ORM migration?
options: Moving an ORM library from one programming language to another
A versioned script that describes changes to the database schema, allowing controlled evolution of the data model
Migrating data between different ORM libraries
Converting raw SQL queries to ORM query syntax
explanation: ORM migrations are versioned scripts that define incremental database schema changes (create/alter tables), enabling teams to track and apply schema changes consistently.

text: What is the purpose of database transactions?
options: To track financial transactions in e-commerce applications
To group multiple database operations into an atomic unit that either all succeed or all fail
To transfer data between different database servers
To translate SQL queries between different database dialects
explanation: Database transactions group multiple operations into an atomic unit, ensuring that either all operations succeed (commit) or none take effect (rollback).

text: What is the difference between embedding and referencing in MongoDB?
options: Embedding uses foreign keys while referencing stores data inline
Embedding stores related data within the same document while referencing stores a reference (ID) to data in another collection
Referencing is faster than embedding for all query types
Embedding only works with string data types
explanation: Embedding nests related data directly within a document for fast reads, while referencing stores IDs pointing to separate documents, normalizing data like in relational databases.

text: What is the purpose of the GROUP BY clause in SQL?
options: To group CSS styles by their selector type
To group rows that share a value in specified columns so aggregate functions can be applied to each group
To group multiple SQL statements into a single transaction
To organize database tables into groups for administration
explanation: GROUP BY groups rows with identical values in specified columns into summary rows, enabling aggregate functions like COUNT, SUM, and AVG to operate on each group.

text: What is Prisma in modern web development?
options: A CSS library for creating prismatic color effects
A next-generation ORM for Node.js and TypeScript that provides type-safe database access
A JavaScript testing framework for database queries
A database migration tool exclusive to PostgreSQL
explanation: Prisma is a modern ORM for Node.js and TypeScript that provides a type-safe query API, schema definition language, and migration system for database management.

text: What is a database view?
options: A graphical interface for viewing database tables
A virtual table created by a stored SQL query that presents data from one or more tables
A CSS view for styling database-generated HTML
A user permission level for viewing database records
explanation: A database view is a virtual table defined by a SQL query that presents data from underlying tables, simplifying complex queries and providing a layer of abstraction.

text: What is the purpose of database seeding?
options: Planting physical storage devices in a server room
Populating a database with initial or test data for development, testing, or application setup
Creating seed backups of the database for disaster recovery
Seeding random values into database encryption algorithms
explanation: Database seeding populates tables with predefined initial or test data, used for development environments, testing, or providing default application data.

text: What is database sharding?
options: Breaking a database into fragments after a critical failure
Distributing data across multiple database instances by splitting rows based on a shard key for horizontal scalability
Creating read-only copies of a database for backup
Compressing database files into smaller shards for storage
explanation: Database sharding horizontally partitions data across multiple database instances using a shard key, enabling the database to scale beyond the limits of a single server.

text: What is a write-ahead log (WAL) in databases?
options: A log file that records all write operations before they are confirmed to the user
A technique where changes are first written to a log before being applied to data files, ensuring crash recovery and durability
A log that documents planned database schema changes ahead of time
A journal of SQL write statements for audit compliance
explanation: WAL records all modifications to a log file before they are applied to the actual data files, ensuring that committed transactions can be recovered after a crash.

text: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking in databases?
options: Optimistic locking uses exclusive locks while pessimistic uses shared locks
Optimistic locking checks for conflicts at update time using version numbers, while pessimistic locking acquires locks before reading data
Pessimistic locking is always faster than optimistic locking
Optimistic locking works only with NoSQL databases
explanation: Optimistic locking allows concurrent reads and detects conflicts using version numbers at write time, while pessimistic locking acquires exclusive locks upfront to prevent conflicts.

text: What is a materialized view in databases?
options: A view that displays material design styles for database tables
A precomputed view that stores query results physically and can be refreshed periodically, unlike regular views that execute queries on access
A view that materializes only when a specific user logs in
A virtual view that is computed in real-time with each access
explanation: A materialized view physically stores the result of a query, providing faster access at the cost of storage and requiring periodic refreshes to stay current.

text: What is the purpose of database replication?
options: To replicate the database UI across different browsers
To maintain copies of data on multiple servers for high availability, fault tolerance, and read scaling
To duplicate database queries for performance testing
To replicate CSS styles across multiple database views
explanation: Database replication maintains synchronized copies of data on multiple servers, providing high availability, disaster recovery, and distributing read queries across replicas.

text: What is a CTE (Common Table Expression) in SQL?
options: A CSS template expression for database-driven styles
A named temporary result set defined within a WITH clause that can be referenced within the same query
A client-side table element for rendering database data
A connection timeout expression for database queries
explanation: A CTE is a named temporary result set defined with WITH that exists only for the duration of a single query, improving readability and enabling recursive queries.

text: What is the difference between eventual consistency and strong consistency in distributed databases?
options: Eventual consistency is always faster than strong consistency
Strong consistency guarantees all reads return the latest write immediately, while eventual consistency allows temporary stale reads that converge over time
Strong consistency only works with NoSQL databases
Eventual consistency prevents any writes during synchronization
explanation: Strong consistency ensures every read reflects the most recent write, while eventual consistency allows temporary inconsistency between replicas that converges given time.

text: What is a database connection pool leak?
options: A security vulnerability where database credentials are exposed
A situation where connections are borrowed from the pool but not returned, eventually exhausting available connections
A network leak where database packets are lost during transmission
A memory leak caused by storing too much data in the database
explanation: A connection pool leak occurs when application code borrows database connections but fails to return them (often due to missing error handling), eventually exhausting the pool.

text: What is a query execution plan in databases?
options: A plan for scheduling database queries throughout the day
The strategy the database optimizer chooses to execute a query, including index usage, join methods, and scan types
A written plan for developing new SQL queries
A deployment plan for rolling out database changes
explanation: A query execution plan shows how the database engine will execute a query, revealing index usage, join algorithms, and data access methods for performance optimization.

text: What is the purpose of a database cursor?
options: A visual cursor for navigating database admin interfaces
A database object that enables row-by-row processing of a query result set, useful when set-based operations are insufficient
A CSS cursor style applied to database-driven web elements
A pointer to the current position in a database file on disk
explanation: A database cursor allows row-by-row traversal and processing of a result set, used when complex per-row logic is needed beyond what set-based SQL operations can handle.

text: What is Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)?
options: A technique for sharing scripts between different websites
A vulnerability where attackers inject malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users
A method for cross-referencing JavaScript files across servers
A tool for debugging scripts across multiple browsers
explanation: XSS is a security vulnerability where malicious scripts are injected into trusted websites, executing in victims' browsers to steal data or perform unauthorized actions.

text: What is SQL injection?
options: A technique for inserting SQL databases into web pages
An attack where malicious SQL code is inserted into input fields to manipulate database queries
A method for injecting CSS styles into SQL queries
A tool for automatically generating SQL insert statements
explanation: SQL injection occurs when untrusted input is included in SQL queries without proper sanitization, allowing attackers to read, modify, or delete database data.

text: What is HTTPS and why is it important?
options: A faster version of HTTP that improves page load speed
HTTP with TLS encryption that protects data transmitted between browser and server
A protocol for transferring files between servers securely
A HTTP method for sending secure form data to APIs
explanation: HTTPS uses TLS (Transport Layer Security) to encrypt data between the browser and server, preventing eavesdropping and tampering.

text: What is a strong password policy?
options: A policy requiring passwords to be exactly 4 characters
Rules requiring passwords to meet minimum length, complexity, and uniqueness requirements
A policy that stores passwords in plain text for easy recovery
A policy allowing the same password for all user accounts
explanation: A strong password policy enforces minimum length, character variety (uppercase, lowercase, numbers, symbols), and uniqueness to resist brute-force attacks.

text: What is two-factor authentication (2FA)?
options: Using two different passwords to log in to an account
A security method requiring two distinct forms of verification to access an account
Having two administrators approve every login attempt
Logging in from two different devices simultaneously
explanation: Two-factor authentication requires two different verification methods (like a password plus a phone code) to confirm a user's identity, adding an extra security layer.

text: What is the purpose of input sanitization?
options: To clean up CSS input styles for consistent rendering
To remove or escape potentially harmful characters from user input before processing
To sanitize the visual appearance of input form fields
To compress input data for faster network transmission
explanation: Input sanitization removes or escapes dangerous characters from user input, preventing injection attacks like XSS and SQL injection.

text: What is a firewall in web security?
options: A CSS animation effect that looks like fire
A network security system that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing traffic based on rules
A JavaScript library for form validation
A browser feature that blocks pop-up windows
explanation: A firewall is a security system that monitors and filters network traffic based on predetermined rules, blocking unauthorized access while permitting legitimate communication.

text: What is the purpose of SSL/TLS certificates?
options: To speed up website loading times
To authenticate a website's identity and enable encrypted connections
To certify that a website's CSS is valid
To license the use of specific JavaScript libraries
explanation: SSL/TLS certificates verify the identity of a website and enable encrypted HTTPS connections, ensuring data confidentiality between client and server.

text: What is phishing in cybersecurity?
options: A technique for testing website performance under load
A social engineering attack that tricks users into revealing sensitive information through fake websites or emails
A method for finding and fixing JavaScript bugs
A fishing game embedded in web applications
explanation: Phishing uses deceptive emails, websites, or messages that impersonate legitimate entities to trick users into revealing passwords, credit cards, or other sensitive data.

text: What is the principle of least privilege?
options: Giving all users administrator access for convenience
Granting users only the minimum permissions necessary to perform their tasks
Restricting access to the website during off-peak hours
Using the smallest possible database for each application
explanation: The principle of least privilege restricts users, accounts, and processes to only the minimum access rights needed for their legitimate purpose, limiting potential damage.

text: What is Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)?
options: A technique for forging CSS styles across different websites
An attack that tricks authenticated users into submitting unwanted requests to a site where they are logged in
A method for forging HTTP request headers for testing
A browser bug that sends duplicate requests across tabs
explanation: CSRF exploits a user's authenticated session by tricking their browser into sending unauthorized requests to a target site, often via malicious links or hidden forms.

text: What is the purpose of CSRF tokens?
options: To encrypt CSS files during server-side rendering
To include a unique, unpredictable token with forms that the server validates to prevent forged cross-site requests
To tokenize URL parameters for SEO optimization
To generate unique tokens for caching static assets
explanation: CSRF tokens are unique, unpredictable values embedded in forms and validated server-side, ensuring that requests originate from the legitimate application.

text: What is the difference between stored and reflected XSS?
options: Stored XSS reflects off servers while reflected XSS is stored in databases
Stored XSS persists malicious scripts in the database, while reflected XSS includes the script in the URL and reflects it back in the response
Reflected XSS is more dangerous than stored XSS
Stored XSS only affects the attacker while reflected XSS affects all users
explanation: Stored XSS saves malicious scripts in the database (affecting all users who view the data), while reflected XSS embeds scripts in URLs that execute when the link is clicked.

text: What is the Same-Origin Policy?
options: A policy requiring all scripts to originate from the same developer
A browser security mechanism that restricts scripts from accessing resources from a different origin (protocol, domain, or port)
A server policy that only allows requests from the same IP address
A CSS policy that prevents style inheritance across different files
explanation: The Same-Origin Policy prevents scripts from one origin from accessing resources of another origin (different protocol, domain, or port), protecting against cross-site attacks.

text: What is the purpose of the HttpOnly flag on cookies?
options: To make cookies only transmit over HTTP, not HTTPS
To prevent client-side JavaScript from accessing the cookie, mitigating XSS-based cookie theft
To allow the cookie only for HTTP GET requests
To restrict the cookie to HTTP/1.1 connections only
explanation: The HttpOnly flag prevents JavaScript from accessing the cookie via document.cookie, protecting session tokens from being stolen through XSS attacks.

text: What is a brute force attack?
options: An attack that physically damages server hardware
An attack that systematically tries all possible passwords or keys until the correct one is found
An attack that overwhelms a server with excessive network traffic
An attack that exploits known software vulnerabilities directly
explanation: A brute force attack tries every possible combination of passwords or encryption keys until finding the correct one, countered by rate limiting and account lockouts.

text: What is the purpose of the Secure flag on cookies?
options: To encrypt the cookie value using AES encryption
To ensure the cookie is only sent over encrypted HTTPS connections
To make the cookie secure from all types of web attacks
To require user authentication before the cookie is set
explanation: The Secure flag ensures that the cookie is only transmitted over HTTPS connections, preventing it from being intercepted over unencrypted HTTP.

text: What is the purpose of parameterized queries?
options: To add CSS parameters to HTML query selectors
To separate SQL code from user data, preventing SQL injection by treating input as values rather than executable code
To parameterize HTTP query strings for SEO optimization
To define parameters for JavaScript callback functions
explanation: Parameterized queries (prepared statements) separate SQL logic from data values, ensuring user input is treated as data and never as executable SQL code.

text: What is a DDoS attack?
options: A database denial of service that corrupts stored data
A Distributed Denial of Service attack that overwhelms a server with traffic from multiple sources to make it unavailable
A debugging tool that detects denial-of-service vulnerabilities
A technique for distributing server load across data centers
explanation: A DDoS attack floods a target with traffic from many distributed sources simultaneously, overwhelming the server's capacity and making it unavailable to legitimate users.

text: What is the SameSite cookie attribute?
options: An attribute that ensures cookies work on the same website design
An attribute that controls whether cookies are sent with cross-site requests, helping prevent CSRF attacks
An attribute that limits cookies to the same browser session
An attribute that restricts cookies to same-site CSS resources
explanation: The SameSite attribute restricts when cookies are sent with cross-site requests, with values Strict, Lax, and None, providing built-in CSRF protection.

text: What is a Content Security Policy (CSP) nonce?
options: A one-time CSS class applied to content elements
A unique random value added to script/style tags and the CSP header to allow only specifically tagged inline scripts to execute
A numerical once-per-session token for API authentication
A nonce used to encrypt content before transmission
explanation: A CSP nonce is a unique random value added to both the CSP header and allowed inline script tags, ensuring only scripts with the matching nonce execute, preventing XSS.

text: What is Subresource Integrity (SRI)?
options: A method for validating the structural integrity of HTML elements
A security feature that verifies fetched resources match an expected hash, ensuring CDN-hosted files have not been tampered with
A technique for maintaining the integrity of CSS subselectors
A database integrity check for nested data resources
explanation: SRI uses cryptographic hashes in the integrity attribute of script/link tags to verify that resources fetched from CDNs have not been modified or compromised.

text: What is the purpose of the Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header?
options: To enforce strict CSS transport animations between page loads
To instruct browsers to only connect to the site over HTTPS for a specified duration, preventing protocol downgrade attacks
To enforce strict data types in form inputs during transport
To restrict the transport of large files to dedicated servers
explanation: HSTS tells browsers to only communicate with the server over HTTPS for a set period, preventing SSL stripping and protocol downgrade attacks.

text: What is OAuth 2.0?
options: A CSS framework for creating authentication forms
An authorization framework that enables third-party applications to access user resources without sharing passwords through delegated tokens
A JavaScript library for handling browser authentication
A database authentication protocol for SQL connections
explanation: OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework where users grant third-party apps limited access to their resources via access tokens, without sharing their credentials.

text: What is a JSON Web Token (JWT) structure?
options: A single encrypted string containing user credentials
Three Base64URL-encoded parts (header, payload, signature) separated by dots, where the signature verifies integrity
A JSON file containing token configuration settings
Two parts consisting of a username and encrypted password
explanation: A JWT consists of three Base64URL-encoded parts separated by dots: header (algorithm), payload (claims), and signature (integrity verification).

text: What is the purpose of security headers like X-Content-Type-Options?
options: To set the content type of CSS stylesheets
To prevent browsers from MIME-sniffing responses away from the declared content type, mitigating content-type-based attacks
To define which content types are supported by the API
To optimize content delivery based on file type detection
explanation: X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff prevents browsers from overriding the server-declared content type, stopping attacks that exploit MIME type confusion.

text: What is the purpose of rate limiting with token bucket algorithm?
options: To limit the rate of CSS token processing in the browser
To control request rates by allowing bursts up to a bucket capacity while refilling tokens at a steady rate
To limit the number of JWT tokens issued per user
To throttle database token generation for encryption
explanation: The token bucket algorithm allows short bursts of requests up to the bucket capacity while tokens are added at a fixed rate, providing flexible rate limiting.

text: What is a zero-day vulnerability?
options: A vulnerability that takes zero days to patch after discovery
A previously unknown security flaw that is exploited before the vendor has released a fix
A vulnerability that only exists on the first day of deployment
A security flaw that affects exactly zero users
explanation: A zero-day vulnerability is a security flaw unknown to the vendor being actively exploited before a patch is available, giving defenders zero days to prepare.

text: What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption?
options: Symmetric encryption is for files while asymmetric is for network traffic
Symmetric encryption uses one shared key for both encryption and decryption, while asymmetric uses a public-private key pair
Asymmetric encryption is always faster than symmetric encryption
Symmetric encryption uses two keys while asymmetric uses one key
explanation: Symmetric encryption uses a single shared secret key for both operations, while asymmetric encryption uses a public key for encryption and a private key for decryption.

text: What is the purpose of a Web Application Firewall (WAF)?
options: A firewall that blocks all traffic to web applications
A security layer that filters, monitors, and blocks malicious HTTP/S traffic to and from web applications based on configurable rules
A frontend framework for building firewall configuration pages
A tool for testing web application performance under load
explanation: A WAF sits between clients and the web application, inspecting HTTP traffic and blocking attacks like SQL injection, XSS, and other OWASP Top 10 threats based on rules.

text: What is page load time?
options: The time it takes to write the HTML code for a page
The total time from initiating a page request to the page being fully rendered in the browser
The time the server takes to compile JavaScript files
The time required to upload a page to the web server
explanation: Page load time measures the duration from when a user requests a page to when it is fully rendered and interactive in the browser.

text: What is image optimization in web performance?
options: Adding more images to make a website look better
Reducing image file sizes through compression, proper formats, and sizing without significant quality loss
Optimizing the position of images on a web page
Using only vector graphics on every web page
explanation: Image optimization reduces file sizes through compression, format selection (WebP, AVIF), and responsive sizing to improve page load times.

text: What is browser caching?
options: A process for caching JavaScript code during compilation
Storing copies of web resources locally in the browser to avoid re-downloading them on subsequent visits
A technique for caching database queries in the browser
A method for storing user passwords in the browser
explanation: Browser caching stores copies of static resources (CSS, JS, images) locally so they do not need to be re-downloaded on subsequent page visits.

text: What is minification used for in web performance?
options: To minimize the number of pages on a website
To remove unnecessary characters from code files to reduce their size
To minimize the number of colors used in a design
To reduce the number of database queries per page
explanation: Minification removes whitespace, comments, and shortens variable names in CSS, JavaScript, and HTML files to reduce file sizes and improve load times.

text: What is lazy loading?
options: Loading a website slowly on purpose for testing
Deferring the loading of non-critical resources until they are needed, like images below the fold
A CSS technique for slow fade-in animations
Loading all resources at once to avoid future delays
explanation: Lazy loading defers loading of off-screen or non-critical resources until they are needed, reducing initial page load time and bandwidth usage.

text: What is the purpose of using a CDN for performance?
options: To create duplicate versions of a website
To serve content from servers geographically close to users, reducing latency
To compress all database queries for faster execution
To convert images to text for faster rendering
explanation: A CDN caches content on servers distributed worldwide, serving assets from the location nearest to each user to minimize latency and improve load times.

text: What is Gzip compression?
options: A JavaScript library for compressing images
A file compression method that reduces the size of HTTP responses sent from server to browser
A technique for compressing CSS selectors into shorter names
A database compression algorithm for reducing storage
explanation: Gzip is a compression algorithm that reduces the size of text-based HTTP responses (HTML, CSS, JS) during transfer, typically achieving 60-80% size reduction.

text: What are Core Web Vitals?
options: The core HTML elements required for a valid web page
A set of Google metrics (LCP, INP, CLS) that measure real-world user experience for loading, interactivity, and visual stability
The essential CSS properties for building responsive layouts
The fundamental JavaScript functions built into browsers
explanation: Core Web Vitals are three Google metrics: Largest Contentful Paint (loading), Interaction to Next Paint (interactivity), and Cumulative Layout Shift (visual stability).

text: What is code splitting?
options: Splitting code between frontend and backend teams
Breaking JavaScript bundles into smaller chunks that are loaded on demand
Dividing CSS code into separate files by color theme
Splitting a single database into multiple databases
explanation: Code splitting breaks large JavaScript bundles into smaller chunks loaded on demand, reducing the initial download size and improving page load performance.

text: What is the purpose of the loading='lazy' attribute on images?
options: To make images load with a slow fade-in animation
To defer loading the image until it is near the viewport, saving bandwidth
To load images in a lower quality format first
To delay all images until JavaScript has fully loaded
explanation: The loading='lazy' attribute tells the browser to defer loading the image until it is about to scroll into the viewport, reducing initial page load.

text: What is the Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) metric?
options: The total time to download the largest JavaScript file
The time it takes for the largest visible content element (image or text block) to render in the viewport
The number of CSS paint operations performed during page load
The size in pixels of the largest element on the page
explanation: LCP measures when the largest visible content element (image, video, or text block) in the viewport finishes rendering, indicating perceived load speed.

text: What is the Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) metric?
options: The total number of CSS layout changes during page load
A metric that measures unexpected visual movement of elements during a page's lifetime
The cumulative time spent computing CSS layouts by the browser
The shift in page dimensions when switching between portrait and landscape
explanation: CLS quantifies unexpected layout shifts where visible elements move from their expected position, caused by late-loading images, ads, or dynamic content insertion.

text: What is the purpose of prefetching resources?
options: To fetch resources before the server is ready to serve them
To download resources the user is likely to need next in the background during idle time
To pre-download all CSS files before any HTML is parsed
To fetch data from a backup database before the primary
explanation: Prefetching downloads resources likely needed for future navigations during browser idle time, using link rel='prefetch', making subsequent page loads faster.

text: What is the difference between preload and prefetch?
options: Preload and prefetch are identical browser hints with no differences
Preload fetches resources needed for the current page with high priority, while prefetch downloads resources for future navigations with low priority
Prefetch has higher priority than preload for resource loading
Preload only works with JavaScript files while prefetch works with all files
explanation: Preload (rel='preload') fetches critical resources for the current page at high priority, while prefetch (rel='prefetch') downloads resources for likely future navigations at low priority.

text: What is render-blocking and how does it affect performance?
options: A CSS technique for blocking elements from rendering on screen
Resources like CSS and synchronous JavaScript that prevent the page from rendering until they are fully loaded and processed
A server-side block on rendering certain page content
A browser security feature that blocks rendering of unsafe content
explanation: Render-blocking resources (CSS, synchronous JS) must be fully downloaded and processed before the browser can render the page, delaying First Contentful Paint.

text: What are the benefits of using WebP or AVIF image formats?
options: They provide higher resolution than PNG and JPEG images
They offer better compression ratios with comparable or superior quality, resulting in smaller file sizes
They are the only formats supported by modern browsers
They automatically resize images based on the viewport
explanation: WebP and AVIF provide superior compression compared to JPEG and PNG, delivering comparable visual quality at significantly smaller file sizes for faster loading.

text: What is debouncing in the context of web performance?
options: A technique for removing bounce effects from CSS animations
A technique that delays executing a function until a specified time has passed since the last invocation
A method for detecting and fixing bounced email addresses
A process for reducing server bounce rates in analytics
explanation: Debouncing delays function execution until a pause in rapid invocations (e.g., keystrokes), reducing unnecessary computation and API calls.

text: What is throttling in web performance optimization?
options: Reducing server bandwidth to limit user access
Limiting a function to execute at most once per specified time interval regardless of how often it is called
Slowing down CSS animations for smoother playback
Throttling database queries to prevent server overload
explanation: Throttling ensures a function executes at most once per specified interval, useful for scroll or resize handlers to maintain a consistent execution rate.

text: What is the purpose of the requestAnimationFrame API?
options: To request new animation assets from the server
To schedule visual updates to run before the next browser repaint for smooth 60fps animations
To animate HTML form elements on submission
To request permission to play animated GIFs
explanation: requestAnimationFrame schedules a callback to run before the next browser repaint, enabling smooth animations synchronized with the display refresh rate.

text: What is Time to First Byte (TTFB)?
options: The time it takes to write the first byte of code
The time between the browser sending a request and receiving the first byte of the server's response
The time to download the first JavaScript file
The total time for the browser to render the first pixel
explanation: TTFB measures the time from the browser's request to receiving the first byte of the response, reflecting server processing speed and network latency.

text: What is the purpose of the Performance Observer API?
options: To observe and grade the performance of CSS animations
To asynchronously observe performance measurement entries like paint timing, resource loading, and long tasks
To observe server performance metrics from the client
To monitor the performance of database queries in real time
explanation: The Performance Observer API asynchronously listens for performance entries (paint timing, resource loading, long tasks), enabling real-user monitoring without polling.

text: What is the difference between HTTP caching headers Cache-Control and ETag?
options: Cache-Control is for caching HTML while ETag is for caching JavaScript
Cache-Control defines caching policies (max-age, no-cache) while ETag provides a version identifier for conditional revalidation
ETag replaces Cache-Control in HTTP/2 and is no longer needed
Cache-Control and ETag serve the same purpose and are interchangeable
explanation: Cache-Control sets caching directives (duration, behavior), while ETag is a version identifier that enables conditional requests (If-None-Match) for efficient revalidation.

text: What is the purpose of resource hints like preconnect?
options: To connect CSS resources to JavaScript modules
To establish early connections (DNS, TCP, TLS) to important third-party origins before requests are made
To preconnect database instances before queries are sent
To connect multiple CDN nodes together for load balancing
explanation: link rel='preconnect' tells the browser to establish DNS, TCP, and TLS connections to specified origins early, eliminating connection overhead when resources are requested.

text: What is the PRPL pattern in web performance?
options: A CSS color pattern using purple gradients for design
A pattern of Push critical resources, Render initial route, Pre-cache remaining routes, and Lazy-load remaining routes
A server-side pattern for processing, routing, parsing, and logging
A testing pattern for Performance, Reliability, Portability, and Latency
explanation: PRPL stands for Push/Preload critical resources, Render the initial route quickly, Pre-cache remaining assets, and Lazy-load additional routes on demand.

text: What is the purpose of the Long Tasks API?
options: To track long-running database queries from the browser
To detect JavaScript tasks that block the main thread for more than 50 milliseconds, impacting responsiveness
To manage long-running CSS animation sequences
To schedule tasks that need to run for extended periods
explanation: The Long Tasks API reports when JavaScript tasks exceed 50ms on the main thread, helping identify performance bottlenecks that cause jank and poor interactivity.

text: What is the impact of layout thrashing on web performance?
options: It causes CSS layouts to break across different screen sizes
It occurs when JavaScript reads and writes DOM layout properties in a loop, forcing the browser to recalculate layout repeatedly
It increases the file size of HTML layout templates
It thrashes the server's memory when rendering complex pages
explanation: Layout thrashing occurs when JavaScript alternates between reading and writing layout properties, forcing synchronous layout recalculations (reflows) that degrade performance.

text: What is the purpose of the 'content-visibility: auto' CSS property for performance?
options: To automatically show or hide content based on user permissions
To skip rendering of off-screen content, allowing the browser to avoid layout, style, and paint work until the element scrolls into view
To automatically adjust content visibility based on screen brightness
To make content visible only to search engine crawlers
explanation: content-visibility: auto enables the browser to skip rendering work for off-screen elements, substantially improving initial rendering performance for long pages.

text: What is the difference between client-side and server-side caching strategies?
options: Client-side caching is always more effective than server-side caching
Client-side caching stores responses in the browser using HTTP headers, while server-side caching stores computed data in memory (like Redis) to reduce database load
Server-side caching only works with static files
Client-side caching is controlled by the server while server-side caching is controlled by the browser
explanation: Client-side caching uses HTTP headers (Cache-Control, ETag) to store responses in the browser, while server-side caching uses in-memory stores like Redis to cache computed data.

text: What is Brotli compression and how does it compare to Gzip?
options: Brotli is a CSS compression technique while Gzip compresses JavaScript
Brotli is a newer compression algorithm that typically achieves 15-25% better compression ratios than Gzip for text-based web assets
Gzip always produces smaller files than Brotli compression
Brotli and Gzip use the same compression algorithm with different names
explanation: Brotli is a modern compression algorithm by Google that typically achieves 15-25% better compression than Gzip for web assets, though with slower compression speed.

text: What is the Interaction to Next Paint (INP) metric?
options: The time between two consecutive CSS paint events
A Core Web Vital that measures the latency of all user interactions throughout the page lifecycle, representing overall responsiveness
The time between the first and last paint during page load
A metric measuring how quickly new content is painted after scrolling
explanation: INP measures the worst interaction latency throughout a page visit (excluding outliers), representing how responsive the page is to user inputs like clicks, taps, and key presses.

text: What is unit testing in web development?
options: Testing an entire web application as a single unit
Testing individual functions or components in isolation to verify they work correctly
Testing the physical server hardware units
Testing the visual design of individual page sections
explanation: Unit testing verifies that individual functions, methods, or components produce correct output for given inputs, tested in isolation from other parts of the system.

text: What is a test case?
options: A physical case that stores test equipment
A set of conditions and expected results used to verify a specific aspect of the software
A CSS class applied during testing mode
A folder structure for organizing test files
explanation: A test case defines specific inputs, execution conditions, and expected results to verify that a particular feature or function works as intended.

text: What is Jest in JavaScript testing?
options: A CSS animation library for testing visual effects
A JavaScript testing framework developed by Meta for unit and integration testing
A JavaScript library for creating mock servers
A browser extension for manual testing workflows
explanation: Jest is a popular JavaScript testing framework by Meta that provides test running, assertions, mocking, and code coverage out of the box.

text: What is an assertion in testing?
options: A declaration of a new test variable
A statement that checks whether an expected condition is true in the test output
A method for asserting control over test execution order
A comment explaining the purpose of a test file
explanation: An assertion is a statement in a test that verifies an expected condition, such as expect(result).toBe(5), failing the test if the condition is not met.

text: What is end-to-end (E2E) testing?
options: Testing only the first and last lines of code in a file
Testing the complete application flow from user interaction through all system layers
Testing CSS from the first rule to the last rule
Testing the start and end points of API endpoints
explanation: E2E testing simulates real user scenarios by testing the entire application flow, including frontend interactions, API calls, and database operations.

text: What is a mock in testing?
options: A fake UI design used for prototyping
A simulated object that replaces a real dependency to isolate the code being tested
A copy of the production database for testing
A draft version of a test case before finalization
explanation: A mock is a simulated object or function that replaces a real dependency, allowing tests to control behavior and verify interactions in isolation.

text: What is integration testing?
options: Testing the integration of CSS with HTML files
Testing how multiple units or modules work together as a combined group
Testing the process of integrating new team members
Testing the installation of software dependencies
explanation: Integration testing verifies that multiple components, modules, or services work correctly together, catching issues that unit tests might miss.

text: What is code coverage in testing?
options: The percentage of CSS covering the visible page area
A metric showing what percentage of the codebase is executed by tests
The number of comments covering the code documentation
The extent to which code covers all user requirements
explanation: Code coverage measures the percentage of code (lines, branches, functions) that is executed during test runs, indicating how thoroughly the code is tested.

text: What is Cypress used for?
options: A CSS preprocessor for generating tree-shaped layouts
A JavaScript-based end-to-end testing framework for web applications
A database testing tool for SQL query validation
A server monitoring tool for tracking uptime
explanation: Cypress is a modern E2E testing framework that runs tests directly in the browser, providing fast, reliable testing with automatic waiting and time-travel debugging.

text: What is regression testing?
options: Testing to revert code to a previous version
Re-running tests after code changes to ensure existing functionality still works correctly
Testing that reverts the application to an earlier state
Testing outdated features that are scheduled for removal
explanation: Regression testing re-runs existing tests after code changes to verify that previously working features have not been broken by new modifications.

text: What is Test-Driven Development (TDD)?
options: Developing tests after the entire application is completed
A development approach where tests are written before the implementation code, following a red-green-refactor cycle
Testing only the parts of code driven by user requirements
A method for driving automated test execution on servers
explanation: TDD follows a cycle: write a failing test (red), write minimal code to pass (green), then refactor, ensuring code is always covered by tests.

text: What is the difference between a stub and a mock in testing?
options: Stubs and mocks are identical testing constructs with no differences
A stub provides predefined responses to calls, while a mock additionally verifies that specific interactions occurred
A mock provides data while a stub verifies behavior
Stubs are for unit tests while mocks are for integration tests
explanation: Stubs provide canned responses without verifying interactions, while mocks also set expectations about how they should be called and fail tests if those expectations are not met.

text: What is snapshot testing?
options: Taking screenshots of the application during tests
Capturing the rendered output of a component and comparing it against a stored reference to detect unintended changes
Testing that snapshot backups of databases are restorable
Comparing before and after snapshots of server configurations
explanation: Snapshot testing captures a serialized representation of a component's output and compares it to a stored snapshot, flagging any unexpected changes.

text: What is the testing pyramid?
options: A physical pyramid-shaped testing device for hardware
A strategy recommending many unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests in the middle, and fewest E2E tests at the top
A ranking system for testing frameworks from best to worst
A visual representation of test coverage percentages
explanation: The testing pyramid recommends a large base of fast unit tests, a middle layer of integration tests, and a small top of slow E2E tests for optimal cost-effectiveness.

text: What is the purpose of a test fixture?
options: A physical fixture for mounting test servers
A predefined state or data setup used to establish consistent conditions for test execution
A CSS fixture for testing visual layouts
A fixed schedule for running automated tests
explanation: A test fixture sets up a known, consistent state (data, configuration, objects) before tests run, ensuring reproducible and reliable test results.

text: What is React Testing Library's philosophy?
options: To test React components by inspecting their internal state and methods
To test components the way users interact with them, querying by accessible roles and text rather than implementation details
To generate random test data for React component stress testing
To automatically test all React components without writing test code
explanation: React Testing Library encourages testing components based on user behavior, querying elements by role, label, and text rather than internal implementation details.

text: What is the purpose of a CI/CD pipeline in testing?
options: To create and design CSS interface components
To automatically run tests on every code change and deploy the application if all tests pass
To compile and install CSS dependencies continuously
To continuously integrate CSS changes with JavaScript files
explanation: CI/CD pipelines automatically build, test, and deploy code on every commit, catching bugs early and ensuring only tested code reaches production.

text: What is visual regression testing?
options: Testing CSS for visual regressions in color accuracy
Comparing screenshots of UI components against baselines to detect unintended visual changes
Testing that visual designs match the original wireframes
Regressing visual effects to simpler versions for performance
explanation: Visual regression testing captures screenshots of components or pages and compares them pixel-by-pixel against stored baselines to detect unintended visual changes.

text: What is API testing?
options: Testing the visual appearance of API documentation pages
Validating that API endpoints return correct responses, status codes, and handle edge cases properly
Testing the speed of API abbreviation parsing
Testing CSS APIs for browser compatibility
explanation: API testing validates endpoints for correct responses, status codes, error handling, authentication, and performance without testing the UI layer.

text: What is the purpose of test doubles in testing?
options: To run each test twice for verification
To substitute real dependencies with controlled implementations (mocks, stubs, fakes, spies) during testing
To duplicate test files for backup purposes
To test both positive and negative scenarios in pairs
explanation: Test doubles (mocks, stubs, fakes, spies, dummies) replace real dependencies with controlled alternatives, isolating the system under test.

text: What is mutation testing?
options: Testing how the application handles data mutations in state management
A technique that introduces small changes (mutations) to the source code to verify that existing tests catch the errors
Testing database mutation operations like INSERT and UPDATE
Testing the mutation of CSS variables during runtime
explanation: Mutation testing injects small faults (mutants) into the code and checks if tests detect them, measuring test suite effectiveness beyond simple code coverage.

text: What is property-based testing?
options: Testing CSS properties for correct rendering across browsers
A testing approach that verifies general properties hold for a wide range of automatically generated inputs rather than specific examples
Testing object properties in JavaScript for correct types
Testing database property constraints for validation
explanation: Property-based testing (using tools like fast-check) generates random inputs to verify that invariants and properties hold across many cases, finding edge cases manual tests miss.

text: What is contract testing in microservices?
options: Testing legal contracts before signing agreements with clients
Verifying that service interactions conform to agreed-upon API contracts between consumer and provider
Testing smart contracts deployed on blockchain networks
Validating employment contracts in HR management systems
explanation: Contract testing (using tools like Pact) verifies that API consumers and providers adhere to a shared contract, catching breaking changes without full integration testing.

text: What is chaos engineering in the context of testing?
options: A methodology for creating chaotic, disorganized test suites
A practice of deliberately injecting failures into systems to test their resilience and discover weaknesses
A random approach to writing tests without structure or planning
A testing technique that randomly changes test assertions
explanation: Chaos engineering intentionally introduces failures (network issues, service crashes) into production-like systems to verify resilience and uncover weaknesses before real outages.

text: What is the difference between black-box and white-box testing?
options: Black-box tests use dark themes while white-box tests use light themes
Black-box testing tests functionality without knowing internal code, while white-box testing examines internal logic and structure
White-box testing only works with TypeScript while black-box works with JavaScript
Black-box testing is automated while white-box testing is always manual
explanation: Black-box testing validates functionality based on inputs and outputs without knowledge of internals, while white-box testing examines internal code paths and logic.

text: What is test flakiness and why is it problematic?
options: Tests that produce inconsistent CSS rendering across browsers
Tests that intermittently pass or fail without code changes, eroding team confidence in the test suite and wasting investigation time
Tests that run too slowly due to flaky network connections
Tests that only work on certain operating systems
explanation: Flaky tests pass and fail non-deterministically due to timing issues, shared state, or external dependencies, undermining confidence in CI results and wasting debugging time.

text: What is load testing and how does it differ from stress testing?
options: Load testing measures page load time while stress testing measures CSS rendering time
Load testing evaluates performance under expected load levels while stress testing pushes beyond normal capacity to find breaking points
Stress testing is a subset of load testing with identical methodology
Load testing only applies to databases while stress testing applies to APIs
explanation: Load testing verifies performance under expected concurrent users and transactions, while stress testing pushes beyond capacity to find failure thresholds and recovery behavior.

text: What is the purpose of test containers?
options: CSS containers used to wrap test result displays
Lightweight, disposable Docker containers that provide real instances of databases and services for integration testing
HTML container elements for embedding test reports
Virtual containers for isolating unit test execution
explanation: Test containers (Testcontainers library) spin up real instances of databases, message brokers, and services in Docker containers for integration tests with realistic dependencies.

text: What is the Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern in testing?
options: A CSS arrangement pattern for test page layouts
A test structure that sets up conditions (Arrange), performs the action (Act), and verifies the outcome (Assert)
An authorization pattern with three levels of access control
A database pattern for arranging, activating, and asserting data
explanation: AAA structures each test into three phases: Arrange (set up test data and conditions), Act (execute the code being tested), and Assert (verify the expected outcome).

text: What is the purpose of Playwright in testing?
options: A tool for writing and managing play scripts in web content
A cross-browser E2E testing framework that supports Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit with auto-waiting and parallel execution
A CSS library for creating theatrical animation effects
A JavaScript library for creating interactive story-based interfaces
explanation: Playwright is a modern E2E testing framework supporting Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit, with features like auto-waiting, network interception, and parallel test execution.

text: What is web deployment?
options: Writing code for a web application
The process of making a web application available and accessible on a server
Designing the visual layout of a website
Testing a website for compatibility issues
explanation: Web deployment is the process of publishing a web application to a server or hosting platform, making it accessible to users over the internet.

text: What is a production environment?
options: The IDE where developers write production code
The live environment where the application is accessible to real end users
A staging area for testing before deployment
The development machine where code is compiled
explanation: The production environment is the live system where the actual application runs and is accessed by real users.

text: What is Docker used for in deployment?
options: A programming language for writing deployment scripts
A platform for packaging applications into portable containers that run consistently across environments
A tool for docking web pages together into a single site
A database system for storing deployment configurations
explanation: Docker packages applications and their dependencies into containers that run consistently across development, staging, and production environments.

text: What is continuous integration (CI)?
options: Integrating CSS files continuously into HTML pages
The practice of frequently merging code changes into a shared repository with automated builds and tests
Continuously updating the server hardware components
The process of integrating new team members into a project
explanation: Continuous integration automatically builds and tests code whenever developers push changes to the shared repository, catching integration issues early.

text: What is a staging environment?
options: A CSS stage for animated transitions between pages
